Records of Searching for Spirits, Volume Sixteen* *Records of Searching for Spirits, Volume Seventeen* *Records of Searching for Spirits, Volume Eighteen* *Records of Searching for Spirits, Volume Nineteen* *Records of Searching for Spirits, Volume Twenty *Preface to Records of Searching for Spirits* By Gan Bao, of Xincang, serving as a Scattered Rider Attendant of the Jin dynasty Although I have investigated ancient records and texts, and gathered forgotten or overlooked material from contemporary sources, such things are not something that can be directly heard or seen by one's own ears and eyes. How, then, could I claim that there are no inaccuracies? Wei Shuo's exile was recorded differently in the *Zuo Zhuan* and the *Gongyang Zhuan*, and Jiang Ziya's service to the Zhou dynasty was recounted in two ways in *Records of the Grand Historian*. Such cases are not uncommon. From this, it is clear that the difficulty of obtaining accurate information has been so since ancient times. Even official documents and their fixed language, based on the official histories and records, can still be subject to such issues; how much more so when one looks back to events a thousand years prior, records of unusual customs, pieces together fragments from incomplete sources, and inquires about past events from the elderly? To ensure that events have no double tracks and that words have no divergent paths, so that they may be considered trustworthy, this has indeed been a flaw in earlier histories. However, the state does not abolish the position of those who record and annotate, and scholars do not cease their studies and readings. Is this not because the losses are minor while the gains are great? If what I have compiled here builds upon earlier records, then it is not my fault. If I were to investigate recent events and there were any inaccuracies, I would willingly share the blame with earlier scholars and Confucian scholars. In my writings, I also aim to demonstrate that the ways of the gods are not false. The various schools of thought and writings are too numerous to fully review, and what can be heard and seen cannot be fully recorded. I have roughly selected what is sufficient to illustrate the principles of the Eight Categories, and to complete my modest account. I hope that future scholars who take an interest will record its essence, find it pleasing to the mind, and view it without criticism.

### The Search for the Supernatural: Volume One Shen Nong struck various plants with a red stick, fully understanding their properties of toxicity, coldness, warmth, and their scents and flavors, and cultivated and spread various grains. Therefore, he was known throughout the world as Shen Nong. Chi Songzi was the rain god during Shen Nong's time. He consumed the Ice Jade Powder, which granted him the ability to enter fire without being burned. He traveled to Mount Kunlun, often entering the stone chamber of the Queen Mother of the West, moving with the wind and rain, up and down. The young daughter of Emperor Yan pursued him, and she too attained immortality, leaving together. By the time of Emperor Ku, he returned as the rain god, wandering among humans. Today's rain gods are descendants of him. Chi Jiangzi Yu was a man from the time of the Yellow Emperor. He did not eat grains but instead consumed the flowers of various herbs. By the time of Emperor Yao, he became a carpenter. He could move with the wind and rain, up and down. He sometimes sold his nets at the city gate, hence he was also called Net Father. Ning Fengzi was also from the time of the Yellow Emperor. He was traditionally known as the chief potter of the Yellow Emperor. A strange man passed by and asked him to manage the fire. He could produce five-colored smoke. After a while, he taught Ning Fengzi, who accumulated fire and burned himself, rising with the smoke. When his ashes were examined, his bones were still visible. People buried him in the north of Ning Mountain. Therefore, he was known as Ning Fengzi. Wei Quan was a father who gathered herbs on Mount Hui. He enjoyed eating pine seeds. His body was covered in hair, seven inches long. His eyes were square-shaped. He could fly and chase horses. He gave pine seeds to Emperor Yao, but Yao did not have time to consume them. The pine seeds were from a simple pine tree. Those who consumed them lived for three hundred years.

Peng Zu was a high-ranking official during the Shang dynasty. His surname was Qian, and his given name was Keng. He was a descendant of the Yellow Emperor, the second son of Lu Zhong. He lived through the Xia dynasty and into the late Shang dynasty, and was said to have lived for 700 years. He often consumed lingzhi mushrooms. In Luyang, there is a temple dedicated to Peng Zu, and it is said that prayers for rain and wind were always answered. There were often two tigers near his shrine, and after visiting the shrine, one could see the tracks of these two tigers. Shou Fu's disciple could control fire. He ate peach blossoms and served as a dragon teacher for King Kongjia. King Kongjia could not maintain his mind and killed him, burying him outside the city. One day, a storm welcomed him. The trees in the mountains caught fire. King Kongjia offered sacrifices and prayed, but he did not return and died. Gao You, from the Western Zhou period, was a person from the Shu region. During the reign of King Cheng of Zhou, he enjoyed carving wooden sheep and selling them. One day, he rode a wooden sheep into the Shu region. The kings, princes, and nobles of Shu followed him to Suishan, a mountain in the southwest, which is very high and has many peaches. Those who followed him did not return, and they all attained immortality. The locals have a saying: "If you get a peach from Suishan, even if you do not become immortal, you can still be proud." Cui Wenzi was from Mount Tai. He studied immortality under Prince Qiao. Prince Qiao transformed into a white rainbow and gave him medicine. Cui Wenzi was surprised and struck the rainbow with a spear, hitting it and causing the medicine to fall. He looked down and saw Prince Qiao's body. He placed it in a room and covered it with a worn basket. Soon, it transformed into a large bird and flew away.

冠先, a man from the state of Song, made his living by fishing. He lived by the banks of the Su River for over a hundred years, catching fish, which he sometimes released, sometimes sold, and sometimes ate himself. He always wore formal attire and enjoyed planting litchi trees, eating their fruits. When King Jing of Song asked him about his methods, he refused to reveal them and was executed. Several decades later, he sat on the city gate of Song, played the qin, and stayed for several days before departing. Every household in Song worshipped him. Qin Gao, a man from the state of Zhao, was skilled in playing the qin. He served as a retainer to King Kang of Song and practiced the techniques of water management and purification. He wandered between Jizhou and Zhuo Commandery for over two hundred years. Later, he entered the Zhuo River, retrieved a dragon's offspring, and made an appointment with his disciples. He said, "Tomorrow, all of you should purify yourselves and wait." They set up a shrine by the water. Indeed, he rode a red carp out of the water and sat in the shrine. There were thousands of people watching. He stayed for a month and then returned to the water. Tao An Gong, a metalworker from Liuan, frequently worked with fire. One day, a burst of fire erupted, producing a purple flame that reached the sky. He prostrated himself in the forge and begged for mercy. Shortly after, a red bird landed on the forge and called out, "Tao Gong! Tao Gong! The forge has become connected to heaven. On the seventh day of the seventh month, we will welcome you with a red dragon." On that day, Tao Gong rode the red dragon and left towards the southeast. Many people from the city came to bid him farewell, all saying their goodbyes.

There was once a person who spent seven years in Mount Jiao. Laojun gave him a wooden drill and instructed him to drill through a stone plate, which was five feet thick. He said, "When this stone is drilled through, you will attain enlightenment." After forty years, the stone was drilled through, and he then obtained the elixir of immortality and the secret formula. Lu Shaoqian, a person from Shanyang, was visited by Emperor Wen of Han, who once disguised himself and carried gold to inquire about his Daoist practices. Shaoqian held a golden staff and an ivory fan as he answered the door. Prince Huai of Anhui, who was fond of Daoist practices, set up a kitchen and staff to welcome guests. On the morning of the first day of the first month, eight old men came to the door to request an audience. The gatekeeper informed the prince, and the prince sent a messenger to reject them, saying, "Our prince desires longevity, but you have no method to halt aging; I dare not inform him." Knowing they would not be seen, the old men transformed into eight young boys, their faces as rosy as peach blossoms. The prince then received them with great ceremony and music, entertaining the eight old men. He took a lute and played it, singing: "Bright and clear, the sky shines over the four seas. Knowing I love the Dao, you come down from above. You will grant me feathers to fly. Rising on blue clouds, treading on Liangfu. Seeing the three luminaries, meeting the Big Dipper. Driving the winds and clouds, commanding the jade maidens." This is what is known today as the Huai'an Ode.

Liu Gen, courtesy name Jun'an, was from Jingzhao Chang'an. During the reign of Emperor Cheng of Han, he entered Mount Song to study the Dao. He met a strange person who taught him secret techniques, and thus became an immortal. He could summon ghosts. The magistrate of Yingchuan, Shi Qi, considered this as witchcraft and sent someone to summon Liu Gen, intending to punish him. When Liu Gen arrived at the magistrate's office, the magistrate said, "You can make people see ghosts; can you make them appear in their true form? If not, you will be punished." Liu Gen replied, "It is very easy." He borrowed the magistrate's brush and inkstone to write a talisman, then tapped the table with it. Soon, he suddenly saw five or six ghosts, who tied two prisoners in front of the magistrate. The magistrate looked closely and recognized them as his parents. They bowed to the ground in front of Liu Gen and said, "Our son has caused trouble; we deserve to die a thousand deaths." Liu Gen scolded the magistrate, "Your descendants cannot honor your ancestors. What crime have you committed against the immortals to cause such trouble for your parents?" The magistrate was filled with grief and fear, wept, and begged for forgiveness. Liu Gen silently vanished, and no one knew where he went. During the reign of Emperor Ming of Han, Wang Qiao, a Shangshu Lang from Hedong, served as the magistrate of Ye. Wang Qiao had supernatural powers and often traveled from the county to the capital every first day of the month. The emperor was curious about his frequent visits and noticed that he arrived without any entourage. He secretly ordered the Grand Historian to observe him. The Grand Historian reported that when Wang Qiao was about to arrive, there were always two cranes flying from the southeast. The emperor then hid and waited, saw the cranes, and set up a net to catch them. However, they only caught a pair of shoes. The emperor ordered the Shangshu to identify the shoes, which turned out to be the shoes given to the Shangshu officials over four years.

Jì Zǐxùn, whose origins are unknown, appeared in the Eastern Han Dynasty and visited Luoyang, where he met with high-ranking officials at several locations. Everywhere he went, people greeted him with a small amount of wine and a bit of dried meat, saying, "We have nothing else to offer, but we hope to convey a small intention." During his visit, hundreds of people gathered to drink and eat with him, and they consumed all day without finishing. After he left, it was said that white clouds rose from dawn until dusk. At one time, an elderly man claimed that when he was a child, he had seen Jì Zǐxùn selling medicine in Kuaiji, and he looked just as he did now. Jì Zǐxùn did not enjoy staying in Luoyang and eventually disappeared. During the Zhengshi period, someone in Dongbachi, east of Chang'an, saw him rubbing a bronze figure with an elderly man. They said to each other, "We just saw it being cast, and it is nearly five hundred years old." The onlookers called out to him, "Master Jì, please stay a little longer." Jì Zǐxùn responded politely, and although he seemed to move slowly, even a galloping horse could not catch up to him. Hán Yīnshēng, a beggar from the banks of the Wei River in Chang'an, was known for his miraculous abilities. He often begged in the market, and although the people there grew tired of him and sprinkled filth on him, he remained clean as if nothing had happened. The local officials learned of this and tried to capture him, binding him with chains and shackles, but he continued to beg in the market. When they tried to kill him, he vanished. The house of the person who had sprinkled the filth on him collapsed, killing several people. A saying circulated in Chang'an: "Give a beggar a cup of fine wine to avoid the guilt of causing a house to collapse."

Ping Changsheng of Gucheng Town, whose origins are unknown, died and was revived multiple times. People of the time did not believe it. Later, a great flood occurred, causing widespread harm, but Ping was always shouting on the Qiemu Mountain, saying, "Ping Changsheng is here." Clouds and rain appeared, and he predicted that the water would stop in five days. When the water stopped, people went up the mountain to worship him, but they only saw Ping wearing a robe, holding a staff, and wearing a leather belt. After several decades, he became a gatekeeper in Huayin. Zuo Ci, whose courtesy name was Yuanfang, was from Lujiang. He had spiritual powers from a young age. Once, while he was at Cao Cao's banquet, Cao smiled and turned to the guests, saying, "Today we have a grand gathering, and most delicacies are prepared. The only thing lacking is the Wusongjiang perch for sashimi." Zuo said, "This is easy to obtain." He then asked for a copper basin to hold water and set up a bamboo pole with bait in the basin. In a short while, he pulled out a perch. Cao clapped his hands in delight, and the guests were all amazed. Cao said, "One fish is not enough for all the guests; two would be better." Zuo then baited the pole again, and in a short while, he pulled out two more, each over three feet long, fresh and appealing. Cao then personally prepared the sashimi, and the guests were served. Cao said, "Now that we have the perch, I regret not having Sichuan ginger." Zuo said, "That can also be obtained." Cao feared that he might buy it nearby, so he said, "I once sent someone to Sichuan to buy silk. You can instruct them to tell my messenger to buy two more bolts." The messenger left and returned shortly with the ginger. He also said, "I saw your messenger at the silk shop, who had already been instructed to buy two more bolts." After more than a year, when Cao's messenger returned, he indeed had two more bolts. When asked about it, the messenger said, "On a certain day last month, I saw someone at the silk shop who gave me your instructions."

After the official went out to the outskirts, hundreds of scholars followed him. Fang then brought a jar of wine and a piece of dried meat, and personally poured the wine for all the officials, who were all drunk and full. The official was surprised and sent someone to find out the reason. He went to the wine shop and found that all the wine and dried meat had disappeared. The official was angry and secretly wanted to kill Fang. Fang was in the official's seat, about to be arrested, but suddenly disappeared into the wall. The official then offered a reward to capture him. Someone saw him in the market, wanting to capture him, but the people in the market all looked like Fang, so no one knew who he was. Later, someone met Fang at the top of Yangcheng Mountain and chased him again. He then entered the sheep flock. The official knew he could not catch him, so he ordered someone to tell him among the sheep: "Cao Cao will not kill you anymore; I just wanted to test your skills. Now that it has been proven, I only want to meet you." Suddenly, an old ram bent its front knees, stood up like a person, and said, "So quickly!" People said, "This is the sheep," and rushed to it. And hundreds of sheep turned into rams, all bending their front knees and standing up like people, saying, "So quickly!" Thus, no one knew what to do. Laozi said, "The great worries I have are because I have a body; if I had no body, what worries would I have?" Those like Laozi can be said to be able to have no body. How far-reaching is this!

Sun Ce desired to cross the river and attack Xu, traveling together with Yu Ji. At that time, there was a severe drought everywhere. Sun Ce urged the generals and soldiers to quickly lead the ships, sometimes personally getting up early to oversee and urge them. He saw that many of the generals and officials were in Yu Ji's company. Sun Ce took this opportunity to become angry and said, "Am I not as capable as Yu Ji? And yet you all rush to follow him first." He then ordered Yu Ji to be brought to him and harshly questioned him, saying, "The sky is dry and it does not rain, making the roads difficult to traverse. I have come out early myself, but you do not share in my worries and sorrows. Instead, you sit calmly in the ship, acting like a ghost or supernatural being, disrupting my ranks. Now I will eliminate you." He had Yu Ji bound and placed on the ground to be exposed to the sun, and told him to pray for rain. If he could move heaven and it rained by noon, he would be pardoned; otherwise, he would be executed. Soon after, clouds rose up, merging together inch by inch. By noon, it began to rain heavily, filling the streams and ravines. The soldiers were delighted, believing that Yu Ji would certainly be pardoned, and they all went to congratulate and comfort him. However, Sun Ce proceeded to kill Yu Ji. The soldiers mourned and cherished his body. That night, a cloud suddenly arose and covered it. The next morning, when they went to look, Yu Ji's body was nowhere to be found. After killing Yu Ji, Sun Ce often sat alone and seemed to see Yu Ji by his side. He deeply disliked this and became somewhat unbalanced. Later, when he was treating his wounds and they were healing, he looked into a mirror and saw Yu Ji in it. He turned around but could not see him. This happened three times. Sun Ce struck the mirror and shouted loudly, causing his wounds to burst open, and he died shortly thereafter. (Yu Ji was a Taoist priest from Langya.)

Jie Yan, of unknown origin, resided in Jian'an Fangshan. He followed his teacher, Baiyang Gong Du, to learn the Dao of Xuan Yi Wuwei. He possessed the ability to transform and become invisible. He often traveled to the Eastern Sea and briefly passed through Moling, where he met the ruler of Wu. The Wu ruler kept Jie Yan and built palaces and temples for him, sending people multiple times a day to inquire about his well-being. Jie Yan sometimes appeared as a child and sometimes as an old man, ate nothing, and did not accept any gifts. The Wu ruler wanted to learn his art, but Jie Yan refused to teach for months because the ruler had many concubines. Angered, the Wu ruler ordered Jie Yan to be bound and had armored soldiers draw their bows to shoot him. The bow was drawn, but the ropes remained tied, and no one knew where Jie Yan had gone. During the Wu period, there was a man named Xu Guang who often performed magic in the market. He once asked someone for a melon, but the owner refused to give it. Xu Guang then asked for a seed, stuck it in the ground, and planted it. Soon the melon grew, spread, bloomed, and bore fruit. He then took it to eat and gave it to the spectators. The seller looked at what he had sold, and it was all gone. Xu Guang's predictions about drought and flood were very accurate. Passing by the gate of General Sun Chen, he lifted his clothes as he hurried, and his feet were wet from stepping in mud. Someone asked why, and he replied, "The stench of blood is unbearable." Sun Chen heard this and killed him. When his head was cut off, there was no blood. When Sun Chen deposed the young emperor and replaced him with Emperor Jing, and was about to pay homage at the tomb, a strong wind overturned his carriage. He saw Xu Guang on a pine tree, clapping and mocking him. Sun Chen asked his attendants, but none saw anything. Soon after, Emperor Jing had Sun Chen executed.

Ge Xuan, living in the residence of his father, Xiao Xian, learned the "Nine Elixirs Scripture" from Zuo Yuanfang. While dining with a guest, they spoke of transformational matters. The guest said, "After the meal, the master could perform a special trick." Ge Xuan replied, "Are you not eager to see something?" Then he spat out the food in his mouth, which turned into hundreds of large bees. All the bees gathered on the guest's body but did not sting him. After a while, Ge Xuan opened his mouth, and the bees flew back in. He chewed and ate them, and they were still rice. He also pointed at frogs and various insects like sparrows, making them dance in rhythm like humans. In winter, he provided fresh melons and dates for the guest; in summer, he brought ice and snow. He also threw dozens of coins into the well. Ge Xuan called out to them with a vessel above the well, and the coins flew out one by one. He set up wine for the guest, and no one passed the cup; it came to the guest on its own, and if not finished, it would not leave. Once, while sitting on a balcony with the ruler of Wu, he saw people performing rain rituals. The emperor said, "The people are longing for rain, can it be obtained?" Ge Xuan replied, "Rain is easy to obtain!" He wrote a talisman and placed it in the shrine, and in an instant, the sky darkened and it rained heavily. The emperor asked, "Is there fish in the water?" Ge Xuan wrote another talisman and threw it into the water, and soon, hundreds of large fish appeared. He had them prepared.

Wu Meng, a man from Puyang, served in Wu state and held the position of magistrate in Xianyang, settling in Fenning due to his service. He was extremely filial. He encountered a sage named Ding Yi, who taught him a divine method; he also obtained secret magical talismans, and his Daoist arts became widely popular. Once, he saw a strong wind and wrote a talisman, which he threw onto the roof, and an azure bird carried it away. The wind immediately stopped. Someone asked him why he did this. He replied, "There is a boat on South Lake that encountered this wind, and a Taoist priest sought help." This was verified to be true. When the magistrate Yu Qing died, Wu Meng said, "His time has not yet come to an end; I shall appeal to heaven for him." He then lay down beside the corpse, and after several days, both he and the magistrate came back to life. Later, when he took his disciples back to Yuzhang, the river was flowing very rapidly, and people could not cross. Wu Meng then used his white feather fan to draw on the water, turning it into a land path, and they walked across it at a leisurely pace. After they had crossed, the water returned to its original state. The onlookers were amazed. Once, while serving as governor of Xunyang, a fierce storm arose at the home of a military officer named Zhou. Wu Meng immediately wrote a talisman and threw it onto the roof, and the wind calmed down shortly thereafter. Yuan Ke, a man from Jiyin, was handsome, and many people in his town wanted to marry him, but he never married. He once cultivated five-colored aromatic herbs for many years and consumed their fruits. Suddenly, a five-colored divine moth appeared on the herbs, and Yuan Ke collected it and placed it on a cloth, which produced silkworms. When it was time for the silkworms to spin cocoons, a divine woman appeared at night to help him raise them, also feeding them with the aromatic herbs. He obtained 120 silkworm cocoons, each as large as a jar, and it took six to seven days to unravel each one completely. After they were all unraveled, the woman and Yuan Ke both ascended to immortality, and their whereabouts are unknown.

In the Han dynasty, there was a man named Dong Yong from the region of Qi. When he was young, he was orphaned and lived with his father in a workshop, working hard in the fields and traveling in a carriage made of deer skin. After his father's death, he had no means to bury him, so he sold himself into slavery to provide for the funeral. His master, recognizing his virtue, gave him a thousand coins and sent him away. After completing his three-year mourning period, Dong Yong wanted to return to his master to fulfill his duties as a slave. On the way, he met a woman who said, "I wish to be your wife." He agreed, and they went together. The master told Dong Yong, "I have given you money." Dong Yong replied, "I have benefited from your kindness, and my father's funeral has been properly arranged. Although I am a lowly person, I am determined to serve diligently and with effort to repay your great virtue." The master asked, "What can a woman do?" Dong Yong said, "She can weave." The master said, "If that is so, just have your wife weave for me a hundred bolts of silk." Dong Yong's wife then wove for the master's household, completing the task in ten days. The woman then went out and said to Dong Yong, "I am the Weaver Girl from heaven. Because of your great filial piety, the Heavenly Emperor has sent me to help you repay your debt." After saying this, she ascended into the sky and disappeared, and no one knew where she went. Initially, the Concubine Gouyi was found guilty and executed by imperial command. After her burial, her body did not decay, and her fragrance could be smelled for more than ten miles. She was buried in Yunling, and the emperor mourned her deeply. Suspecting that she was not an ordinary person, the emperor ordered the tomb to be opened and inspected. The coffin was found to be empty except for a pair of shoes. Later, when Emperor Zhao ascended the throne, he reburied her, and the coffin was again found to be empty, with only a pair of silk shoes remaining.

A woman named Duran Xiang, claiming to be from Nankang, appeared during the spring of the fourth year of Jianye. She frequently visited Zhang Chuan, who was seventeen years old at the time. Zhang saw her carriage outside his door, and a servant delivered a message: "I was born of my mother and have been entrusted to you as a spouse; should you not respect and follow me?" Zhang, whose name had been changed to Shuo, called the woman forward. She appeared to be sixteen or seventeen, and her story seemed distant and ancient. She had two maidservants: the elder was named Xuanzhi, and the younger was named Songzhi. She arrived in a jeweled carriage drawn by a blue ox, with all necessary provisions for food and drink. She recited a poem: "My mother resides on a sacred mountain, occasionally wandering through the clouds and heavens. Many women attend her in their ceremonial attire, never venturing beyond the walls of her palace. The flying wheel brings me here; why should I be ashamed of earthly impurities? Follow me, and we shall share in fortune; beware, for misfortune may follow if you do not." On the day of the eighth month of that year, she returned and recited another poem: "I wander freely between the Milky Way and the heavens, breathing the air of the Nine Peaks. The stream flows without delay, and the weak waters cannot hinder me." She presented three tuberous roots, each as large as an egg, and said, "Eat these, and you will fear neither the waves nor the cold and heat." Shuo ate two of them, but she insisted he finish the last one. She then said, "I was originally destined to be your wife; our feelings are not distant. However, our fates are not yet aligned, so we must part for now. When the great age of the eastern direction in the year of the wood ox arrives, I will return to seek you." When Lan Xiang arrived, Shuo asked about prayers and rituals. She replied, "Eliminating evil can cure diseases, but excessive or improper worship is of no benefit." She used medicine as a means of eliminating evil.

During the JiaPing era, Hui Jibei Commandery's minor official Xian Chao, whose courtesy name was Yiqi, was alone at night when he dreamed of a goddess coming to him. She introduced herself as: "A heavenly jade maiden from Dong Commandery, surnamed Chenggong and given name Zhiqiong, who lost her parents at an early age. The Heavenly Emperor pitied her loneliness and hardship and sent her to marry him." During his dream, Chao was deeply moved by her spirit, admiring her beauty and uniqueness, which were beyond the appearance of an ordinary person. Upon waking, he cherished and longed for her presence, feeling as if she were sometimes there and sometimes not, for several nights. One day, she appeared clearly to visit him, driving a carriage pulled by oxen, accompanied by eight maids, dressed in silk and brocade embroidered clothes. Her appearance and figure were like those of a flying immortal, and she claimed to be seventy years old, yet looked like a girl of fifteen or sixteen. On the carriage, there were five sets of blue and white glassware. They ate strange and exotic foods, delicacies, and sweet wine together.

She said to Chao, "I am a celestial jade maiden, sent down to marry, thus I came to follow you. I do not speak of your virtue. Previously, I felt the movement of fate, and it is fitting that we become husband and wife. I cannot bring you benefit, nor can I cause you harm. However, in terms of travel, you can always drive a light carriage and ride a fat horse; in terms of food, you can always enjoy distant flavors and exotic dishes; in terms of clothing, you can always have silk and fine fabrics without shortage. But as a divine being, I will not bear children for you, nor do I have a jealous nature, and it will not harm your marital duties. Thus, we become husband and wife." She gave him a poem as a gift, the text of which reads: "Drifting and meeting, encountering and wandering, clouds and stones nurture the orchid. One flower does not need moisture, supreme virtue aligns with the time. Gods and immortals are not false, they come to meet you in response to your fate. Accepting me will bring glory to your five clans, rejecting me will bring disaster." This is the general outline of the poem, which is over two hundred words long and cannot be fully recorded. She also annotated the *Yijing* in seven volumes, with hexagrams, images, and *gua* titles.

The classical language is both meaningful and can be used for divination, much like the works of Yang Xiong's *Taixuan* and Xue's *Zhongjing*. Chao was able to understand its meaning and used it for divination, creating a book on marital relations. For seven or eight years, after his parents arranged a marriage for him, they shared meals during the day and slept separately at night, with her coming at night and leaving in the morning, swiftly like flying. Only Chao saw her, while others did not. Even in a dark room, he would hear voices, and he often saw traces but never saw her form. Later, people asked about it out of curiosity, and the secret was revealed. The jade maiden then requested to leave, saying, "I am a divine being. Although I have been with you, I do not wish others to know. But your nature is careless, and now everything is known. I will no longer communicate with you. We have been together for many years, and our bond is not light. If we part now, how could we not be sorrowful? The situation leaves me no choice."

"Each one strive hard!" He summoned a court attendant to pour wine, drank and ate, opened the basket, took out two sets of woven skirts and shirts and gave them to Chao. He also gave him a poem, embraced and bid farewell, with tears and sobs flowing uncontrollably, he solemnly mounted the carriage and departed swiftly and quickly. Chao felt grief and emotion for several days, almost to the point of exhaustion. Five years after leaving, Chao served as a messenger from the prefecture and arrived in Luoyang, reaching Fish Mountain in Jibei, traveling west on the road, looking far away and seeing a horse-drawn carriage at the end of a winding road, it seemed to know Qiong. He hurried to the front, and indeed, it was. Then, he lifted the curtain and met, sorrow and joy intermingled intensely. He controlled the left side and pulled the strap, rode together to Luoyang, then became a household or family, overcoming and restoring old friendship. By the middle of the Tai Kang reign, they were still together. But not daily coming and going, every on the third day of the third month, the fifth day of the fifth month, the seventh day of the seventh month, the ninth day of the ninth month in the morning, and the fifteenth day, they would descend, coming and going for a night and then leave. Zhang Maosan wrote the Ode to the Goddess for this.

**Records of the Supernatural, Volume Two** The Marquis of Shouguang, a man of the Han Dynasty during the reign of Emperor Zhang, possessed the ability to expose and confront all ghosts and spirits, forcing them to bind themselves and reveal their forms. In his village, there was a woman who was afflicted by a spirit. The Marquis exposed the spirit, capturing a large snake several zhang in length, which died outside the door, and the woman then recovered. There was also a large tree, which had a spirit. Anyone who stopped under it would die, and birds flying over it would also fall. The Marquis exposed the tree spirit, causing the tree to wither and fall in midsummer. A large snake, seven or eight zhang long, hung dead in the tree. Emperor Zhang of Han heard about these events and summoned the Marquis to inquire. The Marquis replied, "Yes, such things have occurred." The emperor said, "There have been strange occurrences in the palace, especially after midnight, when several people dressed in red, with loose hair, and carrying torches, would appear. Can you expose them?" The Marquis replied, "These are minor spirits, easy to dispel." The emperor pretended to send three people to test the Marquis. The Marquis then devised a method, and the three people fell to the ground immediately, losing consciousness. The emperor was surprised and said, "They are not spirits, I was testing you." He then had them released. Some say that during the reign of Emperor Wu of Han, there were often strange occurrences in the palace, with several people dressed in red, with loose hair, and carrying candles, walking around. The emperor said to Liu Ping, "Can you get rid of them?" Liu Ping replied, "Yes." He then threw an azure talisman at them, and several ghosts fell to the ground. The emperor was surprised and said, "I was testing you." They were released and revived.

Fan Ying lived in seclusion on Mount Hu. Once, a strong wind suddenly arose from the southwest. Fan Ying told his disciples, "There is a great fire in Chengdu." He then held water in his mouth and spat it out. He ordered them to calculate the time, and later, someone came from Shu who reported, "On that day, there was a huge fire. A cloud rose from the east, and shortly after, it rained heavily, extinguishing the fire." In Fujian, there was a woman named Xu Deng who transformed into a man. She met Dongyang Zhao Bing, who was also skilled in magic. During a time of war and chaos, they met by a stream and each boasted about their abilities. Xu Deng first stopped the stream from flowing, and Zhao Bing then made the willows sprout. The two looked at each other and laughed. Xu Deng was older, and Zhao Bing regarded him as a teacher. After Xu Deng's death, Zhao Bing traveled east to Chang'an. The common people did not know about this, and Zhao Bing entered a thatched house, sitting on a tripod to cook. The owner was surprised and amazed, but Zhao Bing smiled and did not respond. The house was not damaged. Once, Zhao Bing approached the water to ask for a crossing, but the boatman refused. Zhao Bing then spread a canopy, sat inside, emitted a long sigh to call the wind, and crossed the turbulent stream. The common people respected and admired him, and those who followed him felt like they were returning home. The magistrate of Chang'an disliked that he misled the people and arrested and killed him. The people built a shrine in Yongkang in his memory, and even now, mosquitoes cannot enter.

Xu Deng and Zhao Bing esteemed simplicity and frugality. They offered the gods water from the east-flowing river and prepared a type of dried meat by peeling the bark off mulberry trees. Chen Jie visited various deities, and the God of the Eastern Sea gave him a blue robe made of woven material. Bian Hong from Xuanzhou served as an official in Guangyang. After his mother's death, he returned home. Han You went to visit him, but it was already night. Upon exiting, he told his followers, "Quickly prepare yourselves, I will leave tonight." The followers said, "It is already night, and we have to travel through grasslands over dozens of miles. Why are you in such a hurry to leave?" Han You replied, "Blood covers the ground here. How can I stay?" Despite their efforts to persuade him, he would not stay. That night, Bian Hong suddenly went mad, strangled his two sons, and killed his wife. He also killed two of his father's maids, all of whom were wounded. He then fled away. After several days, he was found in the forest in front of his house, having already hanged himself. Ju Daolong was skilled in magical arts. He once said, "A man from the East Sea named Huang Gong was skilled in magic, controlling snakes and taming tigers. He always carried a red gold knife. When he grew old, he drank excessively. At the end of the Qin dynasty, a white tiger appeared in the East Sea. The emperor sent Huang Gong with the red gold knife to subdue it. His magic did not work, and he was killed by the tiger." Xie Jiu once had guests. He threw a red ink talisman into a well, and two carp leaped out. He immediately ordered them to be prepared as sashimi. Everyone at the gathering got a taste.

During the Yongjia period of the Jin Dynasty, a person from Tianzhu (ancient India) arrived in the south of the Yangtze River. This person possessed various esoteric skills: he could cut off his tongue and reattach it, and he could breathe fire. People gathered to watch wherever he went. When he was about to cut off his tongue, he first showed his tongue to the audience, then cut it with a knife, causing blood to flow onto the ground. He then took the severed tongue, placed it in a container, and passed it around for the audience to see. The audience saw that half of the tongue was still intact, and then he reattached it by holding it in his mouth. After a short while, the audience saw that his tongue appeared whole again, and they could not tell if it had actually been cut. For the silk trick, he took a piece of silk and asked someone to hold one end while he held the other. He then cut the silk in half, and when he brought the two halves together, the silk was seamlessly reconnected, just as it had been before. Many people doubted that this was an illusion, so they secretly tested it and found that the silk was indeed cut. For the fire-breathing trick, he first placed some medicine in a container, took a piece of fire, mixed it with rice, and blew on it several times. He then opened his mouth, and fire filled his mouth. He used this fire to cook, and it was indeed fire. He also took paper and threads and threw them into the fire. The audience watched as they burned completely, but when he picked them up from the ashes, they were the same as before. The king of Funan, Fan Xin, kept tigers in the mountains. If someone committed a crime, they were thrown to the tiger. If the tiger did not attack, the person was pardoned. Therefore, the mountain was called "Dachong" (meaning "tiger") and also "Daling" (meaning "great spirit"). He also kept ten crocodiles. If someone committed a crime, they were thrown to the crocodiles. If the crocodile did not attack, the person was pardoned. If the person was innocent, the crocodile would not attack either. Therefore, there was a crocodile pool. He also once boiled water and placed a golden ring in the boiling water, then dipped his hand into the water. If the person was innocent, his hand would not be burned, but if the person was guilty, his hand would burn immediately.

Jia Peilan, the servant girl of Empress Qi, later married Duan Ru of Fufeng and recounted: "When I was in the palace, we often enjoyed music and dance together, competing in wearing exotic costumes to celebrate good times. On the 15th day of the 10th month, we would enter the Temple of the Divine Lady, offering pork and rice to the gods, playing the flute, striking the zither, and singing the songs of the Divine Lady. Then, we would link arms and step on the ground in rhythm, singing the song of the Crimson Phoenix coming, which is a witch's custom. By the 7th day of the 7th month, we would approach the Hundred Sons Pool, performing the music of Yutian. After the music ended, we would tie together with colored threads, calling it 'connected ribbons.' On the 4th day of the 8th month, we would go out through the northern door of the carved house and play Go under the bamboo. The winner would have good fortune all year, while the loser would suffer illness all year. We would take threads and pray to the North Star for a long life, thus avoiding misfortune. In September, we would wear wormwood, eat qiong cakes, and drink chrysanthemum wine, which is said to grant longevity. When chrysanthemums bloom, we would collect their stems and leaves, mixing them with millet and rice. By the next year's 9th day of the 9th month, it would be ready to drink, hence it is called 'chrysanthemum wine.' On the first day of the first month, we would wash at the edge of the pond, eat qiong cakes, and purify away evil spirits. On the 3rd day of the 3rd month, we would set up music by the flowing water. Thus, throughout the year, we engaged in these customs."

During the reign of Emperor Wu of Han, he favored Lady Li, and after her death, he continued to miss her deeply. A Daoist from the state of Qi named Li Shaoweng claimed he could summon her spirit. At night, he set up curtains and lit candles, and had the emperor observe from a distance. The emperor saw a beautiful woman in the tent, resembling Lady Li, who moved around but could not be fully seen. The emperor became even more sorrowful and wrote a poem: "Is it you? Is it not you? Standing and looking at her, graceful and beautiful, why so slowly does she come?" He ordered the Music Bureau to have musicians sing and play it. In the city of Yingling in Beihai, there was a Daoist who could make living people see those who had died. A man from the same region whose wife had died several years ago heard about the Daoist and went to see him. He said, "I wish to see my deceased wife once more, so that I may die without regret." The Daoist replied, "You may go and see her. If you hear the sound of drums, leave immediately, do not linger." He then taught the man how to meet his wife. Soon after, the man saw her; they spoke, and their emotions were as strong as if she were alive. After a long time, he heard the sound of drums, regretfully unable to stay. As he was about to leave, she suddenly caught the hem of her clothes in the door, pulling them apart and departing. A year later, the man died. His family buried him, and when they opened his tomb, they found the hem of his wife's clothes under the coffin lid.

During the reign of Wu's Sun Shao, he fell ill and sought a shaman to examine him. He found one and decided to test his abilities. The emperor then had a goose killed and buried in the garden, built a small house, and placed a bed and table inside, dressing a woman's shoes, clothes, and accessories on it. He instructed the shaman to examine it and said, "If you can describe the appearance of the ghostly woman in the tomb, you will receive a generous reward, and I will believe you." The shaman remained silent all day. The emperor pressed him urgently, and he finally said, "I did not see any ghost, but I saw only an old white goose standing on the tomb. That's why I didn't immediately report it. I suspected that it was a transformation of a ghost or deity and waited to see its true form before making a decision. It did not change, and I don't know why, but I dare to report the truth." Wu's Sun Jun killed Zhu Zhu and buried her at Shi Zi Gang. After surrendering and ascending the throne, Sun Hao intended to reinter her. The tombs were so close together that they were indistinguishable. But the palace women recognized the clothes Zhu Zhu wore when she died. So he sent two shamans to different places to wait for her spirit, instructing them to observe and discern her spirit without getting too close. After a while, both shamans reported seeing a woman. She was about thirty years old, wearing a green silk cap, purple and white striped skirt, and red silk shoes. She walked halfway up Shi Zi Gang, leaning on her knees and sighing deeply. She paused briefly, then walked to another tomb and stopped. She lingered for a long time, then suddenly disappeared. The shamans' reports matched perfectly. They opened the tomb, and the clothes were as described.

Xiahou Hong claimed that he saw ghosts and spoke with them. General Xie Shang of the Western Region's horse suddenly died, causing him great distress. Xie said, "If you can bring this horse back to life, then you truly are able to see ghosts." Xiahou Hong left and returned after a long while, saying, "The temple god took pleasure in your horse, so he took it. It will be revived now." Xie sat opposite the dead horse, and soon, the horse suddenly ran back from the door, passed by the horse's corpse, and disappeared. Immediately, it began to move and stood up, walking. Xie said, "I have no heir, which is a punishment for me alone." Xiahou Hong remained silent for a time. He said, "What I have seen recently are just small ghosts; they certainly cannot explain the source of this." Later, he unexpectedly encountered a ghost riding a new carriage, accompanied by about ten people, wearing a blue silk cloth robe. Xiahou Hong held the cow's nose in front, and the person in the carriage asked him, "Why are you blocking me?" Xiahou Hong said, "I want to ask something. General Xie Shang of the Western Region has no son. This gentleman is a man of elegance and prestige, and it would be unfortunate for his lineage to end."

The soldiers were moved and said, "What you say is exactly my son. When he was young, he made a pact with a maid in the household not to remarry, but broke it; now that this maid has died, she is accusing him in heaven, which is why he has no children." Huang reported all this. The person apologized and said, "I did indeed have this matter when I was young." Huang saw a big ghost holding a spear and halberd, with several small ghosts following him, in Jiangling. Huang was afraid and stepped off the road to avoid him. After the big ghost passed, Huang caught a small ghost and asked, "What is this thing?" The ghost replied, "This spear and halberd are used to kill people. If they hit the internal organs, no one survives." Huang asked, "Is there a cure for this disease?" The ghost said, "Apply a black chicken to it, and it will heal." Huang asked, "Where are you going now?" The ghost said, "He is going to Jingzhou and Yangzhou." At that time, people were dying from internal organ diseases, and no one survived. Huang then taught people to kill black chickens and use them to treat the disease, and out of ten, nine or eight were cured. Now, whenever someone is treated for internal organ diseases using black chickens, it is because of Huang's method.

**Volume Three of *Search for the Supernatural*** During the Yongping era of the Han dynasty, Zhongli Yi of Huiji, whose style name was Zi A, served as the magistrate of Lu. Upon assuming office, he spent his own thirteen thousand coins, giving them to Kong Su, the head of the household affairs department, to repair the carriage of the Master [Confucius]. He personally entered the temple and wiped the table, mat, sword, and shoes. A man named Zhang Bo was weeding the courtyard when he found seven jade discs buried in the soil. Zhang Bo concealed one of them and presented six to Yi. Yi ordered the clerk to place them before the table. In the hall where Confucius once taught, at the head of the bed, there was a suspended jar. Yi summoned Kong Su and asked, “What is this jar?” Kong Su replied, “It is the jar of the Master. There is a red-inscribed text on the back, and no one dares to open it.” Yi said, “The Master was a sage. He left this jar to hang as a sign for future scholars.” He then opened it and found within a plain manuscript, which read: “In later generations, my writings will be studied by Dong Zhongshu. My carriage will be protected, my shoes wiped, and my chest opened by Huiji Zhongli Yi. There were seven jade discs; Zhang Bo concealed one.” Yi immediately summoned Zhang Bo and asked, “There were seven jade discs—why did you conceal one?” Zhang Bo bowed and produced it.

Duan Yi, courtesy name Yuanzhang, was from Guanghan Xindu. He studied the *Yijing* (Book of Changes) and mastered divination. One day, a student came to learn from him. After many years, the student believed he had roughly mastered the essential techniques and decided to return to his hometown. Duan Yi prepared a medicinal ointment and sealed a note in a tube, telling the student, "In case of an emergency, open it." When the student arrived at Jiamei, he got into a dispute with an official over crossing the ferry. The official struck and broke the head of one of the student's followers. The student opened the tube and found a message that said, "When you reach Jiamei, if you fight with an official and someone's head is broken, use this ointment to wrap the wound." The student followed the instructions, and the injured person was healed immediately. Right Fu Feng's Zang Zhongying served as a Supervisory Censor. One day, while his family was preparing food and setting up tables, some unclear dust fell and contaminated the food. As the cooking neared completion, they couldn't find the pot. Suddenly, the weapons and crossbows moved on their own. A fire started from the chests and boxes, burning all the clothes and belongings, yet the chests and boxes remained intact. One day, all the women and maidservants suddenly lost their mirrors; several days later, the mirrors were thrown from the hall to the courtyard, accompanied by a voice saying, "Return your mirrors." A granddaughter, about three or four years old, went missing, and despite searching, no trace was found. After two or three days, she was found crying near the latrine under the excrement. Such strange occurrences were not isolated. Xu Jishan from Runan, who was skilled in divination, was consulted, and he said, "There must be an old green dog spirit and an inner servant named Yi Xi working together to cause these events. If you truly want to stop them, kill the dog and send Yi Xi back to his hometown." Zang Zhongying followed his advice, and the strange occurrences ceased. Later, he was transferred to the position of Chief Clerk of the Grand Commandant and promoted to the governor of the state of Lu.

太尉乔玄，字公祖，梁国人也。初为司徒长史，五月末，于中门卧，夜半后，见东壁正白，如开门明。呼问左右。左右莫见。因起自往手扪摸之，壁自如故。还床，复见。心大怖恐。其友应劭，适往候之，语次相告。劭曰：“乡人有董彦兴者，即许季山外孙也。其探赜索隐，穷神知化，虽眭孟，京房，无以过也。然天性褊狭，羞于卜，筮者间来候师。”王叔茂谓往迎之。须臾，便与俱来。公祖虚礼盛馔，下席行觞。彦兴自陈：“下土诸生，无他异分。币重言甘，诚有踧踖。颇能别者，愿得从事。”公祖辞让再三，尔乃听之，曰：“府君当有怪，白光如门明者。然不为害也。六月上旬，鸡明时，闻南家哭，即吉。到秋节，迁北行，郡以金为名。位至将军三公。”公祖曰：“怪异如此，救族不暇，何能致望于所不图？此相饶耳。”至六月九日，未明。太尉杨秉暴薨。七月七日，拜钜鹿太守。“钜”边有金。后为“度辽将军，”历登三事。

Guan Lu, courtesy name Gongming, was a man from the region of Pingyuan. He was skilled in divination using the Yi Jing. Wang Ji, courtesy name Boyu, was the governor of Anping and from Donglai. His household frequently experienced strange occurrences, so he sent Guan Lu to cast the Yi Jing for him. After the hexagram was cast, Guan Lu said, "Your hexagram indicates that there will be a lowly woman who gives birth to a boy. The child will fall to the ground and immediately walk into the stove and die. Additionally, there will be a large snake on the bed, holding a pen, and everyone will watch, and it will leave shortly. Also, a crow will enter the room and fight with a swallow, the swallow will die, and the crow will leave. These are the three signs." Wang Ji was greatly surprised and said, "The profound meaning of your divination reaches such a level. Please interpret whether these signs are auspicious or ominous." Guan Lu replied, "There is no other misfortune. It is only because the guesthouse has been long unused, and the spirits of chimui and wangling have caused these strange occurrences. The child walking is not due to its own ability, but it is the demon of Song Wuji leading it into the stove. The snake holding the pen is actually the ghost of an old clerk. The crow fighting with the swallow is the ghost of an old bell-ringer. The true nature of the spirits cannot be harmed by demons. The changes in all things are not stopped by the way. The floating spirits of long ago will inevitably follow a certain fate. In your hexagram, the signs are visible, but there is no indication of misfortune. Therefore, I know these are false signs and not omens of disaster. There is nothing to worry about."

In the past, the cauldron of King Gaozong was not a place for a wild chicken to crow; the steps of King Taiwu were not a place for a mulberry tree to grow. However, when a wild bird crowed once, Wu Ding became a sage king; when mulberry and grain grew temporarily, King Taiwu revived. Knowing that these three events were not auspicious, I wish the governor to maintain his integrity and moral character, act with dignity and grandeur, and not be tainted by supernatural deceptions. Later, nothing else happened. After the governor was relocated to Annan, Lu's hometown was in Taiyuan. Lu asked, "You previously discussed strange occurrences with the governor, saying, 'An old clerk turned into a snake, and an old bell-ringer turned into a crow.' These were originally human beings. Why did they transform into such lowly forms? Were these transformations predicted by the hexagrams or was it your own interpretation?" Lu replied, "Unless one understands the nature and the way of heaven, how can one go against the hexagrams and rely on personal judgment? The transformation of all things does not have a fixed form; human transformations do not have a definite form. Sometimes large things become small, and small things become large; there is no inherent superiority or inferiority. The transformation of all things is a universal principle. Therefore, the father of the Shang Dynasty founder, Gun, the son of the Han Dynasty founder, Liu Ruyi, and the founder of the Han Dynasty, Liu Bang, turned into a yellow bear and a gray dog, respectively. These were once the highest positions but now they are among the lowly. Moreover, snakes are associated with the positions of the earth and fire, and crows are associated with the sun. These are clear symbols of darkness and the flow of the sun. For a clerk and a bell-ringer to transform into a snake and a crow, respectively, is excessive."

Guan Lu arrived at Pian and saw Yan Chao, whose appearance indicated he would die young. Yan's father then asked Guan Lu to extend his life. Guan Lu said, "Go back and get a pound of clear wine and deer meat. On the day of the rabbit, go to the south side of the wheat field under a large mulberry tree, where two people are playing chess. Then just pour the wine and place the meat in front of them, drink it all and refill, stopping when it is finished. If they ask you, just bow to them and do not speak. There will certainly be someone to save you." Yan followed his instructions and went there, and indeed he saw two people playing chess, frequently placing meat and pouring wine in front of them. The person playing chess was greedy and only drank and ate the meat. After several rounds, the person sitting on the north side suddenly saw Yan there and shouted, "Why are you here?" Yan only bowed to him. The person sitting on the south side said, "Just now, we drank his wine and ate his meat; how could we be无情 (without feeling)?" The person sitting on the north side said, "The documents are already determined." The person sitting on the south side said, "Let me see the documents." They saw that Yan Chao's lifespan was only nineteen years, so they took a pen and wrote, "Save him to live until ninety years old." Yan bowed and returned. Guan Lu told Yan, "Great help, now you will gain additional years of life. The person sitting on the north side is the North Star (Big Dipper), and the person sitting on the south side is the South Star (Southern Dipper). The South Star records births, and the North Star governs deaths. All people are conceived and pass through the South Star and then the North Star; all requests are directed to the North Star."

The women of the magistrate of Xindu were terrified, suffering alternately from illness. They asked Zuo to divinate the cause. Zuo said, "There are two dead men in the northwest corner of your hall: one man holds a spear, and the other holds a bow and arrow. Their heads are inside the wall, and their feet are outside. The man with the spear is responsible for stabbing the head, hence the severe headache; the man with the bow and arrow is responsible for shooting the chest and abdomen, hence the pain in the heart and inability to eat. They wander during the day and come to haunt the sick at night, causing fear." They then dug into the room, going down eight feet, and indeed found two coffins: one coffin contained a spear; the other contained a horn bow and arrows, which had been there for a long time and were mostly decayed, but the iron and horn were still intact. They moved the bones twenty miles away from the city and buried them, and there was no more illness. Guo En, a resident of Licao, whose courtesy name was Yibo, and his three brothers all suffered from a leg disease. They asked Zuo to divinate the cause of the illness. Zuo said, "There is your ancestral tomb in the hexagram, and there is a female ghost in the tomb. It is not your paternal grandmother but your maternal aunt. In times of famine, someone must have taken several handfuls of rice, pushed it into the well, making a sound, and then pushed a large stone down, breaking her head. The lonely spirit is in pain and has appealed to heaven."

Chunyu Zhi, courtesy name Shuping, was from Jibei Lu. He was profound and reserved in character, thoughtful and righteous. When young, he was a scholar who could perform divination using the *I Ching* and was skilled in counter-charms. A man from Gaoqing named Liu Rou, while sleeping at night, was bitten by a mouse on his left middle finger, which he found very unpleasant. He consulted Chunyu Zhi about it. Chunyu Zhi divined and said, "The mouse originally intended to kill you but could not, and will make it die in return." He then instructed Liu to write the character '田' (field) with red ink on his wrist, three inches behind the wrist crease, about one inch and two parts square, and to expose his hand at night while sleeping. A large mouse died at his feet. In Shangdang, there was a man named Bao Yuan whose family suffered many deaths, illnesses, and poverty. Chunyu Zhi divined for him and said, "Your dwelling is inauspicious, which is why you are in distress. Your house has a large mulberry tree to the northeast. Go directly to the market, enter and walk dozens of steps, and there will be someone selling new whips. Buy them and return, hanging them on this tree. In three years, you will suddenly gain wealth." Bao Yuan followed the advice and went to the market, indeed finding new whips and hanging them. In three years, when he dug a well, he found tens of thousands of coins and over twenty thousand copper and iron items. His businesses were expanded, and the sick recovered. A man from Qiao named Xiahou Zao, whose mother was ill and weak, intended to consult with Chunyu Zhi. Suddenly, a fox appeared at the door, facing him and howling. Xiahou Zao was greatly startled and fearful. He then rushed to consult with Chunyu Zhi. Chunyu Zhi said, "The disaster is very urgent. You must return quickly, where the fox howled. Clutch your chest and cry, making your family surprised and alarmed. Everyone must come out; if one person does not come out, do not stop crying. But the disaster can be avoided." Xiahou Zao returned as instructed, and his mother also came out despite being ill. When the family gathered, the five rooms of the main hall collapsed suddenly.

The Commandant Zhang Shao's mother was gravely ill. With wisdom, he divined the situation and instructed someone to go west to the market to buy a monkey and tie it to his mother's arm. He ordered others to beat and make noise, always making it move, and to release it after three days. Zhang Shao followed his instructions. When the monkey went out, it was immediately killed by a dog, and his mother's illness then improved. Guo Pu, courtesy name Jingchun, traveled to Lujiang and advised the governor Hu Mengkang to urgently retreat south. Hu did not listen, so Guo prepared to leave quickly. He loved one of the governor's maids but could not obtain her. Therefore, he took three dou of small beans and spread them around the governor's house. The next morning, the governor saw thousands of red-clad figures surrounding his home. Upon looking at them, they disappeared. He greatly disliked this and asked Guo to cast a divination. Guo said, "Your household should not keep this maid. Sell her twenty li to the southeast, and be careful not to haggle over the price. Then this demon can be dispelled." Guo secretly had someone buy the maid at a low price and threw a talisman into the well. Thousands of red-clad figures one by one jumped into the well. The governor was very pleased. Guo took the maid away, and after several weeks, Lujiang fell. Zhao Gu's horse suddenly died, and he was very sad and regretful. He asked Guo Pu about it. Guo said, "Send dozens of people with bamboo poles to travel east for thirty li. There will be mountains, forests, and trees. Stir and beat them. An object will appear, and you should quickly bring it back." Following his advice, they indeed found an object that resembled a monkey. They brought it back, entered the house, and saw the dead horse. The monkey jumped and ran to the dead horse's head, blowing and sucking its nose. Soon, the horse could stand up. It struggled and neighed, eating and drinking as usual. The previous object was no longer seen. Zhao Gu found it strange and provided generous support.

Yangzhou Deputy Magistrate Guo Qiu's elder sister fell ill shortly after her tenth birthday, and she remained sick for over fifty years. When she reached that age, she had Guo Pu perform a divination, which yielded the hexagram "Great Exceeding" with a changing line to "Ascending." The divination text read: "The Great Exceeding hexagram signifies an unfavorable meaning. The graves and tombstones are like withered poplars without blossoms. The wandering spirits are agitated, and a dragon carriage is seen. The body is burdened with heavy misfortune and entangled with evil spirits. The law is due to the cutting of sacrifices and the killing of a sacred snake. It is not one's own fault but a blemish of the ancestors. According to the hexagram, it can be interpreted as such." Guo Qiu then investigated the family's history and discovered that their ancestors had once cut down a large tree and killed a sacred snake, which led to his sister's illness. After her illness began, thousands of birds would circle her house, and people found this strange. One day, a county farmer passing by saw a dragon pulling a carriage, its colors dazzling and its size extraordinary, but it soon disappeared. In another instance, Fang Shubao from Yixing contracted a severe fever and was on the verge of death. He asked Guo Pu to divine his fate, and the result was unfavorable. Fang was advised to seek a white ox to ward off evil. However, he could not find one, but only Yang Zixuan had a white ox, who refused to lend it. Guo Pu arranged for the white ox to be brought, and on that very day, a large white ox came from the west and directly came to visit Fang. Fang was startled, and his illness immediately subsided.

In the Western Region of Sichuan, there was a man named Fei Xiaoxian who was renowned for his skills in divination and fortune-telling. A wealthy merchant named Wang Min, who had traveled to Chengdu for trade, sought a reading from Fei. Fei said, "Do not stay where you are told to stay, and do not wash where you are told not to wash. A stone of grain can be ground into three dou of rice. It will thrive in light and perish in darkness." He warned Wang repeatedly, instructing him to memorize these words. Wang took note of them. As he was about to leave, it began to rain heavily, and he took shelter under a roof. The path was crowded with travelers. Reflecting, Wang thought, "Do not stay where you are told to stay, is this not what he meant?" He decided to brave the rain and continue his journey. Soon after, the roof collapsed, but he alone escaped unharmed. Wang's wife had already conspired with a neighbor to forge a lifelong alliance, and upon his return, she planned to poison him. When Wang arrived, his wife told her accomplice, "Tonight, the one who has just bathed is my husband." As they prepared to eat, she called Wang to bathe and change his headwear. Wang realized, "Do not wash where you are told not to wash, is this not what he meant?" He firmly refused. His wife became angry and, ignoring his refusal, bathed herself. That night, she was killed. When Wang awoke, he called out to his neighbors, who gathered to see what had happened. None of them could understand why she had been killed. Wang was then arrested and subjected to interrogation. The case was concluded, but he could not defend himself. The local magistrate recorded the case, and Wang wept, saying, "If I am to die, I will die, but Fei's words have ultimately proven to be false." The officials reported this to a higher authority. The magistrate had not yet decided on a punishment for Wang. He asked, "Who is your neighbor?" Wang replied, "Kang Qi." The magistrate then sent someone to arrest Kang Qi. "The killer of your wife must be this person." And indeed, it was. The magistrate then told his subordinates, "A stone of grain can be ground into three dou of rice, is this not Kang Qi?" Thus, the case was clarified, and the truth of the earlier warning about light and darkness was revealed.

Wei Zhao, a resident of Hongshou Pavilion in Ruyin, was skilled in the *Yi Jing* (Book of Changes). On his deathbed, he wrote on a wooden tablet and gave it to his wife, saying: "After my death, there will be a great famine. Even then, do not sell the house. In the spring of the fifth year after my death, there will be an imperial envoy who will stay at this pavilion. His surname is Gong, and he owes me money. Use this tablet to demand repayment from him. Do not fail in your promise." After his death, she indeed became very poor and wanted to sell the house many times, but remembering her husband's words, she would stop. On the appointed day, there was indeed an envoy surnamed Gong who stayed at the pavilion. The wife then took the tablet to demand repayment. The envoy held the tablet, not knowing what to say, and said, "I have never owed anyone money. Why would this be so?" The wife replied, "My husband, on his deathbed, wrote these instructions for me by hand and told me not to lie. I dare not deceive you." The envoy pondered for a long time and then realized, ordering to take out the yarrow stalks for divination. The divination was complete, and he clapped his hands and sighed, saying, "How wise was Master Wei! He hid his brilliance and his presence, and no one knew about it. He was like a mirror reflecting both prosperity and adversity, and one who could foresee good and bad omens." He then told his wife, "I do not owe any money. My virtuous husband had hidden gold. It is known that after my death, there will be a temporary period of poverty, so he hid the gold to wait for better times. The reason I did not tell my children and grandchildren was because I feared that if the gold ran out, the poverty would continue indefinitely. Knowing that I was skilled in the *Yi Jing*, he used this tablet to convey his intentions. There are five hundred pounds of gold, stored in a blue jar, covered with a copper plate, buried in the eastern part of the main hall, one zhang below the ground, and nine chi into the ground." The wife returned and dug it up, and indeed found the gold, just as predicted.

Han You, courtesy name Jingxian, was from Shu in Lujiang. He was skilled in divination and also practiced the method of suppressing evil spirits as taught by Jing Fang. Liu Shize's daughter was afflicted by a spirit, and for many years, witches performed rituals to exorcise the spirit, searching in the vicinity of abandoned tombs and old cities, and found dozens of weasels and alligators, but her illness still did not improve. Han You consulted the oracle, ordered a cloth bag to be made, and instructed to place it between the window and door when the girl's illness flared up. Han You closed the door and made a sound, as if driving away something. After a short while, he saw the bag swell up like it had been inflated. He then tore it open. The girl's illness flared up again. Han You then made two more leather bags and placed them one on top of the other, arranged as before. The bag swelled up again, so he quickly tied the mouth of the bag and hung it on a tree. After about twenty days, it gradually diminished. When he opened it to see, there were two pounds of fox fur. The girl's illness was then cured. Yan Qing from Kuaiji was skilled in divination. A villager named Wei Xu wanted to travel east. It was a year of famine, and there were many bandits. He asked Yan Qing to consult the oracle. Yan Qing said, "You should be very careful not to travel east. You will surely encounter sudden harm, but it will not be a robbery." Wei Xu did not believe him. Yan Qing said, "If you must go, you should have a way to ward off the danger. You can look for a white male dog from the home of a single mother outside the western gate of the city and tie it to the front of your boat." Wei Xu searched but only found a mottled dog, not a white one. Yan Qing said, "Even a mottled one is sufficient. However, I regret that the color is not pure. It will only cause minor harm, affecting only domestic animals. You no longer need to worry." Wei Xu traveled halfway when the dog suddenly made a loud noise, as if it had been struck by someone. By the time he looked, the dog was already dead, having vomited out over a dou of black blood. That night, several white geese on Wei Xu's estate died without reason. Wei Xu's family was safe.

Hua Tuo, a renowned physician from Pei, had the courtesy name Yuanhua and was also known as Fu. Liu Xun from Langya served as the governor of Hedong and had a daughter who was nearly twenty years old. She suffered from an itchy but painless ulcer on her left knee, which would heal after several days but recur after about ten days, repeating this cycle for seven or eight years. They invited Hua Tuo to examine her. Hua Tuo said, "This is easily treatable." He required rice husks, a yellow dog, and two good horses. He tied a rope around the dog's neck and had the horses pull the dog, changing the horses when they became exhausted. After the horses had traveled over thirty miles, the dog could no longer walk, so they had people drag it by hand, covering a distance of nearly fifty miles. Then, Hua Tuo gave the woman medicine to drink. She immediately fell into a deep sleep, unaware of her surroundings. Hua Tuo then took a large knife and cut open the dog's belly near the front of its hind legs, positioning the cut over the woman's wound, leaving a gap of two or three inches. After a short while, a snake-like creature emerged from the wound. Hua Tuo struck the snake's head with an iron mallet, causing it to move violently within the skin for a long time. After a moment, it stopped moving, and he pulled it out. The snake was about three feet long, entirely a snake, but it had eyes without pupils and backward-pointing scales. Hua Tuo applied ointment to the wound, and it healed in seven days. Once, while traveling, Hua Tuo encountered a man suffering from a throat condition, unable to swallow food. His family carried him in a cart to seek medical help. Hua Tuo heard his groans and stopped to examine him. He told the man, "There is a vendor selling garlic paste and vinegar by the roadside. Take three liters and drink it, and your illness will go away." The man followed Hua Tuo's advice and immediately vomited up a snake.

The Search for the Supernatural, Volume Four** The Wind Lord and the Rain Lord are stars. The Wind Lord is the star Jī (箕), and the Rain Lord is the star Bì (畢). Zheng Xuan said that the Lord of the Center and the Lord of Fate are the fourth and fifth stars of the constellation Wén (文). The Rain Lord is also known as Píngyì (屏翳), Hào Píng (號屏), and Xuánmíng (玄冥). Zhang Kuān of Shu Commandery, courtesy name Shūwén, served as a Grand Attendant during the reign of Emperor Wu of Han. On a visit to the Temple of Heaven at Gānquán, they reached the Wei Bridge, where a woman was bathing in the Wei River. Her breasts were seven chi long. The emperor found this unusual and sent someone to inquire about her. She said, "The person in the seventh carriage behind the emperor knows me." At that time, Zhang Kuān was in the seventh carriage. He replied, "She is a celestial star. If the person in charge of the sacrifice does not maintain purity and cleanliness, a woman will appear." King Wen appointed Tai Gong Wàng as the magistrate of Guantán. After one year, the wind did not rustle the leaves. King Wen dreamed of a beautiful woman who was weeping on the road. He asked her why she was crying. She said, "I am the daughter of Mount Tai, married to a man in the East Sea. I wish to return home, but the magistrate of Guantán, who has great virtue, is blocking my path. If I pass, there will be a great wind and heavy rain, which will damage his virtue." King Wen woke up and summoned Tai Gong Wàng to ask him about it. That day, there was indeed a sudden heavy rain and strong wind, which passed over Tai Gong Wàng's town. King Wen then appointed Tai Gong Wàng as the Grand Marshal.

Hu Mu Ban, courtesy name Ji You, was from Tai Shan. Once, while near the side of Mount Tai, he suddenly encountered a red-clothed horseman in the trees who called out to him, saying, "The deity of Mount Tai summons you." Hu Mu Ban was startled and hesitated before responding. Another horseman then emerged and called to him. He followed them for several dozen steps, and the horseman asked him to close his eyes briefly. Soon, he saw a grand palace with strict etiquette. Hu Mu Ban then entered the palace to pay respects, and the deity served him food. The deity said to him, "I wish to see you, not for any other reason, but to send a letter to my son-in-law." Hu Mu Ban asked, "Where is the lady?" The deity replied, "She is the wife of the river god." Hu Mu Ban then traveled westward, as instructed by the deity, and called out for a servant in green clothes. Soon, a female servant appeared, took the letter, and disappeared. Shortly after, she reappeared and said, "The river god wishes to briefly see you." The servant also asked him to close his eyes. Hu Mu Ban then paid respects to the river god, who prepared a grand feast and spoke kindly. Before leaving, the river god said to Hu Mu Ban, "I am grateful for your journey to deliver this letter, and I have no other gift to offer." He then ordered his attendants to bring his green silk shoes, which he gave to Hu Mu Ban. Hu Mu Ban then opened his eyes and suddenly found himself back on the boat. He spent a year in Chang An before returning. Upon reaching the side of Mount Tai, he did not dare to pass by unnoticed, so he knocked on a tree and introduced himself by name, having returned from Chang An, and wished to report his experiences.

A short while later, the former attendant appeared, leading 班 according to the proper procedure. He then delivered a letter. The official requested, "We should part." He reported to 班 again, finished speaking, and went to the toilet. Suddenly, he saw his father wearing chains and being a prisoner, along with hundreds of people like him. 班 approached, bowed, and wept, asking, "Why have you come to this situation, father?" His father replied, "Unfortunate, I died and was sent to the underworld for three years. Now it has been two years. The hardships are unbearable. Knowing that you are recognized by the official, you can present my request. I beg to be exempted from this labor. I merely wish to obtain the village god." 班 followed the instruction and bowed deeply to present his request. The official said, "Life and death are different paths and cannot be close. My life is nothing to be spared." 班 earnestly requested, and the official finally agreed. Thereupon, 班 took leave and returned home. After a year, almost all of his children died. Frightened, 班 went to Mount Tai again, tapped on the tree to request an audience. The former attendant welcomed him and let him see the official. 班 then explained, "My former words were inadequate. When I returned home, all my children died. Now fearing that the disaster has not yet ended, I came to inform, humbly requesting pity and rescue." The official clapped his hands and laughed, saying, "I formerly told you: life and death are different paths and cannot be close." He immediately ordered to summon 班's father. 班's father arrived shortly and was asked in the courtyard, "You formerly requested to return to the village god, should have brought blessings to the family, but the grandchildren died all. Why?" He answered, "Having been away from the village for a long time, I was glad to return. Moreover, I encountered ample food and drink, and truly missed my grandchildren, so I summoned them." Thereupon, he was replaced. His father wept and left. 班 then returned home. Afterwards, all his children were safe.

During the Song Dynasty, Feng Yi of Huayin, a village near the river embankment in the prefecture of Hongnong, drowned on the first day of the lunar month that fell on the "Shàng Gēng" (upper stem) while crossing the river. The Heavenly Emperor appointed him as the River Lord. The *Wuxing Shu* (Book of the Five Elements) also states: "The River Lord dies on the day of 'Gēngchén,' and one should not repair or travel by boat, as one will drown and not return." In the southern part of Yuhang County in Wu, there is a lake called Shanghu. In the center of the lake, there is a pond.
