One day, a man rode his horse to watch a performance, accompanied by three or four others. They went to Cen Village to drink, became slightly tipsy, and returned in the evening. It was hot, so he dismounted and lay down in the water, resting his head on a stone. His horse ran away on its own, and his companions all went after it, not returning until late in the evening. When he woke up, it was already approaching dusk, and he saw no one or his horse. A young woman, about sixteen or seventeen years old, appeared and said, "Lady respectfully greets you. The day is already late, and this place is very frightening. What are you going to do?" He asked, "What is your name, Lady? How did you suddenly appear before me?" Then a young man, about thirteen or fourteen years old, arrived, very intelligent, riding in a new carriage with twenty people following him. He called out for the man to get on the carriage and said, "The elder wants to see you briefly." They turned the carriage around and left. Along the way, they passed through a lit-up landscape, seeing cities and towns. After entering the city, they went to a hall where there was a banner inscribed with "River Lord's Message."

Suddenly, a man appeared, around thirty years old, with an appearance as beautiful as a painting, accompanied by many attendants. Both of them were pleased, and he commanded to serve wine, smiling and saying, "I have a daughter, who is quite intelligent, and I wish to give her to you as a wife." This person knew it was a spirit and dared not refuse. He then commanded: "Prepare everything for the marriage with the official." It was reported: "Everything is ready." So, he gave silk and linen robes, and gauze and silk skirts, gauze shirts, trousers, shoes, and sandals, all finely made. He also gave ten minor officials and dozens of attendants in green robes. The woman, around eighteen or nineteen years old, had a graceful and charming appearance, and they were married immediately. On the third day, a grand assembly of guests greeted the new bride. On the fourth day, he said, "The rites are limited, so send you away." The woman gave her husband a golden jar and a sachet of musk as a farewell gift, and they parted with tears. She also gave him ten thousand coins and three volumes of prescriptions, saying, "You can use these to perform good deeds and spread virtue." She added, "I will come to fetch you in ten years." This person returned home and refused to marry another. He left his family to become a monk. The three volumes of prescriptions included one volume on pulse diagnosis, one volume on decoctions, and one volume on pills. He traveled to heal and treat, and all his remedies were miraculously effective. After his mother grew old and his brother died, he returned to marriage and official duties.

In the 36th year of Qin Shi Huang's reign, the envoy Zheng Rong came from the east and was about to enter the Hangu Pass. Traveling west to Huayin, he saw a white carriage and white horse descending from Mount Hua. Suspecting it was not a human, he stopped on the road and waited for it. The figure approached and asked Zheng Rong, "Where are you going?" Zheng Rong replied, "To Xianyang." The figure on the carriage said, "I am the messenger of Mount Hua. I wish to entrust a scroll of writing to the lord of Haochi. When you go to Xianyang, pass by Haochi, see a large paulownia tree with inscribed stones, knock on the tree, and someone will respond." Zheng Rong took the scroll and followed the instructions. When he passed by Haochi and saw the large paulownia tree, he knocked on it with a stone, and indeed, someone came to take the message. The following year, Qin Shi Huang died.

Zhang Pu, courtesy name Gongzhi, was of unknown origin. He served as the governor of Wu Commandery and was summoned back, traveling via Mount Lu. His children watched in the shrine room, and a maid pointed to a statue and jokingly said, "To match you with this." That night, Zhang Pu's wife dreamed that Lord Lu sent a proposal saying, "My unworthy son is grateful for your choice and sends his humble intentions." The wife woke up feeling strange. The maid explained the situation. The wife became frightened and urged Zhang Pu to leave quickly. In the middle of the river, the boat would not move. The entire ship was shaken with fear. They then all threw objects into the water, but the boat still would not move. Someone said, "Throw a girl." Then the boat moved forward. Everyone said, "The spirit's intention is clear. To sacrifice one girl to destroy an entire family, what shall we do?" Zhang Pu said, "I cannot bear to see it." He then went up to the flying pavilion, lay down, and ordered his wife to drown the girl in the water. The wife then replaced her with the orphaned daughter of Zhang Pu's deceased elder brother. She placed a mat in the water, and the girl sat on it, and the boat then moved forward. When Zhang Pu saw the girl, he became angry and said, "How can I face the world with a face?" He then threw his own daughter. After crossing the river, he saw two girls far below. A clerk stood on the riverbank and said, "I am the main clerk of Lord Lu. Lord Lu thanks you. Knowing that spirits are not suitable matches, and respecting your righteousness, he has returned both girls to you." Later, when asked about it, the girls said, "We only saw good houses and officials, and did not realize we were in the water."

A minor official from Jiankang named Cao Zhe was welcomed by an envoy from Mount Lu and paired with a woman named Wan. Cao Zhe felt uneasy in body and mind and repeatedly requested to withdraw. Wan secretly shed tears and composed a poem to bid farewell. She also presented a finely woven undergarment and shirt. On the shores of Gongting Lake stood a temple dedicated to a solitary stone. Once, a merchant was traveling to the capital and passed by the temple. He saw two women who said, "Can you buy two pairs of silk shoes for us? We will reciprocate with great favor." The merchant reached the capital, bought fine silk shoes, and placed them in a box. He also bought a writing knife and put it in the box. After returning, he placed the box and incense in the temple and left, forgetting to take the writing knife. When he reached the middle of the river, a carp suddenly jumped into his boat. He opened the fish's belly and found the writing knife inside.

A man from Nanzhou sent an official to present a rhinoceros horn hairpin to Sun Quan. As the boat passed by the Palace Pavilion Temple, the official sought divine assistance. Suddenly, the deity issued an order: "I need your rhinoceros horn hairpin." The official, terrified and dared not respond, soon found the hairpin placed in front of him. The deity then said, "Wait until you reach the Stone City, and I will return your hairpin." The official, having no choice, continued on his journey, resigned to losing the hairpin and facing the death penalty. By the time he reached the Stone City, a large carp, three feet long, suddenly jumped into the boat. When he cut it open, he found the hairpin inside. Later, Guo Pu crossed the river, and Yin You, the governor of Xuancheng, appointed him as a military advisor. At that time, a creature as large as a water buffalo appeared. It was gray in color, had short legs, feet like an elephant's, and was white on its chest and tail. It was strong but slow, and it came to the city gate, amazing everyone. Yin You ordered someone to ambush and capture it. He instructed Guo Pu to cast a divination, which resulted in the hexagram of Retreat and the hexagram of Influence. The creature was named "Donkey Rat." Just as the divination was completed, the ambushers stabbed it with a spear, more than a foot deep. The county official offered a sacrifice and requested its killing. The shaman said, "The temple deity is displeased. This is the envoy of the Donkey Mountain Lord of Pingting. It came from Jing Mountain, just passing by, so there's no need to harm it." The creature then left and was never seen again.

Lu Ling Ou Ming, accompanying merchants, passed by Pengze Lake, and often threw items from his boat into the lake, saying, "As a ritual offering." After several years, he passed by again and suddenly saw a wide road in the lake, with much dust on it, and several officials came by carriage and horse to greet him, saying, "This is the envoy of the Lord of Qinghong." Soon, they reached the place, and there was an official residence, with officials and soldiers under the door. Ou Ming was very frightened. The official said, "There is nothing to be afraid of! The Lord of Qinghong is grateful for your previous and current offerings, so he invites you. He will surely give you a valuable gift. Do not take anything else, just seek 'Ruyuan.'" After seeing the Lord of Qinghong, Ou Ming sought "Ruyuan." The official sent Ruyuan to follow Ou Ming back. Ruyuan was a servant of the Lord of Qinghong. Ou Ming took Ruyuan back with him, and all his wishes were fulfilled. Within a few years, he became very wealthy. To the west of Yizhou and the east of Yunnan, there is a shrine carved out of the mountain and stone. Below the shrine, there is a deity worshiped there, who calls himself Huang Gong. He says, "This deity is the spirit of Zhang Liang's teacher, the Yellow Stone Elder." He is pure and does not kill animals. Those who pray hold one hundred coins, a pair of brushes, and a ball of ink, and place them in the stone room. They go forward to ask for something, and first hear a voice from the stone room. Soon, they ask, "What does the person desire?" After they speak, they clearly tell about good or bad fortune, but cannot see his form. It is still like this today. During the Yongjia period, a spirit appeared in Yanzhou, calling himself Fan Daoji. There was a woman, called Cheng's Lady. She loved music and could play the konghou. When she heard people singing and playing music, she immediately started dancing.

Dai Wenmou, a man from Pei State, lived in seclusion in Yangcheng Mountain. Once, while in his guest hall, during a meal, he suddenly heard a god call out, "I am a messenger of the Heavenly Emperor. I wish to descend and rely on you. Is that acceptable?" Dai Wenmou was greatly surprised. The god said, "You doubt me." Dai Wenmou then knelt and said, "I live in poverty, I fear I am not worthy enough for you to descend." After that, he swept and cleaned, set up a place, and offered food morning and evening, being very careful. Later, in the room, he whispered to his wife, "This might be an evil spirit relying on us." His wife replied, "I also doubt it." When the time for the offering came, the god said, "I am here to help you, I did not expect you to have doubts." While Dai Wenmou was apologizing, suddenly there were shouts like dozens of people in the hall. Looking out, he saw a large bird with five colors, followed by dozens of white pigeons, flying northeast into the clouds and disappearing. Mi Zhu, courtesy name Zizhong, was from Qiu, Donghai. His ancestors were engaged in trade, and his family had immense wealth. He often traveled from Luoyang, and before reaching home by dozens of miles, he saw a beautiful young woman on the road who asked him for a ride. After traveling about twenty miles, the woman thanked him and said, "I am a messenger of the Heavenly Emperor. I am going to burn Mi Zhu's house in Donghai. I am grateful for your kindness, so I am telling you this." Mi Zhu then privately asked her, "Can you not burn it? If you can, please tell me." The woman said, "I cannot avoid it. If you want to save your house, you should hurry. I will move slowly, and by noon, the fire will start." Mi Zhu then hurried home, and by the time he arrived, he had moved all his valuables outside. By noon, the fire had started and spread rapidly.

During the reign of Emperor Xuan of Han, there was a man from Nanyang named Yin Zifang, who was extremely filial. He accumulated kindness and loved to give. He enjoyed worshiping the stove god. On the day of the New Year's Eve, while cooking in the morning, the stove god appeared in human form. Yin Zifang bowed twice and received the blessing, and since his family had yellow sheep, he used them to offer sacrifices. After this, he suddenly became immensely wealthy. He owned over seven hundred acres of land, carriages, horses, and servants, comparable to a feudal lord. Yin Zifang once said: "My descendants will surely become powerful, recognized for three generations, and thus flourish." His family had four marquises and governed dozens of regions. Therefore, after that, his descendants would always offer yellow sheep on the day of the New Year's Eve. In Wu County, there was a man named Zhang Cheng. One night, he woke up and suddenly saw a woman standing at the south corner of his house, raising her hand to call him. She said: "This is your family's silkworm room. I am the deity of this place. Next year on the fifteenth day of the first lunar month, you should make white porridge and pour oil on top." From then on, he had a great harvest of silkworms every year. Today's practice of making porridge with oil is similar to this. In Yuanchang, there was a woman from the Dai family who had been ill for a long time without recovery. She saw a small stone statue resembling a human figure and said to it: "You have a human form, are you a god? If you can cure my chronic illness, I will honor you." That night, she dreamed that someone told her: "I will protect you." After that, her illness gradually improved. Thus, a shrine was built at the foot of the mountain. The Dai family became shamans, and the shrine was named Dai Hou Temple. In Yangxi, there was a man named Liu (Wangsi), the magistrate, who once said: "I will become a god after my death." One night, he drank to intoxication and died without illness. In the rain and wind, his coffin was lost. At night, they heard the sounds of thousands of people eating in Jing Mountain. The villagers went to see, and the coffin had already turned into a mound. Thus, it was renamed Jun Mountain, and a shrine was established to worship him.

**Volume Five of the Search for Spirits** Jiang Ziwén, a man from Guangling, was a notorious drunkard, a lecher, and a provocateur without bounds. He often said to himself, "My bones are pure; after my death, I shall become a deity." At the end of the Han dynasty, he served as a magistrate in Moling. While pursuing bandits to the foot of Zhong Mountain, the bandits struck him on the forehead, and he tied them up with his sash. Shortly thereafter, he died. During the early reign of the Wu state's founding emperor, his former officials saw him on the road, mounted on a white horse, holding a white feather, and accompanied by attendants as in life. Those who saw him fled in terror. Jiang Ziwén pursued them, saying, "I shall become the land deity of this place, bringing blessings to your people. You should inform the common folk to build an altar for me. If you do not, there will be great calamity." That summer, a major epidemic struck, and the people secretly feared and moved about, with some secretly building altars. Jiang Ziwén then descended through a medium, saying, "I will greatly protect and bless the Sun family; you should build an altar for me. If you do not, I will send small insects into people's ears, causing disaster." Soon, tiny insects like horseflies entered people's ears, killing them, and doctors could not cure them. The people were even more terrified. The Sun ruler did not believe this. Jiang Ziwén then descended again through a medium, saying, "If you do not worship me, I will cause a great fire disaster." That year, fires broke out in many places, reaching the imperial palace. The court believed that if the spirit had a place to rest, it would not cause harm, and they should pacify it. Thus, they sent envoys to confer Jiang Ziwén as the Marquis of Zhongdu and his younger brother Jiang Xù as the Colonel of Changshui, both with seals and ribbons. They built a temple for him. They renamed Zhong Mountain as Jiang Mountain, which is now located northeast of Jiankang. From then on, the disasters ceased, and the people greatly revered him.

Liu Chifu, a man, dreamed that Jiang Hou summoned him to serve as his secretary. The deadline was pressing, so he went to the temple to make a plea: "My mother is old, my child is young, and the circumstances are too dire. I humbly request your pardon. Wei Guo from Kuaiji is highly talented and skilled in serving the gods; I request that he be nominated to take my place." He knelt and bled. The temple priest said, "I especially wish to trouble you, but who is Wei Guo, and why should he be nominated?" Liu Chifu persisted in his request, but it was finally refused, and soon after, Liu Chifu died. During the Xianning era, the Grand Master of Rites, Han Bo, the Governor of Kuaiji, Wang Yun, and the Grand Attendant, Liu Dan, visited the Jiang Hou temple together. The temple had several statues of women, all very beautiful. The officials were drunk and pointed to the statues to playfully match them with each other. That very night, the three of them had the same dream in which Jiang Hou sent a messenger to inform them, saying, "Our daughters are all plain, yet you have honored us with your attention." He set a date, saying, "We will welcome you all." The officials, finding their dreams unusually consistent, decided to visit the temple to inquire, and indeed, they all had the same dream, perfectly matched. They were greatly alarmed. They prepared three sacrificial animals and went to the temple to beg for forgiveness. They also had another dream in which Jiang Hou personally came to them and said, "Since you have already honored us, we are truly greedy, but the appointed date is imminent; how can you now change your mind?" After a short time, all of them died.

A Woman Named Wangzi in Kuaiji's Yuyao County In the eastern countryside of Yuyao County in Kuaiji Commandery, there lived a girl named Wangzi, whose surname was Wu. She was sixteen years old and possessed a beautiful appearance. Her village had a ritual performer who could invoke deities through drumming and dancing, and he invited her to participate. She agreed and followed him. As they walked along the pond, halfway through their journey, she suddenly saw a nobleman who was exceptionally handsome. The nobleman was riding in a boat, accompanied by more than ten strong men. He had someone ask Wangzi, "Where do you wish to go?" She replied in detail about the situation. The nobleman said, "I am also heading there; you may come aboard and go with me." Wangzi politely declined. Suddenly, the nobleman disappeared. After Wangzi had worshiped at the altar, she saw the nobleman from the boat, sitting upright and composed, which turned out to be the image of Jiang Hou. He asked Wangzi, "Why did you come so late?" and then threw two oranges at her. Over time, the nobleman appeared repeatedly, and their affection deepened. Whenever Wangzi desired something, it would descend from the sky. Once, she thought of eating a pair of fish, and fresh fish appeared as if by her thoughts. Wangzi's fragrance spread for several miles, and there were many miraculous occurrences. The entire county served and worshiped her. After three years, Wangzi suddenly developed external thoughts, and the nobleman ceased to appear.

Xie Yu of Chen Commandery, serving as the governor of Langya Commandery, was in the capital city where tigers frequently attacked and killed many people. One day, a man took his young wife on a small boat, with a large knife placed on the boat, and arrived at a patrol post in the evening. The patrol officer spoke to them, saying, "This area has recently become overgrown with weeds. You are traveling in such a small boat, which is quite risky. It would be better to stay here for the night." After their conversation, the patrol officer returned to his duties. The woman stepped onto the shore and was immediately taken by a tiger. Her husband drew his knife, shouted, and tried to chase after the tiger. He had previously been devoted to Lord Jiang, so he called for help. They continued their journey for ten miles when suddenly a mysterious black-clad figure appeared as a guide. The man followed the guide, and they traveled another twenty miles to a large tree and a cave. The tiger cubs, hearing the sound, thought their mother had returned and ran out. The man killed the cubs. He then hid behind the tree with his knife for a long time. When the tiger returned, it dragged the woman to the ground and carried her into the cave. The man cut the tiger in half with his knife. The tiger died, and the woman survived. In the morning, she regained consciousness and could speak. When asked about what happened, she said, "The tiger initially carried me on its back and then dropped me when it reached the cave. I have no other injuries, only those from the grass and trees." They helped her back to the boat. That night, she dreamed of a person who told her, "Lord Jiang has come to help you, did you know?" When they returned home, she sacrificed a pig to Lord Jiang.

In the county of Quanjiao in Huainan, there was a young woman named Ding, originally from the Ding family of Danyang. She was sixteen years old when she married into the Xie family of Quanjiao. Her mother-in-law was harsh and cruel, imposing strict time limits on tasks, and those who failed to meet them were beaten mercilessly. On the ninth day of the ninth lunar month, she hanged herself. She then became a spirit and was known among the people. Through a shaman, she spoke and said, "Consider the women of ordinary households, who work tirelessly. Let them avoid the ninth day of the ninth lunar month and refrain from work." She appeared in a light blue robe, wearing a green canopy, accompanied by a maid, and sought to cross the Niu Zhu Ferry. There were two men fishing together, and she called out to them for help. The two men laughed and teased her, saying, "If you become our wife, we will help you cross." The spirit replied, "I thought you were good men, but you know nothing. If you are human, I will make you die in the mud; if you are spirits, I will make you drown." She then retreated into the grass. Soon after, an old man came by boat, carrying reeds.

The old woman asked to be ferried across. The old man said, "The boat has no load, how can one cross without being properly loaded? I fear it might not be suitable for carrying." The old woman said it was no trouble. The old man then took out half a bundle of reeds, placed them in the boat, and slowly ferried her across. Upon reaching the south bank, as he was about to leave, he said to the old man, "I am a ghost or spirit, not a human. I can cross by myself, but it is appropriate to make the common people vaguely aware. Your kindness in ferrying me across with the reeds deeply moved me, and I will have something to thank you with. If you quickly return, you will surely see something and gain something." The old man said, "I fear that the dry and wet conditions won't be suitable, so how could I dare to accept your thanks?" The old man returned to the west bank and saw two men lying in the water. He walked several miles ahead and found thousands of fish jumping at the water's edge, blown by the wind onto the shore. The old man then abandoned the reeds, loaded the fish, and returned home. Thus, the old woman returned to Dan Yang. People in southern Jiangnan all called her Ding Gu. On the ninth day of the ninth month, they do not work, considering it a day of rest. Now, she is worshipped everywhere.

The Scattered Rider (Scatter Rider) Wang You, gravely ill, bid farewell to his mother. Soon after, he heard a visitor arrive and say, “From a certain county, a certain village, a certain person, who once served as a Deputy Governor.” Wang You had also heard of this person’s name before. After a while, the visitor suddenly appeared and said, “I share a natural affinity with you and your class of officials, and we are also from the same region, so we are quite familiar with each other. This year, the state has important affairs, and three generals have been dispatched to conscript more than ten of us to serve as assistants in the administration of Lord Zhao. In this sudden situation, I saw that you have a grand mansion and large house, so I have come to seek your aid, and we get along very well—it is hard to describe.” Wang You realized the visitor was a spirit or ghost and said, “Unluckily, my illness is severe, and death is imminent. I have encountered you, and I entrust my life to you.” The visitor replied, “Life must end; this is an inevitable fate. The status of the deceased is not determined by their rank in life. I now command an army of three thousand, and I need you to handle the documents for me. A position like this is rare and hard to obtain—you should not refuse it.” Wang You said, “My mother is old, and I have no brothers. If I were to die, there would be no one to support her.” He then wept bitterly, unable to control himself. The visitor, moved with sorrow, said, “Your position is that of a high-ranking official, yet your family has no surplus wealth. I heard you had said goodbye to your esteemed wife, and your words were filled with sorrow. Then you are indeed a national hero—how can you be allowed to die? I shall help you.” He then got up and left. The next day, he came again. On the following day, he came once more.

佑曰：“卿许活吾，当卒恩否？”答曰：“大老子业已许卿，当复相欺耶！”见其从者数百人，皆长二尺许，乌衣军服，赤油为志。佑家击鼓祷祀，诸鬼闻鼓声，皆应节起舞，振袖飒飒有声。佑将为设酒食。辞曰：“不须。”因复起去。谓佑曰：“病在人体中，如火。当以水解之。”因取一杯水，发被灌之。又曰：“为卿留赤笔十余枝，在荐下，可与人使簪之。出入辟恶灾，举事皆无恙。”因道曰：“王甲、李乙，吾皆与之。”遂执佑手与辞。时佑得安眠，夜中忽觉，乃呼左右，令开被，“神以水灌我，将大沾濡。”开被。而信有水在上被之下，下被之上，不浸，如露之在荷。量之，得三升七合。于是疾三分愈二。数日。大除。凡其所道当取者，皆死亡。唯王文英，半年后乃亡。所道与赤笔人，皆经疾病及兵乱，皆亦无恙。初，有妖书云：“上帝以三将军赵公明、钟士季各督数鬼下取人。”莫知所在。佑病差，见此书，与所道赵公明合焉。 佑 said, "You promised to save me, will you fulfill your promise?" The other replied, "I, the elder, have already promised you, how could I deceive you again!" He saw hundreds of followers, all about two feet tall, dressed in black military uniforms with red oil as a mark. At Yu's home, drums were beaten for prayers and offerings, and upon hearing the drum sounds, all the spirits danced in rhythm, their sleeves rustling loudly. Yu intended to prepare wine and food for them. They declined, saying, "It is not necessary." Then they stood up and left. One of them told Yu, "The illness within your body is like fire. It should be extinguished with water." He then took a cup of water and poured it over Yu's head while he was under the covers. Another said, "I have left you more than ten red pens under the mat. You can give them to others to wear. This will protect against evil and misfortune, and ensure safety in all your endeavors." They mentioned, "Wang Jia and Li Yi, I have given them to both of you." They then took Yu's hand and bid farewell. At that time, Yu was able to sleep soundly, and in the middle of the night, he suddenly woke up and called for his attendants, telling them to open the bedding, "A spirit has poured water on me, it will soak me." They opened the bedding, and indeed, there was water between the top and bottom layers, not seeping through, like dew on a lotus leaf. They measured it and found it to be three liters and seven shengs. As a result, his illness improved by two-thirds. After several days, he was completely recovered. All those mentioned as being taken away had died. Only Wang Wenyi died six months later. Those given red pens, having gone through illness and war, remained unharmed. Initially, there was a magical book that said, "The Heavenly Deity has sent three generals, Zhao Gongming and Zhong Shiji, each leading several spirits to take people." Their whereabouts were unknown. After Yu recovered from his illness, he saw this book, and it matched the description of Zhao Gongming.

Zhou Shi from Xiangpu in the Han dynasty once traveled to the Eastern Sea. On the road, he met a government official who was carrying a scroll and asked Zhou Shi to let him ride. After traveling for more than ten miles, the official said to Zhou Shi, "I will be gone for a short while. Please keep this book in your boat and be careful not to open it." After the official left, Zhou Shi secretly opened the book and found it to be a record of all the dead people. His name was listed below. Soon, the official returned, and Zhou Shi was still looking at the book. The official became angry and said, "I specifically warned you not to open it, but you disregarded my warning!" Zhou Shi knelt down and begged for mercy, bleeding from his head. After a long while, the official said, "I am grateful for your kindness in letting me ride. This book cannot remove your name. You have already passed the point of no return. Return home and do not leave your house for three years, and you can be saved. Do not tell anyone about seeing my book." Zhou Shi returned home and did not go out. After two years, his family began to wonder why he was staying indoors. One day, a neighbor died suddenly, and Zhou Shi's father became furious and sent him to attend the funeral. Zhou Shi had no choice but to step out the door. As soon as he stepped outside, he saw the official again. The official said, "I told you not to leave your house for three years, but now you have gone out. What can I do? If you had not shown yourself, you would have avoided trouble and punishment. Now that you have been seen, there is nothing I can do. In three days, at noon, I will come to take you." Zhou Shi returned home, crying and telling his parents what had happened. His father did not believe him. His mother guarded him day and night. On the third day at noon, the official indeed came to take him, and Zhou Shi died.

Zhang Zhu of Nandun was sowing grain in a field when he saw a plum stone and wanted to take it away. Glancing back, he saw an empty mulberry tree with soil in it, so he planted the stone and watered it with leftover water. Later, people saw that the plum tree grew repeatedly in the mulberry tree and spread the news. Someone with an eye ailment rested in the shade and said, "The plum tree has healed my eye. I will thank it with a pig." The eye ailment, though minor, also healed on its own. The news spread like wildfire, and the blind regained their sight. The area became famous, and thousands of carriages and riders arrived, bringing abundant food and drink. After more than a year, Zhang Zhu returned from a journey and saw the tree. He was surprised and said, "What kind of god is this? It is just what I planted." He then cut it down. During the regency of Wang Mang, Liu Jing reported to the emperor, "Xindang, the village head of Linzi County in Qi Province, often dreamed of someone saying, 'I am a messenger of heaven. The acting emperor should become the true emperor. If you do not believe me, a new well will appear in this village.' The village head looked and found a new well that went a hundred feet deep."

*Records of Searching for the Gods, Volume Six* Monsters and spirits are manifestations of spiritual energy attached to physical objects. When spiritual energy is in chaos within, physical objects change outwardly; form, spirit, and essence are the inner and outer workings. Their origin lies in the Five Elements, and they connect to the Five Constant Virtues. Though they undergo rise and fall, transformation in myriad forms, all signs of auspiciousness or misfortune can be categorized and discussed. During the reign of Jie of Xia, Mount Lishan disappeared; during the reign of Qin Shi Huang, the Three Mountains vanished; in the thirty-second year of King Xian of Zhou, the grand shrine of Song's Daqiuyue disappeared; at the end of Emperor Zhao of Han's reign, the shrine of Changyi, Tengzhou, vanished. The *Book of Changes* (易传) by Jing Fang states: "When mountains move silently, it is a sign of widespread warfare and the fall of the state." Therefore, in Shanxi, Shanxi, there is a strange mountain known as the "Ghost Mountain," which is said to have originated from Mount Dongwu in Dongwu County, by the sea. One night, it was dark with wind and rain, and the next morning, Mount Dongwu was found there. The people were astonished and named it the "Ghost Mountain." At the same time, the mountain in Dongwu County also disappeared overnight. Those who recognized its shape knew it had moved there. Today, there is a place called Dongwuli at the foot of the Ghost Mountain, which is named after the mountain's origin. Additionally, a mountain from Jiaozhou moved to Qingzhou. All such movements of mountains are extraordinary events that defy explanation. These two events are not detailed in the records of their respective eras. The *Book of Documents* (尚书) in the chapter *Jinteng* (金縢) states: "When mountains move, it is a sign that the ruler is not following the Dao, that the wise and virtuous are not rising, that the fiefdoms are losing their wealth, that rewards and punishments are not decided by the ruler, and that private factions are forming. If this is not corrected, it will lead to a change of dynasty and era."

The Art of Understanding Heaven and Humanity One said, "Those who speak well about heaven must ground themselves in people; those who speak well about people must base themselves on heaven." Thus, heaven has four seasons, with the sun and moon pushing each other, cold and heat alternating, and their cycles turning. When harmonious, they become rain; when angry, they become wind; when dispersed, they become dew; when chaotic, they become fog; when condensed, they become frost and snow; when standing, they become earth formations. These are the constant rules of heaven. Humans have four limbs and five organs, experiencing one waking and one sleeping, breathing in and out, with vital energy flowing and circulating, manifesting as the circulation of blood and qi, appearing as facial complexion, and expressing as sounds. This is also the constant rule of humans. If the seasons lose their order and cold and heat go awry, then the five planets will fluctuate, stars will move erratically, the sun and moon will experience eclipses, comets will fly, and this is a critical sign of the state of heaven and earth. If cold and heat are not in season, this is a sign of the stagnation of heaven and earth. Rocks standing upright and soil rising indicate tumors on the earth; landslides and sinkholes indicate ulcers on the earth; strong winds and heavy rains indicate the earth's outburst of energy; if rain does not fall and rivers and streams dry up, this is a sign of the earth's desiccation. During the reign of King Zhou of Shang, a large turtle grew hair, and a rabbit grew horns, indicating the impending rise of military forces. In the thirty-third year of King Xuan of Zhou's reign, King You of Zhou was born, and in that year, a horse transformed into a fox, indicating a significant and possibly ominous event.

In the second year of Duke Xian of Jin, King Huai of Zhou resided in Zheng, and the people of Zheng entered the royal treasury, where many items transformed into yu, mythical creatures that shot at people. In the second month of the fourth year of King Yin of Zhou, a plant in Qi grew abnormally, over ten feet tall and one foot five inches wide. According to Jing Fang's *I Ching* commentary, "abnormal growth of the earth is a sign: spring and summer bring good omens, while autumn and winter bring bad omens." In the county of Liyang, one night, a part of the land sank into the ground and became a marsh, now known as Mah Lake. It is unknown when this occurred. Yun Dou Shu said, "The sinking of the city, swallowing the sun, and the destruction of the lower parts." In the eighth year of King Ai of Zhou, a woman in Zheng gave birth to forty children, twenty of whom became human, and twenty died. In the ninth year, a pig in Jin gave birth to a human. In the seventh year of the Red Bird period in Wu, a woman gave birth to three children. In the sixth year of King Lie of Zhou, the attendant of Lin Biyang Jun gave birth to two dragons. In the eighth year of Duke Yan of Lu, Duke Xiang of Qi hunted in Bei Qiu and saw a pig. His attendants said, "It is Prince Pengsheng." The prince angrily shot it, and the pig stood up and roared. The prince was frightened and fell off the carriage, injuring his foot and losing his shoes. Liu Xiang believed this was a sign of pig-related disaster. During the reign of Duke Yan of Lu, an inner snake and an outer snake fought in the south gate of Zheng. The inner snake died. Liu Xiang believed this was a sign of snake-related disaster. Jing Fang's *I Ching* commentary said, "Doubt in the succession of the heir leads to the omen of snakes fighting at the national gate." In the nineteenth year of Duke Zhao of Lu, dragons gathered outside the gates of Zheng in Wei Yuan. Liu Xiang believed this was a sign of dragon-related disaster. Jing Fang's *I Ching* commentary said, "The people's hearts are uneasy, leading to the omen of many dragons in the city."

During the first year of Duke Ding of Lu's reign, nine snakes were seen coiling around a pillar. Diviners interpreted this as an omen that the ancestral temple would not be worshipped for nine generations, and thus they established a new palace named "炀宫." In the twenty-first year of Duke Xiao of Qin's reign, a horse gave birth to a human. In the twentieth year of King Zhao of Qin's reign, a male horse gave birth to a foal and died in the process. Liu Xiang believed that these events were all signs of horse-related disasters. Jing Fang's *Yijing* commentary states: "When regional leaders divide power, the omen is a male horse giving birth. When there is no central authority and feudal lords wage war against each other, the omen is a horse giving birth to a human." In the thirteenth year of Duke Xiang of Wei's reign, a woman transformed into a man and had a child with her wife. Jing Fang's *Yijing* commentary states: "When a woman transforms into a man, it is a sign of the rise of a lowly person to power. When a man transforms into a woman, it is a sign of the decline of male authority and the rise of female influence." Another interpretation is: "When men transform into women, it indicates excessive castration; when women transform into men, it indicates the implementation of women's governance." In the fifth year of King Wen of Qin's reign, during a tour in Xuyang, a five-legged cow was presented as a gift. At that time, the State of Qin was heavily taxing and forcing labor, leading to widespread rebellion. Jing Fang's *Yijing* commentary states: "When excessive labor and the taking of people's time occur, the omen is a five-legged cow." In the twenty-sixth year of Emperor Qin Shi Huang's reign, twelve giants, each five meters tall and wearing barbarian clothing, appeared in Lintao. The emperor then ordered the creation of twelve bronze statues to represent them. In the second year of Emperor Hui of Han's reign, on the first day of the first month (癸酉), two dragons appeared in a well in the eastern part of Lanling. They disappeared by the night of the following day (乙亥). Jing Fang's *Yijing* commentary states: "When a virtuous person suffers, the omen is dragons appearing in a well." It also states: "When excessive and brutal punishment is carried out, the omen is a black dragon emerging from a well."

During the twelfth year of Emperor Wen of Han, a horse in the Wu region grew horns in front of its ears, pointing upwards. The right horn was three inches long, the left horn two inches long, and both were two inches wide. Duan Xia believed that a horse should not grow horns, just as Wu should not have raised arms against the central authority. This was seen as an omen of rebellion by Wu. Jing Fang's commentary on the *I Ching* states: "Officials replace superiors, leading to political disorder. The supernatural phenomenon of a horse growing horns. This indicates a shortage of virtuous officials." It also says: "If the emperor personally leads a campaign, a horse will grow horns." In the sixth month of the fifth year of Emperor Wen's posthumous reign, a dog in Qí outside the city gate of Yōng grew horns. Jing Fang's commentary on the *I Ching* states: "Officials fail to govern properly, leading to harm. The supernatural phenomenon of a dog growing horns." In the ninth month of the first year of Emperor Jing's reign, a person over seventy years old in Xia Mi, Jiadong, grew horns, with hair on the horns. Jing Fang's commentary on the *I Ching* states: "The chief minister exercises excessive power. The supernatural phenomenon of a person growing horns." The *Wuxing Zhi* (Treatise on the Five Elements) states that a person should not grow horns, just as feudal lords should not raise arms against the capital. Later, there was the rebellion of the Seven States. By the fifth year of the Tai Shi era of Emperor Wu of Jin, a person in Yuan Cheng, seventy years old, grew horns. This was likely a sign of the chaos caused by Zhao Wang Lun. In the third year of Emperor Jing's reign, a dog in Handan mated with a pig. At that time, the King of Zhao was behaving in a rebellious manner, joining with six other states in rebellion and forming an alliance with the Xiongnu for support. The *Wuxing Zhi* states that a dog mating with a pig is a sign of military disorder, with the pig representing the Xiongnu. Ignoring advice and mating with different species leads to harm. Jing Fang's commentary on the *I Ching* states: "If husband and wife are not strict, the supernatural phenomenon of a dog mating with a pig will occur. This indicates a reversal of virtue, leading to internal strife."

During the eleventh month of the third year of Emperor Jing's reign, there was a fight between white-necked crows and black crows in Lü County of the State of Chu: the white-necked crows were defeated, and thousands of them fell into the Si River and died. Liu Xiang interpreted this as a sign of good or bad fortune. At that time, King Wu of Chu was tyrannical and rebellious, punishing and humiliating Shen Gong, and conspiring with Wu to rebel against the Han dynasty. The fight between the crows was seen as a symbol of military conflict. The smaller white-necked crows represented the weaker side in the conflict. The crows that fell into the water were seen as predicting a watery death. King Wu of Chu did not realize the significance of the omen and subsequently raised an army to join Wu in rebellion. He then fought a great battle with the Han forces, suffered a defeat, and retreated to Dan Tu. He was ultimately killed by the Yue people, fulfilling the prediction of his watery death. Jing Fang's commentary on the *Yijing* states, "Rebellion against close relatives brings the omen of white and black crows fighting in the capital." During King Dan of Yan's rebellion, there was another incident where a crow and a magpie fought in the pool of the Yan palace, and the crow fell into the pool and died. The *Wuxing Zhi* interpreted both events as omens of rebellion by close relatives of the emperor, both of whom were proud and rebellious, and both had the omen of crows and magpies fighting and dying. The events were similar in nature and interpretation, clearly showing the interaction between heaven and humanity. The Yan plot was not discovered, and the king committed suicide in the palace, hence the crow that fell into the water died; King Wu of Chu raised his army in the heat of the sun, suffered a major defeat in the field, hence the black crows died: these were the subtle effects of the will of heaven. Jing Fang's commentary on the *Yijing* states, "Violence and rebellion bring the omen of crows and magpies fighting."

In the sixteenth year of Emperor Jing's reign, Duke Liang of Liang was hunting in the northern mountains when a cow was presented to him, with its hooves protruding from its back. Liu Xiang believed this was a sign of a cow disaster, indicating internal confusion and external excessive earthworks. The hooves protruding from the back symbolize lower elements undermining the upper ones. In the fourth month of the seventh year of Emperor Wu's Taishi era, a snake entered from outside the city wall of Zhao and fought with a snake from the city against the temple of Emperor Wen. The city's snake died. Two years later in autumn, there was an incident involving the Crown Prince Wei, initiated by Jiang Chong from Zhao. In the ninth month of the first year of Emperor Zhao's Yuanfeng era, a yellow rat appeared in Yan and entered from outside the city wall. It fought with a snake from the city against the temple of Emperor Wen, and the city's snake died. At that time, Prince Yan Dan was plotting rebellion, and this was a sign of impending death. According to the commentary on the *I Ching* by Jing Fang, "Not considering the circumstances of the crime, the omen is a rat dancing at the gate."

In the first month of the third year of the Yuanshi era during Emperor Zhao's reign, there was a loud noise resembling thousands of people coming from the south of Mount Wulai on Mount Tai. When the locals went to investigate, they found a large stone standing up by itself. The stone was fifteen feet tall, had a circumference of forty-eight feet, was embedded eight feet deep into the ground, and had three stones as its base. After the stone stood up, thousands of white crows gathered around it. This event was considered a sign of the revival under Emperor Xuan. During Emperor Zhao's reign, in the Shanglin Park, a large willow tree broke and fell to the ground. One day, it stood up again and grew branches and leaves. Insects ate the leaves, forming the words "Gongsun Bingyi has been established." During Emperor Zhao's reign, King Changyi of Changyi saw a large white dog wearing a "Fangshan hat" but without a tail. By the Xiping period, inside the palace, dogs were worn with sashes for amusement. One day, a dog suddenly ran out and entered the door of the Siku official's residence, and those who saw it were all surprised. Jing Fang's *Yi* transmission states: "If the ruler is not upright, ministers may attempt to usurp power, and the sign of this is a dog wearing a hat appearing at the palace gate." In the year Huanglong of Emperor Xuan, a hen in the Luoling area of Weiyang Palace turned into a rooster. The hen turned into a rooster but did not crow, did not lead, and had no spurs. In the year Chuyuan of Emperor Yuan, a hen in the household of a historian in the Chancellor's office gradually turned into a rooster with spurs, crowing, and leading. By the year Yongguang, someone presented a rooster with horns. The Five Elements Records considered these events signs of the Wang family's rise. Jing Fang's *Yi* transmission states: "If a wise person lives in a dark age and feels sorrow over many people in power, the sign of this is a rooster with horns." It also states: "If women rule, the country will not be peaceful, and if a hen crows like a rooster, the ruler will not be honored."

During the reign of Emperor Xuan, in the region between Yan and Dai, there were three men who shared one wife and had four sons. When it came time to divide the family and the children could not be equally distributed, a dispute arose. The Grand Judge Fan Yan寿 adjudicated and said, "This is not human; it should be treated as an animal, following the mother rather than the father." He proposed executing the three men and returning the child to the mother. Emperor Xuan sighed and said, "Why must things be done according to ancient practices? If this is the case, it can be said to be in accordance with reason but unsatisfactory to human emotions." Fan Yan寿 saw the human situation and knew how to apply punishment, but he did not know the future implications of human anomalies. In the eighth month of the second year of Emperor Yuan's Yongguang era, it rained grass, and the leaves intertwined, as large as bullets. In the first month of the third year of Emperor Ping's Yuanshi era, it rained grass again, similar to the time of Yongguang. Jing Fang's *Yijing* commentary says, "The ruler is stingy with rewards, trust is declining, and the virtuous are leaving. The anomaly is the rain of grass." In the fifth year of Emperor Yuan's Jianzhao era, the governor of Yan Prefecture, Hao Shang, prohibited the people from privately establishing shrines. In the shrine of Tuomao Village, Shanyang, there was a large locust tree. An official cut it down, but that night it stood up again in its original place. It is said, "Any dead thing that rises again is a sign of abandonment and revival." This is the response of the founding ancestor.

In the fourth year of the Jian Shi era during the reign of Emperor Cheng of Han, in the ninth month, in the southern part of Chang'an, there were mice carrying yellow straw and cypress leaves, building nests on cypress trees and willows above graves, with the most occurrences in Tongbai. The nests were empty and contained several sheng of dried mouse droppings. At the time, the ministers believed this was a sign of an impending flood. Mice, which are small and steal, usually come out at night and hide during the day, but now they were active during the day, leaving their burrows and climbing trees, which was seen as an omen that lowly people would soon occupy high positions. Tongbai was the location of the garden of Empress Wei Si. Later, Empress Zhao rose from a lowly position to become the most honored, similar to Empress Wei Si. Empress Zhao remained childless and caused harm. The following year, there was a sign of an eagle burning its nest and killing its young. According to Jing Fang's *Commentary on the Changes*, "Officials taking private benefits would lead to the omen of mice building nests." In the first year of the He Ping era during the reign of Emperor Cheng of Han, in the second month on the day Gengzi, in the Shan Sang Valley of Mount Tai, there was an eagle burning its nest. Men named Sun Tong and others heard the sounds of birds and eagles in the mountains and went to see. They found the nest burning and falling into a pond, with three eagle chicks dying. The tree was four zhi around, and the nest was five zhang and five chi above the ground. The *I Ching* says, "When the bird's nest is burned, the traveler first laughs and then weeps." This eventually led to a change in the ruling dynasty. In the first year of the He Ping era during the reign of Emperor Cheng of Han, in the second month, in Chang'an, two men named Shi Liang and Liu Yin lived together. There was a figure resembling a human in their room. When they attacked it, it turned into a dog and ran away. After it left, several men in armor with bows and arrows arrived at Shi Liang's house. Shi Liang and others fought them, and some died or were injured, and they were all dogs. This continued from the second to the sixth month before stopping. According to the *Great Plan*, these were all signs of dog-related disasters, indicating the consequences of not following orders.

In the autumn of the fourth year of the Hongjia era of Emperor Cheng, fish rained down in Xindu, each less than five inches in length. By the spring of the first year of the Yongshi era, four great fish appeared in the Bohai Sea, each six zhang long and one zhang high. In the third year of the Jianping era of Emperor Ai, seven great fish emerged from Pingdu in Donglai, each eight zhang long, one zhang one chi high. All of them died. In the second year of the Xiping era of Emperor Ling, two great fish appeared in the sea off Donglai, each eight or nine zhang long and over two zhang high. *Jing Fang's Commentary on the Yi Jing* says: “When the sea frequently produces great fish, it is a sign that wicked men rise to power and virtuous men are sidelined.” In the second month of the first year of the Yongshi era of Emperor Cheng, a branch grew from a *chū* tree at the postal station in Henan, resembling a human head, with eyes, eyebrows, and beard all present, though lacking hair on the ears. By the tenth month of the third year of the Jianping era of Emperor Ai, a log fell to the ground in Suiping, Suoyang township of Runan, and a branch grew from it, taking on the form of a human figure. Its body was greenish-yellow, its face white, and its head bore a beard. It gradually grew larger, ultimately reaching a length of six inches and one part. *Jing Fang's Commentary* says: “When the virtue of the king declines and the lower people rise, trees will grow in human form.” Soon after, Wang Mang usurped the throne. In the second month of the second year of the Suhe era of Emperor Cheng, a horse in the imperial stables grew horns in front of its left ear, each with a circumference of two inches. At that time, Wang Mang was serving as Grand Marshal, and the first signs of harm to the ruler began from this moment. In the third month of the second year of the Suhe era of Emperor Cheng, in Tianping, Pingxiang, a swallow gave birth to a sparrow, which was fed until it grew large, and both flew away. *Jing Fang's Commentary* says: “When a traitorous minister is in the state, the fault is the swallow giving birth to a sparrow, and the feudal lords will be destroyed.” It also says: “When something is born not of its kind, its offspring will not inherit the world.”

In the third year of the Jianping era of Emperor Ai of Han, a male horse in Dingxiang gave birth to a three-legged foal, which followed the herd in eating and drinking. The *Wuxing Zhi* (Book of the Five Elements) interpreted this as a sign: "Horses are the martial tools of the state. Three legs are an omen of uselessness." In the third year of the Jianping era, a tree in Lingling fell to the ground, with a circumference of one zhang six chi and a length of ten zhang seven chi. The people cut its base, leaving it nine chi or more, all of which withered. After three months, the tree suddenly stood upright in its original place. The *Yi Zhuan* (Commentary on the Book of Changes) by Jing Fang said: "Abandoning the proper, indulging in excess, the omen is that the root will be cut and belong to itself. The empress has dominance, the tree falls, stands again, is cut and withered, but regrows." In the fourth month of the fourth year of the Jianping era, a woman named Tian Wuse in Fangyu, Shanyang, gave birth to a son. Two months before birth, the child cried in her womb. Upon birth, the child was not raised and buried on the road. Three days later, someone passing by heard the child's cry. The mother then dug up the child and raised him. In the summer of the fourth year of the Jianping era, the people of the capital and the counties gathered in the lanes and fields, setting up gambling tools and singing and dancing in honor of the Western Mother. Another book was transmitted saying: "The mother tells the people: 'Those who wear this book will not die. If you do not believe me, look under the door hinge; there will be white hair.'" By autumn, it ceased. In the middle of the Jianping era, a man in Yuzhang transformed into a woman, married, and had a son. Chang'an's Chen Feng said: "Yang transforming into yin, foretells death; the heir, a self-generating image." Another interpretation was: "Married as a woman, bearing a son, will repeat once, then cease." Thus, after Emperor Ai died, Emperor Ping died, and Wang Mang usurped the throne.

In the first year of Yuanshi during the reign of Emperor Ping of Han, in the second month, a woman named Zhao Chun from Shuofang Guangmu died of illness. After she was placed in a coffin, seven days later, she appeared outside the coffin. She claimed to have seen her deceased father, who said, "You are twenty-seven years old; you should not have died." The governor Tan reported this to the court, explaining, "Extreme cold becomes warmth, the lowly rise to the high. Such a marvel, a person dying and coming back to life." Later, Wang Mang seized the throne. In the first year of Yuanshi during the reign of Emperor Ping of Han, in the sixth month, a woman in Chang'an gave birth to a child with two heads, two necks facing each other, four arms all attached to the chest and facing forward, and eyes on the buttocks, about two inches long. The Commentary on the Yi by Jing Fang states, "The separation of the lonely, seeing pigs carrying mud, such a marvel, a person being born with two heads, the lower taking from each other. Good omens, also like people. If like livestock, with heads and eyes below." This is called losing the superior, the government will undergo changes. Such marvels are created to punish the loss of correctness, each representing its own kind. Two necks, the lower is not unified. Many hands, the one appointed is corrupt. Few feet, the lower cannot bear the burden, or does not bear the lower. All lower parts growing above, it is disrespectful; upper parts growing below, it is disrespectful. Born not of its kind, it is licentious; born large, the upper quickly completes; born able to speak, it is fond of emptiness. All marvels follow this pattern. If not changed, it becomes an omen of disaster.

In the first year of the Yuanhe era of Emperor Zhang of Han, a three-legged bird with a red color and horns, about the size of a chicken, was born in Gao Liu, Dai Commandery. After Emperor Huan of Han ascended the throne, a large snake appeared on the De Yang Palace. The magistrate of Luoyang, Chunyu Yi, said, "Snakes have scales, which symbolize military force; their appearance in the imperial palace indicates that a powerful minister will be involved in military matters." He then resigned and fled. By the second year of the Yuanxi era, the Grand General Liang Ji was executed, and his family was arrested and punished, displaying military power in the capital. In the seventh month of autumn in the third year of the Jianhe era of Emperor Huan of Han, meat rained down in the north, resembling sheep ribs, some as large as a hand. At that time, Empress Dowager Liang was regent, and Liang Ji held absolute power, committing arbitrary killings. He executed the Grand Commandant Li Gu and Du Qiao, and the whole country mourned them. Afterward, the Liang family was exterminated. During the Yuangjia era of Emperor Huan of Han, women in the capital made up their eyebrows in a sorrowful style, wore tearful makeup, styled their hair in a drooping manner, walked with bent waists, and laughed with clenched teeth. "Sorrowful eyebrows" were fine and curved; "tearful makeup" was lightly applied under the eyes; "drooping hair" was styled to one side; "bent waist walk" involved walking with a bent waist; "clenched-toothed laugh" resembled laughing with clenched teeth. These trends started by the wife of Grand General Liang Ji, Sun Shou. The capital followed suit, and all regions emulated this. Heaven seems to say, "Soldiers will go to capture: women will be sad, frown and cry; officials and soldiers will drag and pull, bend their backs, make their hair tilt to one side; even if they force a laugh, there will be no joy left." By the second year of the Yuanxi era, Liang Ji and his entire clan were executed.

In the fifth year of Emperor Huan's reign during the Eastern Han dynasty, a cow in Linyuan County gave birth to a chicken with two heads and four feet. Emperor Ling of Han frequently played in the West Garden, ordering the palace ladies to act as innkeepers. He dressed up as a merchant, walking to the inn and asking the ladies to serve wine and food. They enjoyed meals together, considering it a form of entertainment. This was interpreted as a sign of the emperor's impending downfall and demotion to a lowly status. Following this event, widespread chaos ensued. Ancient records mention, "There will be a period of calamity lasting three sevens." Three sevens refer to a period of 210 years, during which there will be a usurpation by an external family member. The red-haired or red-mustached figure, associated with rebellion, will have a short-lived usurpation, lasting for three sixes, or 18 years. Then, a remarkable figure, often associated with a hero or savior, will emerge to restore the imperial lineage. After another period of 210 years, there will be another calamity associated with a figure with yellow hair or beard, leading to widespread chaos once again. From the founding of the Han dynasty by Emperor Gaozu to the end of Emperor Ping's reign, spanning 210 years, Wang Mang usurped the throne, likely due to the influence of the empress dowager. Eighteen years later, rebels led by Fan Zidu rose in Shandong, with red hair or mustaches, and were known as the "Red Eyebrows."

Emperor Guangwu founded the Eastern Han Dynasty, and his successor was named Liu Xiu. By the first year of Emperor Ling's Zhongping era, Zhang Jiao rose up, establishing 36 regions with a following of hundreds of thousands. All of them wore yellow turbans, and thus they were called "Yellow Turban Rebels." To this day, Taoist clothing has become widespread, originating from this movement. The rebellion began in Ye and gathered in Zhending, where they deceived the people with the slogan: "The sky of the old era has died, and the yellow sky has risen. In the year of Jiazi, the world will be greatly blessed." The rebellion began in Ye, marking the start of the era, and gathered in Zhending. The common people knelt and bowed to one another, believing in the movement. The provinces of Jing and Yang were particularly affected. People abandoned their property, wandering the roads, and countless died. Zhang Jiao and his followers initially rose in February and were completely defeated by December. From the rise of Emperor Guangwu to the outbreak of the Yellow Turban Rebellion, less than 210 years passed, and the world fell into chaos. The Han Dynasty's legacy was extinguished, truly fulfilling the prophecy of the 210-year cycle. In the Jianning era of Emperor Ling, men's clothing favored long robes with very short hems, while women's clothing favored long skirts with very short upper sections. This symbolized a lack of balance between the yang and yin, indicating that the world was not yet at peace. Subsequently, great chaos ensued.

Emperor Ling's Jianning third year in spring, in Hebei, there was a woman who ate her husband, and in Henan, there was a man who ate his wife. Husband and wife embody the profound emotional connection of yin and yang, the two fundamental principles. Now they have turned to cannibalism, with yin and yang invading each other—this is not just a minor celestial disturbance. After Emperor Ling's death, the world fell into chaos, with rulers committing unjust killings and officials rebelling. Armies clashed, kin turned against each other, and the suffering of the people reached its peak. Therefore, unnatural phenomena appeared as precursors. I regret not living in the time of scholars like Xin You and Tu Cheng, who could provide deeper insights into these events. In the second month of Emperor Ling's Xiping era, rumors spread in Luoyang: In the eastern wall of the Tiger Banner Temple, there was a yellow-skinned person, whose appearance, eyebrows, and beard were remarkably lifelike. Thousands gathered to see it, causing the inner palace to empty and traffic to be cut off. By the first month of the Zhongping era, Zhang Jiao and his brothers rose up in rebellion in Jizhou, calling themselves "Yellow Heaven." The rebellion spread in all directions, with thirty-six factions emerging. Generals were scattered like stars, and officials and soldiers defected. The rebels were ultimately defeated due to their own exhaustion and lack of resources. In the third year of Emperor Ling's Xiping era, in a specific area, there were two large elm trees, both about four feet tall. One branch of one of the trees grew rapidly overnight, reaching over ten feet in length, with a circumference of one zhang, taking on the appearance of a foreigner, complete with head, eyes, hair, beard, and mustache. In the fifth year, on the day of Renshu in October, near the main hall, there was a locust tree, six to seven zhang in circumference, which uprooted itself and grew upside down, with roots above and branches below. Additionally, during the Zhongping era, in the northwest of Chang'an, about six to seven li away, a face with hair appeared inside an empty tree. According to the *Book of Changes*, these phenomena are signs of disorder in the natural world.

During the first year of Emperor Ling's Guanghe era, a hen chicken in the South Palace's Attendant-in-Attendance residence appeared to be transforming into a rooster, with feathers resembling those of a rooster, but its head crest had not yet changed. In the second year of Emperor Ling's Guanghe era, a woman in Luoyang gave birth to a child outside the western gate of the city: the child had two heads, different shoulders, a shared chest, and both heads facing forward. People considered it an omen of bad luck and abandoned the child. From then on, the court became disordered, with politics dominated by private interests, no distinction between public and private life, symbolized by the two-headed child. Later, Dong Zhuo killed Empress Dowager He, was accused of filial impiety, deposed the emperor, and later killed him. Since the founding of the Han dynasty, there was no greater calamity than this. In the fourth year of Emperor Ling's Guanghe era, a man nine feet tall, dressed in white, appeared at the Yellow Gate Attendant's residence in the South Palace. The Yellow Gate Attendant Xie Bu questioned him, asking, "Who are you?" The man claimed to be Liang Bexia and said that he was appointed by the heavens to be emperor. Xie Bu tried to apprehend him, but the man suddenly disappeared. In the seventh year of Emperor Ling's Guanghe era, grass grew along the roadside in the regions of Chenliu, Jiyang, Changyuan, Jiyin, Dongjun, Yuanju, and Lixu. The grass appeared to be human figures holding weapons. It also took the forms of cows, horses, dragons, snakes, birds, and beasts, with distinct colors and features, including feathers, heads, eyes, feet, and wings. These figures were not just vaguely similar to animals but were highly detailed and realistic. According to ancient sayings, this was a sign of grass demons. That same year, the Yellow Turban Rebellion broke out, and the Han dynasty began to weaken.

In the first year of the Zhongping era of Emperor Ling, on the day Ren Shen in June, a man named Liu Cang from Luoyang, residing outside the western gate of the upper city, had a wife who gave birth to a son with two heads on one body. By the Jian'an period, another woman gave birth to a son with the same condition. In the third year of the Zhongping era, during August, tens of thousands of sparrows were seen at the Mausoleum of Emperor Ling, crying out in extreme sorrow before engaging in chaotic fighting and killing each other, with their heads severed and hanging from thorny branches. By the sixth year, Emperor Ling passed away. The mausoleum symbolizes something grand and elevated; sparrows symbolize titles or ranks. It seems the heavens were warning, saying, "Those who hold titles and wealth and are esteemed will harm each other, leading to their destruction." During the Han dynasty, at grand gatherings in the capital for weddings and other celebrations, the music "Kuaili" was played, followed by "Wangge" after the guests were drunk. "Kuaili" is music associated with funerals, and "Wangge" is a song sung by mourners who hold ropes and sing in harmony. It seems the heavens were warning, saying, "The country is about to face great disaster, and all the nobles and their pleasures will perish." After Emperor Ling's death, the capital fell into ruin, with households containing multiple corpses, some being eaten by insects. Could this be the effect of "Kuaili" and "Wangge"? Towards the end of Emperor Ling's reign, there were rumors in the capital that said, "A marquis is not a marquis, a king is not a king. Thousands of chariots and tens of thousands of horses ascend Mount Beimang." By the sixth year of the Zhongping era, a marquis ascended to the highest position, while Emperor Xian, who had no title, was seized by eunuchs including Duan Gui, with all the high officials and courtiers following him, and they were allowed to return only when they reached the river.

During the early years of Emperor Xian's reign in the Chuping era, a woman surnamed Huang in Changsha died, was buried, and remained in her coffin for over a month. Her mother heard a sound coming from the coffin and opened it, only to find her daughter had come back to life. An interpretation was made: "Extreme yin becomes yang, the lowly become exalted." Later, Cao Cao rose from an ordinary official to become a powerful warlord. In the seventh year of Emperor Xian's reign in the Jian'an era, a man in Yuejun transformed into a woman. At that time, Zhou Jun reported: "Such a transformation also occurred during the reign of Emperor Ai, foreshadowing a change of dynasties." By the twenty-fifth year, Emperor Xian was enfeoffed as the King of Shanyang. In the early years of the Jian'an era, a children's rhyme in Jingzhou said: "Between the eighth and ninth years, things will begin to decline; by the thirteenth year, there will be no survivors." This referred to the fact that since the revival of the Han dynasty, Jingzhou had remained intact; when Liu Biao became its governor, the people enjoyed prosperity. By the ninth year of Jian'an, things began to decline, which was interpreted as Liu Biao's wife dying and his generals falling. By the thirteenth year, there would be no survivors, as Liu Biao was expected to die, leading to the downfall of Jingzhou. At that time, a woman in Huarong suddenly cried out: "A great death is coming." Her words were considered overly extreme, and the county authorities imprisoned her as a troublemaker. After a month, she suddenly cried out in her prison cell: "Governor Liu of Jingzhou will die today." The county immediately sent someone to verify, and indeed, Liu Biao died that day. The county then released her. She continued to sing: "Who would have thought Li Li would become a noble?" Soon after, Cao Cao conquered Jingzhou and appointed Li Li, a man from Zhuo County with the courtesy name Jianxian, as the governor of Jingzhou.

In the first month of the 25th year of the Jian'an era, Wei Wu (Cao Cao) was building the Jianshi Hall in Luoyang when he cut down a tree and blood flowed from it. He then dug up another tree, injuring its roots, and blood flowed from them as well. Wei Wu considered these signs unfavorable and soon fell ill; he died that month. This year marked the beginning of the Huangchu era, the reign of Wei Wu (Cao Pi). In the first year of the Huangchu era, there was an eagle born in a swallow's nest in the Weiyang Palace, with both its mouth and claws red. During the Qinglong era, Emperor Ming began constructing the Lingxiao Pavilion. Just as the construction started, a magpie nested on top of it. The emperor asked Gao Tanglong about this, who replied: "The *Book of Songs* says, 'The magpie has a nest, but the jay occupies it.' Now that we are building palaces, and a magpie comes to nest, this is an omen that the palace will not be completed, and the emperor will not be able to live in it."

In the early years of the Jiaqing era of King Qi of Wei, a supernatural horse emerged from the Bai Ma River, passing through the government pastures at night and calling out. The other horses all responded. The next day, its tracks were found, as large as a hu, having traveled several li before returning to the river. In the first year of the Jingchu era of Wei, a large nestling was born in the home of Li Gai in the state of Wei. It had the shape of an eagle and the beak of a swallow. Gao Tang Long said, "This is a great anomaly for the Wei dynasty. It is advisable to guard against the 'flying eagle' officials within the inner walls." Later, Emperor Xuan rose to power, executed Cao Shuang, and thus came to control the Wei dynasty. In the fifth year of the Jingyao era of Shu, a large tree in the palace courtyard broke off without cause. Qiao Zhou deeply worried about this, but had no one to discuss it with, so he wrote on a pillar, "Many and great, the time of gathering. Complete and bestow, how can there be another?" He said, "Cao means great. Many and great, the world will surely gather. Complete and bestow, how can there be another who can stand?" After Shu fell, everyone considered Qiao Zhou's words to be true. In the first month of the Taiyuan era of Sun Quan, a great wind blew, causing rivers and seas to overflow. The water level rose eight chi above the ground, uprooting two thousand trees in Gaoling, slightly moving stone steles, and causing two gates of Wu City to fly off. The following year, Sun Quan died. In the first month of the Wufeng era of Sun Liang, wild grass in Jiaozhi transformed into rice. When Sanmiao was about to perish, the five grains changed species. This was a grass anomaly. Later, Sun Liang was deposed. In the second month of the Wufeng era of Sun Liang, a large stone in Lili Mountain in Yangxian County stood up on its own. At that time, it was a sign that Sun Hao would succeed the throne after the deposed family.

During the fourth year of the Yong'an era under Emperor Sun Quan's successor, Sun Xiu, a man named Chen Jiao from Wu County died, only to come back to life after seven days, emerging from a grave in Wu County. This event was seen as a sign of good fortune for the fallen families, indicating the rise of Sun Hao. After Sun Xiu's reign, the fashion trend was for longer upper garments and shorter lower garments, with multiple layers on top and fewer on bottom. This reflected the social inequality of the time, with the upper class being more extravagant and the lower class being more frugal, symbolizing an imbalance where the upper class had excess and the lower class had scarcity. In Volume 7 of the *Search for the Supernatural*, it is recorded that during the early Western Han period, wise men predicted: "In the Wei dynasty, there will be an opening of a stone in the west, thousands of miles away, with five horses tied to it, inscribed with 'Greatly Destroy Cao.'" When the Wei dynasty first rose, in Liugou, Zhangye, a stone opened: it was first seen during the Jian'an era, formed during the Huangchu era, and its inscriptions were fully developed during the Taiping era. The stone was seven zhang in circumference, one ren in height, with a dark blue body and white markings: dragons, horses, scales, deer, phoenixes, and immortals were all clearly depicted. This event was seen as a sign of the rise of the Wei and Jin dynasties. By the third year of the Tai Shi era during the Jin dynasty, the governor of Zhangye, Jiao Sheng, reported: comparing the local map with the current stone inscription, the number of characters differed, and he carefully presented the map. The inscription had five horse figures: one had a man wearing a flat hat and holding a spear riding it. Another had a horse-like shape but was incomplete, with inscriptions including "gold," "center," "Grand Marshal," "king," "great good fortune," "correct," and "longevity." Another line of text read: "Gold should be taken."

During the early years of Emperor Wu of Jin's reign in the Tai Shi era, clothing was simple at the top and elaborate at the bottom, with people tucking their garments in at the waist. This fashion trend symbolized a weakening of authority and increasing indulgence among officials. By the end of the Yuan Kang era, women began wearing two pieces of clothing over their collars, reflecting internal social dynamics manifesting externally. Vehicles were valued for being lightweight and delicate, with frequent changes in design, often adorned with white bamboo strips, reminiscent of ancient funeral vehicles. These trends were omens of disaster for the Jin dynasty. Foreign furniture and utensils, such as the *hu* bed and *mo* tray, and foreign foods like *Qiang* stew and *Mo* roast, were favored since the Tai Shi era. Wealthy and influential people would own these foreign items, using them first in important social gatherings, which was a sign of impending invasion by non-Han peoples. In the fourth year of the Tai Kang era, in the *Huiji* region, crabs and frogs turned into mice, causing widespread destruction, particularly by eating rice. These mice had fur and flesh but no bones, could not move beyond a certain point, and after a few days, all became female. In the first month of the fifth year of the Tai Kang era, two dragons appeared in the well of the imperial armory. The imperial armory, a place where the emperor's weapons and treasures are stored, is a deep and secure place, not suitable for dragons. Seven years later, there was conflict among the princes; twenty-eight years later, two Xianbei leaders, named Long, usurped power, fulfilling the earlier omen.

During the sixth year of the Tai Kang era of Emperor Wu of Jin, a two-legged tiger was captured in Nanyang. The tiger is a yin spirit residing in the yang realm, likened to a golden beast. Nanyang is associated with fire. The metal essence (tiger) entering the fire element leads to a loss of form, which is an omen of disorder in the royal family. In the eleventh month of the seventh year of the Tai Kang era, on the day Jingchen, a four-horned creature appeared in Hejian. Heaven warns: "Horns symbolize weapons. The number four represents the four directions or regions. There will be warfare arising from the four regions." Later, the Prince of Hejian united forces from the four regions, leading to chaos. In the ninth year of the Tai Kang era, a dead cow's head spoke in the northern frontier of Youzhou. At that time, the emperor was often ill and deeply concerned about future matters, but did not entrust them with absolute fairness, which is a response to mental confusion or disorder. During the middle of the Tai Kang era, two carp fish appeared on the roof of the armory. The armory is a place for military storage. The fish have scales and armor, symbolizing military equipment. The fish are extremely yin, and the roof is associated with the sun or yang. The appearance of the fish on the roof symbolizes the yin (negative) causing military disaster to the yang (positive). During the early years of Emperor Huai of Jin, the father of the Empress Dowager, Yang Jun, was executed. Arrows flew around the imperial palace. The Empress was demoted to a commoner and died in a secluded palace. By the end of the Yuan Kang era, Empress Dowager Jia exercised power, falsely accused and killed the Crown Prince, and was later executed and demoted. Within ten years, the difficulties caused by the Empress Dowager arose again, fulfilling the prophecy. From this point, chaos and disorder began. The divination book "Yi A" by Jing Fang states: "If fish leave water and fly into the roads, warfare will ensue."

Initially, when making shoes, women's shoes had rounded tops and men's shoes had square tops. The intention behind this was to distinguish between men and women. By the time of the Tai Kang period, women's shoes also had square tops, making no difference from men's. This was seen as a sign of the jealousy of Empress Jia Nanfeng. In the Jin Dynasty, when women tied their hair, they would tightly bind the ring with a cloth, which was called "Xiezi Jui" (a type of hairstyle). This style first started in the palace and quickly spread throughout the country. By the end of the dynasty, there were incidents involving Emperor Huai and Emperor Huizong.
