During the Tai Kang period, people performed the "Jin Shi Ning" dance. In this dance, the hands were pressed down to hold cups and plates, which were then repeatedly flipped. The song accompanying the dance said, "The Jin Shi Ning dance, cups and plates are repeatedly flipped." This was a sign of danger. Cups and plates are drinking vessels, but naming the dance "Jin Shi Ning" suggests that people were living in a state of complacency, unable to think far ahead, like holding a vessel in their hands. During the Tai Kang period, people used felt to make socks and to decorate the waistbands and cuffs of trousers. People joked among themselves, saying, "China will surely be conquered by the barbarians." Felt is produced by the barbarians, and if the Chinese use it for socks, waistbands, and cuffs, the barbarians have three ways to control them, how can they avoid being defeated?

At the end of the reign of Emperor Hui of Jin, the capital cities of Luoyang and Chang'an were filled with the song "Breaking the Willow." The lyrics began with descriptions of the hardships and sufferings of war and ended with accounts of capturing and killing enemies. Afterward, Yang Jun was executed, and the empress dowager was imprisoned and died, which was a sign of the song's prophecy. In the first year of Emperor Wu of Jin's reign, a horse in Liaodong grew horns on its ears, each horn three inches long. When the emperor passed away, the royal family suffered greatly due to the wars. During the reign of Emperor Hui of Jin, women began to wear weapons as decorations. They used gold, silver, ivory, horn, and tortoiseshell to make swords, spears, halberds, and other weapons, wearing them instead of hairpins. The distinction between men and women was considered a fundamental principle of the state, so their clothing and dietary practices were different. Now, women wearing weapons as decorations was a very ominous sign, leading to the events involving Empress Dowager Jia. In the third year of the leap month of Emperor Hui of Jin's reign, the six bells in front of the palace wept, and it took five hours for them to stop. The previous year, Empress Dowager Jia had killed the empress dowager Yang in Jin'yang City, and her wickedness did not cease, so the bells wept, still mourning her. During the reign of Emperor Hui of Jin, there was a person in Luoyang and Chang'an who had both male and female characteristics and could engage in both male and female activities, but they had a strong inclination towards promiscuity. The widespread military conflicts were due to the disruption in the natural balance between male and female energies, leading to the manifestation of supernatural phenomena. In the reign of Emperor Hui of Jin, a woman in Anfeng named Zhou Shining, who was eight years old, gradually transformed into a man. By the time she was seventeen or eighteen, her temperament had fully changed. Her female body did not completely disappear, and her male body was not fully formed, and she had a wife but no children.

In the third month of the fifth year of the Yuankang era, a large snake appeared in Linzi, measuring about ten zhang in length, carrying two smaller snakes. It entered the city through the northern gate, passing through the market and disappearing into the Jing Wang Temple in Han Yang City. In the third month of the fifth year of the Yuankang era, blood flowed in Lu County for over a hundred steps in the east-west direction. Eight years later, Yun Feng rebelled in Xu Zhou, killing and injuring tens of thousands of people. In the seventh year of the Yuankang era, a thunderbolt struck and broke a stone at the southern part of the city, associated with the High Rites of Fertility. The High Rites of Fertility is a temple in the palace where people pray for children. Empress Jia was jealous and intended to kill the heirs, Huai and Min. This natural disaster was a sign of heaven's anger at Empress Jia's actions. During the Yuankang era, people began to use walking sticks with an umbrella-like top, supporting their backs and gradually adding caps or knobs to the ends. When standing still, they planted the sticks. During the reigns of Huai and Min, the royal family faced many troubles, and the central capital suffered defeat. Emperor Yuan, as a vassal prince, established virtue in the east and maintained the empire, fulfilling the prophecy of the walking stick. During the Yuankang era, wealthy and influential young men gathered, their hair loose, drinking naked, and playing with maids and concubines. Those who opposed them would damage their friendships, and those who criticized them would be ridiculed. People of the time were ashamed not to follow this trend, which was a sign of the impending invasion by barbarians from the north. Later, there was the rebellion of the two Hu tribes. In the first year of the Taian era, a large stone floated for two hundred steps and came ashore in Lake Shu in Hu County, Dan Yang. The people were amazed and exclaimed, "The stone is coming to seek something." This was followed by the rebellion led by Shi Bing, who entered Jian Ye (modern-day Nanjing).

In the first month of the Taian era, a man claimed to have entered the palace through the Longmen Gate, bowed twice facing north, and said, "I will become the Minister of the Secretariat." He was immediately apprehended and executed. The highly respected and secret areas of the imperial court, where now a lowly person had managed to enter, and the gatekeepers had not noticed, indicated that the palace would soon become empty, a strange phenomenon where the lower class surpasses the upper class. After this, the emperor moved to Chang'an, and the palace gates became empty. During the middle period of the Taian era, Zhang Cheng, the official in charge of Jiangxia, had a cow that suddenly spoke, saying, "The world is about to be in chaos; I am very tired. What are you riding me for?" Zhang Cheng and several of his followers were all terrified. They then deceived the cow, saying, "Let you return, do not speak again." The cow returned halfway, and when they arrived home, it had not yet been dismounted. The cow spoke again, "Why did you return so early?" Zhang Cheng became increasingly fearful and kept the incident secret. In Anlu County, there was a skilled fortune-teller, and Zhang Cheng consulted him. The fortune-teller said, "Great misfortune. It is not just a single family's calamity; there will be war across the land. The entire region will be destroyed!" Zhang Cheng returned home, and the cow stood on its hind legs and walked. The common people gathered to watch. That autumn, Zhang Chang's rebels rose up. They first attacked Jiangxia, deceiving and dazzling the common people with the claim that the Han dynasty's rule would be restored, there was the auspicious sign of the phoenix, and a sage was in the world. Those who joined the army all wore red headwear to signify the good fortune of the fire element. The common people were swayed, and followed the chaos as if returning home. Zhang Cheng's brothers all became generals and magistrates. Soon after, they were defeated. As a result, the entire region was destroyed, and more than half of the people died or were injured, and Zhang Cheng's family was wiped out. According to the *I Ching* (Book of Changes) by Jing Fang, "A cow can speak; its words can be used to predict good or bad fortune."

Between the years of Yuankang and Tai'an, in the region between the Yangtze and Huai rivers, there were instances of discarded shoes gathering on the roads by themselves, with as many as forty to fifty pairs at a time. People would scatter them into the forest or grass, but the next day, they found them all gathered back together as before. Some said, "A cat was seen carrying and gathering them." The world interpreted this as follows: "Shoes are the lowly clothing of people, symbolizing the labor and humiliation of the lower classes. Discarded shoes represent a state of exhaustion and poverty. Roads are the means by which the four directions are connected, and the routes by which royal commands travel. Now, the discarded shoes gathering on the roads symbolize the exhaustion and illness of the lower classes, who will gather to rebel, cutting off communication and obstructing royal commands." In the first year of Yongxing during the reign of Emperor Huai of Jin, Prince Chengdu attacked Changsha. He retreated his army to the city of Ye and deployed his troops outside the city. That night, the tips of the spears emitted fire, appearing from a distance like hanging candles. Upon closer inspection, the fire disappeared. Ultimately, he met defeat and destruction.

In the first year of the Yongjia era under Emperor Huai of Jin, a servant named Wan Xiang from Wuxian County, Wu Prefecture, gave birth to a child with a bird's head, two legs, horse hooves, one hand, no fur, a yellow tail, and a size comparable to a bowl. In the fifth year of the Yongjia era, a servant of Yan Gen, the magistrate of Fuhuan, gave birth to a dragon, a girl, and a goose. Jing Fang's commentary on the *I Ching* states: "When humans give birth to non-human creatures, it signifies widespread warfare." At the time, Emperor Huai ascended the throne after Emperor Hui, and the empire was in chaos. Soon, he was captured and killed by the Xiongnu in Pingyang. In the fifth year of the Yongjia era, a dog in the household of Zhang Lin from Jiaxing, Wu Prefecture, suddenly spoke human words, saying, "All people will starve to death." Indeed, there followed the Xiongnu invasion, leading to widespread famine across the land. In the eleventh month of the fifth year of the Yongjia era, a strange mouse appeared in Yanling. Guo Pu divined and encountered the hexagram of *Lin* with *Yi* (both danger and gain), and said, "In one of the eastern counties of this prefecture, there will be a malevolent person who seeks to seize power. He will soon die." In the first month of the sixth year of the Yongjia era, four branches of the *zhu yu* tree suddenly grew intertwined in Wuxi County, resembling a *lian li* tree. Previously, when Guo Pu divined the strange mouse in Yanling, he encountered the hexagram of *Lin* with *Yi*, and said, "Later, there will be a strange tree that grows, which will be a sign of rebellion, though it appears auspicious. If this happens, within several hundred miles east and west, there will surely be a rebellion." When this strange tree grew, it was followed by the rebellion of Xu Fu in Wuxing, who killed the governor Yuan Xiu.

During the Yongjia period, a pig gave birth to a human with two heads in the city of Shouchun, but the child did not survive. Zhou Fu took it to observe. Those who know say, "The pig is a northern livestock, a symbol of Hu and Di. Two heads mean no ruler. Born and died, not succeeding." Heaven seems to warn, "It is easy to generate profit-seeking schemes, which will lead to self-destruction." Soon, Yuan Di was defeated. During the Yongjia period, scholars and officials competed to wear freshly made single-layered clothes. Those who know found this strange and said, "This is the ancient linen cloth used by feudal lords to serve the emperor. Wearing it without reason, probably there is a response!" Afterward, Emperor Huai and Emperor Min abdicated. In the past, during Cao Cao's army, they made white hats without reason, which is a sign of mourning and misfortune. Initially, they stitched horizontally at the front to distinguish the back, calling it "Yan's hat," and passed it on. By the time of the Yongjia period, they gradually removed the stitching, naming it "No-Face Hat," and women tied their hair, with the looseness increasing more and more. The hair could not stand up on its own, falling over the forehead, and only the eyes were visible. No-Face means a word of shame. Covering the forehead means a look of shame. Its looseness increased more and more, indicating the loss of ritual and righteousness, letting loose emotions and nature, until its ultimate end, becoming a great shame. Two years later, the Yongjia Rebellion broke out, the world fell apart, people below suffered, and they could not live with shame.

During the fourth year of the Jianxing era of Emperor Min of Jin, the Western capital fell, and the Prince of Jin, who would become Emperor Yuan, gained the allegiance of all under heaven. On the twenty-second day of the tenth month of that year, the wife of a county official named Ren Qiao from Xincai County, aged twenty-five, gave birth to two girls facing each other, with their upper bodies joined from the waist up but separate below the navel. This was considered an omen of the world not yet unified. At the time, the governor of the region, Lü Hui, submitted a report stating: "According to the *Book of Auspicious Signs*: 'Different roots but the same body, called intertwined branches. Different fields but the same grain, called auspicious rice.' Even plants are considered auspicious; now two people with one heart, a divine sign from heaven. The *Book of Changes* says: 'Two people with one heart, their strength can cut through metal.' The auspicious sign appears in the eastern part of Chen, indicating the four seas being united in heart. Overjoyed, I respectfully draw a diagram and present it." At the time, those with knowledge laughed at it. The virtuous say: "It is difficult to know. Even with the talent of Zang Wenzhong, he alone worshipped the sea gull. Recorded in books, never forgotten for a thousand years. Therefore, scholars cannot avoid learning. Ancient people said: A tree without branches is called a cripple, a person without learning is called blind. When one is blinded, it is as if there is a gap. How can one not strive?" In the first year of the Jianwu era of Emperor Yuan of Jin, in June, there was a severe drought in Yangzhou; in December, there was an earthquake in Hedong. Last December, the supervisor of transportation, Chunyu Bo, was executed, and his blood rose to a height of twenty-three feet on a pillar, then fell back down four feet and five inches. At that time, Chunyu Bo was wrongfully executed, leading to three consecutive years of drought. When punishments are arbitrarily imposed and the yin forces are not aligned, the yang forces dominate. Punishment is also a response to wrongful death.

In the seventh month of the first year of the Jianwu era of Emperor Yuan of Jin, in the eastern gate of Jinling, a cow gave birth to a calf with two heads on one body. Jing Fang's *Yijing* commentary states: "When a cow gives birth to a calf with two heads and one body, it is an omen of the world being divided." In the first year of the Taixing era of Emperor Yuan of Jin, in April, an earthquake struck Xiping, and water gushed out. In December, earthquakes struck Luling, Yuzhang, Wuchang, and Xiling, with water gushing out and mountains collapsing. These events were omens of Wang Dun's rebellion. In the first year of the Taixing era, in March, the governor of Wuchang, Wang Liang, had a cow give birth to a calf with two heads, eight legs, and two tails, all in one body. The calf could not be born naturally and was pulled out by ten people using ropes. The calf died, but the mother survived. Three years later, in the imperial gardens, a cow gave birth to a calf with one leg and three tails, which died immediately after birth. In the second year of the Taixing era, a horse born to a local official in Danyang, Puyang Yan, had two heads, separated at the neck, and died shortly after birth. These events were signs of political instability and the rise of powerful private individuals, foreshadowing Wang Dun's rebellion. At the beginning of the Taixing era, a woman was born with her genitalia in her abdomen, below the navel, who came from the central region to the Jiangnan area and was sexually active but did not bear children. Another woman was born with her genitalia on her head, living in Yangzhou, and was also sexually active. Jing Fang's *Yijing* commentary states: "When a person is born with genitalia on their head, it is an omen of great chaos. If it is in the abdomen, it is an omen of conflict. If it is on the back, it is an omen of the end of the dynasty."

During the Taixing period, Wang Dun was stationed in Wuchang. A disaster struck Wuchang, and a fire broke out. People were mobilized to put out the fire, but while they were fighting the fire here, it spread there. Fires broke out in dozens of places across the east, west, south, and north, and continued for several days without ceasing. This is what the ancient saying refers to as "a disaster that arises without reason, which cannot be stopped even by mobilizing troops." This disaster symbolizes a minister acting like a ruler, indicating a loss of proper order. At that time, Wang Dun was disrespectful to the emperor and harbored ambitions to usurp power, hence the disaster. During the Taixing period, soldiers wore red bags tied around their hairbands. A knowledgeable person said, "The hairband is at the head, representing the 'Heavenly' principle, which symbolizes the ruler. The bag represents the 'Earthly' principle, which symbolizes the minister. Now, by tying a red bag around the hairband, it symbolizes the minister encroaching on the ruler's authority. Those who wear clothes have their upper belts only reaching the armpit; those who wear hats tie the belt around their necks, with the lower part pressing against the upper part, leaving no room for the upper part. Those who wear trousers have them straight, without a waistband or hem, indicating a symbol of the lower part being too large." Soon after, Wang Dun plotted rebellion and attacked the capital twice. In the fourth year of Taixing, while Wang Dun was in Wuchang, flowers grew on the ceremonial staffs in the bell pavilion, resembling lotus flowers. After a few days, the flowers withered and fell. Someone said, "The *Yi Jing* says, 'A withered elm tree producing flowers, how can it last long?' Now, flowers are growing on a withered tree, and this is happening in the bell pavilion. It symbolizes the temporary nature of wealth and glory, which are like the fleeting bloom of these flowers." Later, Wang Dun was executed for his rebellion, and his body was publicly displayed as a warning.

The traditional feather fan handle was carved to resemble the shape of a bird's bone, with ten feathers arranged to symbolize completeness. Initially, during Wang Dun's southern campaign, the design was changed to a longer handle, making it easier to hold. However, the number of feathers was reduced to eight. Those who understood criticized this change, saying: "A feather fan is named after a wing. By creating a long handle, one is controlling the wings. By reducing the number of feathers from ten to eight, one is taking something that is incomplete and making it complete. This is likely Wang Dun's attempt to seize power and control the court, and he is using someone without virtue to seize power improperly." During the early years of Emperor Ming of Jin's reign, a large snake appeared in Wuchang, living in an old shrine and emerging to eat from people. Jing Fang's commentary on the *Yijing* (Book of Changes) states: "The appearance of a snake in a town portends war and national disaster within three years." Soon after, Wang Dun rebelled. In the *Search for the Supernatural*, Volume Eight, it is recorded: Shun farmed at Lishan and found the "Jade Calendar" at the edge of a river. Shun understood that he was destined to rule and practiced the Dao diligently. Shun had a dragon-like face, a large mouth, and hands holding the character "褒." Song Jun commented: "Holding the character '褒' means holding the character '褒' in one's hands, symbolizing that through hard work and discipline, one can achieve great fortune." After King Tang of Shang defeated the Xia dynasty, a seven-year drought occurred, and the Luo River dried up. Tang offered himself as a sacrifice in the mulberry forest, cutting off his claws and hair, and praying for blessings from the Supreme Deity. Immediately, heavy rain came, spreading across the land.

Lü Wang fished on the western bank of the Wei River. King Wen of Zhou went out hunting and divined, saying, "Today, in the hunt, I will catch a beast that is neither a dragon, nor a chimeric creature, nor a bear, nor a wild boar. I will surely find a teacher to a king." Indeed, he found Jiang Ziya on the western bank of the Wei River. They conversed, and King Wen was greatly pleased, so he returned together in the same carriage. King Wu of Zhou attacked King Zhou of Shang, reaching the riverbank, where it rained heavily. There was a sudden thunderstorm, and darkness fell. Waves rose in the river, and the people were very frightened. King Wu said, "If I am in the world, who dares to challenge me?" The waves immediately calmed. In the fourteenth year of Duke Ai of Lu, Confucius had a dream at night between three locust trees in Feng and Pei. He saw a red mist rise and called Yan Hui and Zi Xia to go and observe it. They drove to the northwest of Chu, to Fan Street, where they saw a child beating a fish, injuring its left front paw, and covering it with firewood. Confucius said, "Child, come here! What is your surname?" The child replied, "My surname is Chi Song, my given name is Shi Qiao, and my courtesy name is Shou Ji." Confucius said, "Have you seen anything?" The child replied, "I saw a creature that looked like a deer, with a sheep's head, horns, and a fleshy tip on the horns. It was running westward." Confucius said, "The world already has a ruler. It will be Chi Liu. Chen and Xiang will be his auxiliaries. Five stars will enter the well, following the age star." The child removed the firewood and showed Confucius a fish. Confucius hurried to it, and the fish covered its ears, spitting out three scrolls of writing, each three inches wide and eight inches long, with twenty-four characters on each scroll. The scrolls said that Chi Liu would rise when the Zhou dynasty fell, with red vapor arising, fire shining, the black hill commanding, and the emperor of the metal element (Jin dynasty) coming to power.

Confucius edited the *Spring and Autumn Annals* and compiled the *Classic of Filial Piety*. Upon completion, he fasted and bowed towards the North Star, informing the heavens of his work. A dense fog then rose from the ground, and a white rainbow descended from the sky, transforming into a yellow jade, three chi long, with inscribed text. Confucius knelt to receive and read it, which said: "A treasure text appears, held by Liu Ji. The character '卯' (卯), metal, and knife are in the north of the constellation Zhen. The one with the name 'Huo Zi' will be revered by all under heaven." During the reign of Duke Mu of Qin, a man from Chen Cang was digging and found an object that resembled but was not a sheep or a pig. He brought it to Duke Mu of Qin. On the way, he encountered two children who said, "This is called 'Aow,' an earth spirit that resides in the ground and feeds on the brains of the dead. If you wish to kill it, use a pine branch to pierce its head." The earth spirit said, "Those two children are called 'Chenbao.' Whoever captures the male will become king, and whoever captures the female will become a hegemon." The man from Chen Cang abandoned the earth spirit and chased the two children, who transformed into pheasants and flew into the forest. He then reported the incident to Duke Mu of Qin, who organized a large hunting party and indeed captured the female. It then transformed into a stone, which was placed between the Qian and Wei rivers. During the reign of Duke Wen of Qin, a shrine was built for Chenbao. The male flew to Nanyang, where the current Nanyang Chixian county is located. The Qin dynasty wanted to mark this place as a sign of their legitimacy, so they named the county after it. Every time the Chen Cang shrine was worshipped, a red light over ten zhang long appeared, coming from Chixian county and entering the Chen Cang shrine, accompanied by a deep, resonant sound like that of a rooster. Later, Emperor Guangwu of Han rose to power in Nanyang.

The Song official Xing Shi Zichen was knowledgeable about the ways of heaven. In the thirty-seventh year of King Jing of Zhou, King Jing asked, "What signs does the heavens portend?" Xing Shi Zichen replied, "Fifty years from now, on the day Dinghai in May, I will die. Five years after my death, on the day Dingmao in May, the state of Wu will fall. Five years after Wu's fall, you will pass away. Four hundred years after your passing, the state of Zhu will rule the world." Soon, all these predictions came true. The state of Zhu ruling the world refers to the rise of the state of Wei. Both Zhu and Wei belong to the Cao clan, descendants of Zhu. However, the years mentioned are incorrect. Is it because Xing Shi made a mistake in his calculations? Or is it because the passage of time has led to errors in the records?

In the early state of Wu, trust was not firm, and the commanders at the frontier garrisons were all made to hold their wives and children as hostages, known as "Guaranteed Children." Young men who associated and played together numbered in the dozens. During the third month of Yong’an Year Three in the reign of Sun Liang, there appeared an extraordinary child, about four feet tall, around six or seven years old, dressed in green clothing, who suddenly came to join the group of children in play. None of the children recognized him, and they all asked, "Who are you, little boy, and why have you come today?" He replied, "I saw that you were all playing and having fun, so I came!" Upon closer inspection, his eyes emitted a radiant light, shining outward. The children, frightened by this, asked him again why he was there. The child then answered, "Are you afraid of me? I am not a human; I am the planet Mars, and I have something to tell you. The Three Excellencies will fall under the control of the Sima family." The children were greatly alarmed, and some ran to tell the adults. The adults hurried to see what was happening. The child said, "Shall I leave you?" He then leaped upward, transforming into nothingness. Looking up, it seemed as if he was dragging a piece of white silk up to heaven. Those who arrived later still saw him, floating higher and higher, until he disappeared after a short while. At that time, the political situation in Wu was severe, and no one dared to speak of what had happened. Four years later, Shu Han fell; six years after that, Cao Wei was overthrown; and twenty-one years later, Eastern Wu was conquered: all of which fulfilled the prophecy of the Sima family's rise to power. Ma Wu, the Water Conservancy Commissioner, recommended Dai Yang as the Water Conservancy Inspector. Dai Yang requested urgent leave to return to his hometown, intending to go to Luoyang. On his way, he dreamed of a divine being who told him, "Luoyang will fall, and people will all flee south. In five years, there will be a ruler in Yangzhou." Dai Yang believed this prophecy and did not go to Luoyang. As it turned out, all of his dreams came true.

**The Search for the Divine, Volume 9** At the beginning of the Eastern Han dynasty's revival, there was a man named Ying Shu in Ru'an. He had four sons, and they all witnessed divine light illuminating the altar. Ying Shu saw the light and consulted a diviner. The diviner said, "This is a heavenly omen. Will your descendants prosper?" Upon further investigation, they found gold. From then on, Ying Shu's descendants pursued official careers and acquired great reputations. By the time of Ying Chang, the family had maintained prominence for seven generations. General of the Chariots Feng Jun, from Ba Commandery, whose courtesy name was Hongqing, initially served as an Imperial Censor. One day, he opened a chest containing his seals and found two red snakes, each about two feet long, moving north and south. He was greatly alarmed. Xu Ji-shan's descendant Sun Xian, whose courtesy name was Ningfang, possessed secret knowledge passed down from his ancestors. Feng Jun asked him to divinate. Sun Xian said, "This is a good omen. In three years, you will become a frontier general, and your official position will have the character 'east' in its name." Five years later, Feng Jun followed the Grand General on a southern campaign. Soon after, he was appointed as an Imperial Censor, Governor of Liaodong, and General of the Southern Campaign. Zhang Hao of Changshan served as the Administrator of Liangzhou. After a fresh rain, a bird resembling a mountain magpie flew into the market and suddenly fell to the ground. People gathered around it, and it transformed into a round stone. Zhang Hao struck the stone with a hammer and found a golden seal inscribed with the words "Seal of the Lord of Loyalty and Filial Piety." Zhang Hao reported this to the emperor, and the seal was kept in the imperial treasury. Later, the Imperial Censor Fan Hengyi of Ru'an suggested, "In the time of Yao and Shun, there was an ancient official position called 'Lord of Loyalty and Filial Piety.' Since this seal has descended from heaven, it is appropriate to reinstate this position." Zhang Hao eventually reached the rank of Grand Commandant.

In Jingzhao (modern-day Xi'an), there was a family named Zhang who lived alone in a room. One day, a magpie flew in from outside and landed on their bed. The Zhang family member wished aloud, "If this magpie brings me misfortune, let it fly up to the ceiling; if it brings me good fortune, let it enter my embrace." The magpie then flew into the person's arms. When they reached out to touch it, they did not find the magpie, but instead found a golden hook. They treasured it greatly. From then on, the Zhang family's descendants gradually became wealthy, and their wealth increased ten thousandfold. A merchant from Sichuan came to Chang'an, heard about the story, and bribed the maid, who stole the hook and gave it to the merchant. The Zhang family, having lost the hook, gradually declined. Meanwhile, the merchant from Sichuan also frequently encountered poverty and misfortune, not benefiting himself. Someone told him, "It is fate. It cannot be forced." Thus, he returned the hook to the Zhang family, and the Zhang family revived. Hence, in the western part of the pass, the Zhang family is known as the 'Hook-Transmitting Zhang Family.' In the third month of the third year of Emperor Wu's reign during the Han dynasty, it rained heavily. He Bigan was at home, at noon, when he dreamed of noble visitors filling his courtyard with carriages and horses. Upon waking, he told his wife about the dream. Before he finished speaking, an elderly woman, around eighty years old with white hair, appeared at the door, asking to take shelter from the rain. Despite the heavy rain, her clothes did not get wet. When the rain stopped, he sent her to the door and said to her, "You have done good deeds secretly. Today, heaven has given you a scroll, to expand your descendants." She then took out a scroll from her chest, which was in the form of a bamboo slip, nine inches long, containing a total of nine hundred and ninety items, and gave it to He Bigan, saying, "Those who wear seals and sashes among your descendants should be counted in this way."

Hui Shu, courtesy name Yangyuan, was from Fan, Rencheng. He lost his parents at a young age and once visited Yewang. The host's wife gave birth at night, and soon after, they heard the sound of carriages and horses. They asked, "Is it a boy or a girl?" The response was, "A boy." They recorded it. "At fifteen, he will die in battle." They asked again, "Who is the sleeper?" The response was, "Duke Wei Shu." Fifteen years later, he visited the host and asked about the child who had been born. The response was, "He died while pruning mulberry trees, cut by an axe." Hui Shu knew he would become a high-ranking official. Jia Yi served as the Grand Tutor to the Prince of Changsha. On the day of Gengzi in the fourth month, a peng bird flew into his room and settled by the side of his seat. After a long while, it left. Jia Yi consulted a book and interpreted it, saying, "A wild bird entering the room means the master will leave." Jia Yi was troubled by this and thus wrote the "Feng Bird Ode," equating life and death and treating misfortune and fortune equally, dedicating his life to set his mind. Wang Mang acted as regent, and Zhai Yi, the governor of Dongjun, knew he would usurp the Han dynasty and planned to raise righteous troops. His brother Xuan taught many students, filling the room. There were dozens of geese and cranes in the courtyard. A dog came from outside, bit them all, and they all died. They rushed to save them, but all had their heads cut off. The dog ran out of the door, and they searched for it, but could not find it. Xuan deeply resented this. After several days, Wang Mang destroyed his three clans.

The Grand Tutor of Wei, Sima Yi, pacified Gong Sun Yuan and beheaded him and his son. Previously, strange occurrences had repeatedly appeared in the household of Gong Sun Yuan: a dog wore a cap and headpiece, donned a red robe, and climbed onto the roof. Suddenly, a child appeared and died boiled in a steaming pot. In the northern market of Xiangping, a piece of flesh grew, measuring several feet in circumference, with a head, eyes, mouth, and beak, but no hands or feet, and it moved. A diviner said, “It has form but is incomplete, a body but no sound—this kingdom will perish.” In Wu, Zhuge Ke led a campaign against Huainan and returned. On the night of the court assembly, his spirit was disturbed, and he remained awake all night. After dressing carefully, he hurried out, and a dog bit and pulled at his clothes. Zhuge said, “Does the dog not want me to go?” He went out, then returned to sit, and after a while, rose again, with the dog once more pulling at his clothes. Zhuge ordered his attendants to drive the dog away. When he entered, he was indeed killed. His wife, still in the room, said to the maid, “Why do you smell blood?” The maid replied, “I do not.” After a while, the scent grew stronger. Zhuge’s wife then asked the maid, “Why is your gaze so unusual?” The maid suddenly jumped up, her head hitting the beam, and with arms raised and teeth clenched, she said, “Zhuge Gong was killed by Sun Jun.” At this, everyone in the house realized that Zhuge was dead. Soon after, soldiers arrived. In Wu, the garrison commander Deng Xi killed a pig in a ritual offering to a deity. After finishing the ceremony and hanging the pig, he suddenly saw a human head approaching to eat the meat. Deng drew his bow and shot it, and it made a chattering sound, circling the house for three days. Later, someone reported that Deng had plotted rebellion, and his entire family was executed.

Jia Chong was attacking Wu when he was stationed at Xiangcheng. Suddenly, his troops lost track of where he was. One day, Zhou Qin, the commandant under Jia Chong, was napping during the day and had a dream in which over a hundred people led Jia Chong into a path. Zhou Qin woke up in a panic, heard that Jia Chong was missing, and went out to search for him. Suddenly, he saw the path he had dreamed about and followed it. He found Jia Chong walking to a government office, surrounded by many guards. The official was sitting facing south, looking very stern. He said to Jia Chong, "Those who will disrupt my family affairs are certainly you and Xun Xu. You have misled my son and disrupted my grandson. You secretly made Ren Kai demote you but you did not leave, and made Yu Chun curse you but you did not change. Now that the enemy from Wu will be pacified, you just proposed to execute Zhang Hua. Your stupidity and stubbornness are all like this. If you do not repent and be cautious, you will be punished day and night." Jia Chong knelt down and bled from his forehead. The official said, "You have managed to prolong your life and maintain your position due to your service in the government office. Eventually, your heir will die between Zhong and Yu, the crown prince will die from poisoned wine, and your young son will be trapped under dead wood. Xun Xu should also be the same. His ancestors' virtue is slightly thick, so he is behind you. After several generations, the heir to the throne will also decline." After speaking, the official ordered him to leave. Jia Chong suddenly managed to return to the camp, but his appearance was worn out, and his mental state was confused. It took several days for him to recover. Later, Mi died at the place called Zhong, Jia Hou died from poisoned wine, and Jia Wu died from being beaten with a stick. All these events happened exactly as the official had said.

Yu Liang, courtesy name Wenkang, from Yanling, governed Jingzhou. One day, while using the restroom, he suddenly saw something in the toilet that resembled the "Fangxiang," a mythical creature with both eyes completely red and a radiant glow around its body. The creature gradually emerged from the ground. Yu Liang raised his arm and struck it with his fist, and there was a sound upon impact. The creature retracted into the ground, and Yu Liang fell ill as a result. The diviner Dai Yang said, "Formerly, when Su Jun served you, he prayed for blessings at the White Stone Shrine and promised to offer a cow in return. This has never been resolved. Thus, he was being tested by this ghost, and there was no way to save him." The following year, Yu Liang indeed died. Dongyang Liu Cong, courtesy name Dao Hong, lived in Lusu. Every night, there was several liters of blood in his courtyard, without knowing its origin. This happened three or four times. Later, Liu Cong was appointed as a Zhifeng General and sent north to campaign. As he was about to leave, his cooked food turned into insects, and even his family's steamed and fried food turned into insects. The more intense the fire, the more robust the insects. Liu Cong then went north to campaign, but his army was defeated at Tanqiu and he was killed by Xu Kan.

**Search for Gods, Volume Ten** Empress Deng of the Han Dynasty, during her reign as the Consort of Emperor He, once dreamed of climbing a ladder to touch the sky. Her body felt light, clear, and smooth, as if she were touching stalactites. She then drank something while looking up. She consulted a dream interpreter, who said, "Emperor Yao dreamed of climbing to the heavens, and Emperor Tang dreamed of reaching the sky. These were signs of their future greatness. The good fortune is indescribable." Wu, the wife of Sun Jian, dreamed that the moon entered her womb while she was pregnant. Later, she gave birth to Sun Ce. When she was pregnant with Sun Quan, she again dreamed that the sun entered her womb. She told Sun Jian, "When I was pregnant with Ce, I dreamed that the moon entered my womb; now I have dreamed of the sun. What does this mean?" Sun Jian replied, "The sun and moon represent the essence of yin and yang, symbols of the utmost nobility. Our descendants will surely prosper." Cai Mao, a scholar and official of the Han Dynasty, whose courtesy name was Zilǐ, was from Huai, He Nei. While serving in Guanghan, he dreamed of sitting on a grand hall, with three ears of grain at the highest point. Cai Mao picked one of the ears, but then lost it. He asked his subordinate Guo He for an interpretation. Guo He said, "The grand hall represents the image of an official residence. At the highest point, there is grain, which symbolizes the highest salary for a minister. Taking the middle ear of grain is a symbol of the middle platform. In the character, '禾' (grain) and '失' (lost) make '秩' (rank). Although it seems lost, it is actually a salary. There is a vacancy in the central position of the state, you should fill it." Within a month, Cai Mao was summoned to serve in a high position.

Zhou Lanze, a man who was poor but fond of the Way, lost his wife and worked at night, growing tired and resting to sleep. In his dream, the Heavenly Official passed by and pitied him, ordering someone outside to give him something. The Official of Life and Death checked the records and said, "This person's destiny is poverty, limited to this. Only Zhang Cheli should be given a record of ten thousand. Zhang Cheli has not been born, please lend it to him." The Heavenly Official said, "Good." Upon waking, Zhou shared his dream. From then on, the couple united their efforts and worked all day and night to improve their livelihood, achieving everything they set out to do, accumulating wealth to the tune of ten thousand. Previously, there was an old woman named Zhang who had once gone to Zhou's house as a hired worker. They had a wild union, and when the moon was full, she was pregnant. She was sent outside, where she stopped under the carriage house and gave birth to a son. The master went to see and pitied her loneliness and poverty, making porridge to feed her. He asked, "What should you name your child?" The woman said, "Now that he was born under the carriage house, I dreamed that the Heavenly One told me to name him Zhang Cheli." Zhou then realized, "I once dreamed of exchanging money with heaven, and someone told me to lend me Zhang Cheli's money. It must be this child. Wealth should return to him." From then on, his residence declined, and Zhang Cheli grew up to be wealthier than the Zhou family. In the summer of Yang, Lu Fen, styled Shiji, dreamed of entering an ant nest, where he saw a three-room hall with a very precarious structure. He inscribed its name as "Shen Yu Tang." Wu Xuan Cao Ling Shi Liu Zhuo, who was seriously ill, dreamed of a person who gave him a white Yue single robe and said, "You wear the robe. If it gets dirty, burn it, and it will be clean." Liu Zhuo woke up, and indeed there was a robe beside him. When it got dirty, he washed it with fire.

Liu Ya, promoted to the position of *Shuzuo* in the southern region, had a dream where a green spiny lizard fell into his abdomen, after which he suffered from severe abdominal pain. During the Later Han period, Zhang Huan served as the governor of Wuwei. His wife dreamed that the emperor gave her a seal and sash, and she climbed a tower to sing. Upon waking, she told Zhang Huan about the dream. Zhang Huan had it interpreted, and the interpreter said, "Your wife will give birth to a son who will later die in this tower while serving as the governor of Wuwei." Indeed, during the Jian’an period, Zhang Huan’s son, Liu Yong, became the governor of Wuwei and killed the governor (刺史). When the army of Shangdang besieged him, Liu Yong, feeling ashamed of being captured, climbed the tower and committed suicide by fire. Emperor Ling of Han dreamed of Emperor Yuan and became angry, saying, "What crimes did Empress Song commit that you allowed wicked forces to execute her? Prince Ku of Bohai, who had already abdicated, was also executed. Now, the spirits of Empress Song and Prince Ku are petitioning heaven, and the gods are furious, making their punishment inevitable." The dream was clear and insightful. After waking, Emperor Ling was terrified and soon died. During the Wu period in Jiaxing, Xu Bishi fell ill and invited the Taoist priest Lu Shi to perform a ritual. Lu Shi had disciples named Dai Ben and Wang Si, who lived in Haisan. Xu Bishi invited them to assist Lu Shi. During the day, Xu Bishi had a dream where he saw three saddled horses at the North Star gate. The dream said, "Tomorrow, one horse will be for Lu Shi, one for Dai Ben, and one for Wang Si." Lu Shi woke up and told Dai Ben and Wang Si, "You are facing death. Return home quickly and say goodbye to your families." They left without completing their task. Xu Bishi was surprised and kept them. They said, "We are afraid we won’t see our families again." A day later, all three died simultaneously.

The Prefect of Kuaiji, Xie Feng, was on good terms with the Governor of Yongjia, Guo Boyou. One night, Xie had a dream in which he saw Guo playing a game of chupu with someone by the Yangtze River, and because they lost the bet, they were punished by a water deity and drowned. Xie then arranged the funeral affairs for Guo. Upon waking, he immediately went to Guo's residence and played chess with him. After a long while, Xie said, "Do you know why I came?" and told Guo about his dream. Guo, upon hearing this, sighed and said, "Last night, I also had a dream where I was arguing over money with someone, just as you described. How could it be so precise?" Shortly after, Guo went to use the restroom and suddenly collapsed, dying on the spot. Xie then prepared for the funeral. In Jiaxing, there was a man named Xu Tai who lost his parents at a young age. His uncle, Wei, took care of him more than his own parents. When Wei fell ill, Xu cared for him diligently. One night during the third watch, Xu had a dream in which two men arrived on a boat carrying a chest, which they placed at the foot of his bed. They opened the chest and took out a ledger, showing it to Xu and saying, "Your uncle is destined to die." Xu immediately knelt in the dream and pleaded with them. After a long time, the two men asked, "Is there anyone in your county with the same name as your uncle?" Xu thought for a moment and replied, "There is a man named Zhang Wei, but he does not share the same surname." The two men said, "We can also compel him. Considering your filial piety towards your uncle, we will save him for you." With that, they disappeared. Xu woke up, and his uncle's illness soon improved.

The Search for Spirits, Volume XI In the state of Chu, there was a man named Xiong Quchi who, while walking at night, saw a sleeping stone and mistook it for a crouching tiger. He drew his bow and shot at it. The arrow penetrated the stone and broke the bowstring. Looking down, he realized it was a stone. He then shot it again, and the arrow shattered, leaving no trace. In the Han dynasty, there was another archer named Li Guang, who served as the governor of Youbeiping. He once shot at a tiger and struck a stone, just as Xiong Quchi had done. Liu Xiang commented, "When sincerity reaches its utmost, even metal and stone will open up; how much more so, then, for people! If one sings and no one responds, or if one moves and no one follows, there must be something incomplete within. One who can govern the world without leaving their seat must seek it within themselves." The King of Chu was walking in the garden, and a white monkey was there. The king ordered a skilled archer to shoot at it. The archer shot several arrows, and the monkey caught the arrows and laughed. The king then summoned You Ji, who drew his bow, and the monkey immediately hugged the tree and cried. In the time of the Warring States, Geng Lei told King Wei of Wei, "I can shoot and make the bird fall without actually hitting it." King Wei asked, "Then can archery reach this level?" Geng Lei replied, "Yes." Shortly after, they heard a goose flying from the east, and Geng Lei shot, and the bird fell.

King Jing of Qi was crossing the Yangtze and Xiang rivers when a giant turtle bit the left horse and drowned it. Everyone was startled; at this point, Gu Yezhi drew his sword and followed the turtle, swerving five li and backtracking three li, until he reached the foot of the Stone Pillar. He killed the turtle, which turned out to be the giant turtle, holding its head in his left hand and pulling the left horse out with his right hand. He leaped like a swallow and jumped like a swan, emerging from the water, and cried out to the sky, causing the water to flow backward three hundred steps. Onlookers all thought it was the River God. The legendary swordsmiths Gan Jiang and Mo Ye were making swords for the king of Chu, taking three years to complete them. The king became angry and wanted to kill them. They had a pair of swords, one male and one female. When Mo Ye's wife was pregnant, her husband told her, "I made the sword for the king, and it took three years to complete. The king is angry, and if I go, he will definitely kill me. If you give birth to a son, tell him when he grows up: 'Go out the door, look toward the south mountain, where a pine tree grows on a rock, and the sword is on its back.'" So she took the female sword to see the king. The king became furious and had it examined, and it was found that there were two swords, one male and one female, with the female present and the male absent. The king became even angrier and killed her. Mo Ye's son, named Chi, grew up and asked his mother, "Where is my father?" She replied, "Your father made the sword for the king, and it took three years to complete. The king was angry and killed him. Before he left, he entrusted me with a message: 'Tell your son: Go out the door, go to the south mountain, where a pine tree grows on a rock, and the sword is on its back.'" So the boy went out the door and looked south, but saw no mountain, only a pine tree growing on a stone base at the foot of a pillar in the hall. He immediately broke open the back of the pillar with an axe and found the sword. Day and night, he thought about avenging the king of Chu. The king dreamed of a boy with a broad forehead, saying he wanted to take revenge.

The king offered a thousand gold coins for the head of the son of Gan Jiang and Mo Ye. The son heard this and fled into the mountains, singing as he walked. A guest met him and said, "You are young. Why are you crying so bitterly?" The son replied, "I am the son of Gan Jiang and Mo Ye. The king of Chu killed my father, and I wish to avenge him." The guest said, "I have heard that the king offered a thousand gold coins for your head. Bring your head and the sword to me, and I will avenge your father for you." The son said, "That would be wonderful." He immediately cut his own throat, held his head and the sword with both hands, and presented them to the guest, then stood rigidly. The guest said, "I will not fail you." Then the body fell down. The guest took the head to see the king, who was very pleased. The guest said, "This is the head of a brave warrior. It should be cooked in a pot of boiling water." The king followed his advice. The head was boiled for three days and three nights but did not rot. The sword leaped out of the boiling water, stumbled with its eyes, and showed great anger. The guest said, "This sword will not rot. I hope you will come and see it yourself, and it will definitely rot." The king immediately came to see it. The guest held the sword to the king's head, and the king's head fell into the boiling water. The guest also held the sword to his own head, and his head also fell into the boiling water. All three heads rotted simultaneously, and they could not be distinguished. They were then buried together. This is why it is commonly known as the Three Kings' Tomb. Now it is located in the territory of Yichun County, Northern Yu Prefecture.

During the reign of Emperor Wu, Jia Yong from Cangwu served as the governor of Yuzhang. Possessed of divine powers, he crossed the border to attack rebels and was killed by them, losing his head. He mounted his horse and returned to camp, where all rushed to see him. From his chest, he spoke: "The battle was not favorable, and I was injured by the rebels. Do you think it is better to have a head or not to have one?" The officials wept and said, "It is better to have a head." Jia Yong replied, "Not so. Even without a head, it is still good." After he finished speaking, he died. In another incident, Shi Liang, the governor of Bohai, had a sister who was promised in marriage but the engagement did not materialize. Enraged, Liang killed her, cut off her head, and took it home, throwing it into the stove. He said, "I will have it cremated by fire." The head spoke, "Sir, I followed you, but who could have foreseen this?" Later, Liang dreamed that the head said, "Return your belongings to you." Upon waking, he found the perfumed hairpin and golden hairpin that she had given him before. During the reign of King Ling of Zhou, Chang Hong was executed, and the people of Shu collected his blood. After three years, it transformed into a blue gemstone. Emperor Wu of Han embarked on an eastern tour and had not yet left the Hangu Pass when he encountered a creature blocking the road. It was several zhang long, resembling a cow with blue eyes and shining pupils, with four feet and part of its body buried in the ground, moving but not moving away. The officials were terrified. Dongfang Shuo then requested to pour wine over it. After pouring dozens of dou of wine, the creature disappeared. The emperor asked him why it happened. Dongfang Shuo replied, "This is called a manifestation of sorrow and anger. It must be a place where people were buried in the Qin prison, or a gathering place for criminals and convicts. Wine forgets sorrow, hence it can dispel it." The emperor exclaimed, "Ah! Scholars with vast knowledge can understand such things!"

During the Eastern Han Dynasty, Liang Fu, also known as Hanru, was a native of Xindu, Guanghan. As a young man, he served as an assistant official, barely making ends meet. He later became an attendant official, handling both major and minor affairs with thoroughness, earning the respect of the local officials. During a severe summer drought, the magistrate exposed himself in the courtyard in self-punishment, but no rain came. Liang Fu, as an assistant clerk, went out to pray at the mountains and rivers, vowing, "I am the pillar of the county. I have failed to offer advice, accept loyalty, recommend the worthy, and remove the wicked, leading to the separation of heaven and earth, the withering of all things, and the suffering of the people, who have nowhere to appeal. The fault lies entirely with me. Now, the magistrate has self-examined and exposed himself in the courtyard, asking me to confess my sins and pray for the people. Despite my sincere and earnest prayers, there has been no response. I dare to swear: if there is no rain by noon, I will sacrifice myself to atone for my sins." He then piled up firewood and prepared to burn himself. By noon, the mountain atmosphere turned dark, thunder rumbled, and heavy rain began to fall, drenching the entire county. The world praised him for his utmost sincerity. He was a native of Wu County, fond of the Dao and arts, and lived in seclusion. During a severe drought, the people were suffering, and the magistrate, Qian Hong, sent a household official to present a letter and seals, asking him to take office as the governor of Wuxi. He refused. Later, he sighed and said, "There is a disaster in the region, how can I remain devoted to the Dao?" He then traveled to the county, staying in a star-lit house, where the locusts and grasshoppers died, and he immediately retreated. Later, he was nominated as a scholar of integrity and righteousness but refused all nominations, dying at home.

During the Eastern Han Dynasty, Xu Xu, courtesy name Jingqing, was from Youquan in Wu. He began his career as a prison official and administered justice with fairness and thoroughness. When he served as the governor of Xiaohuang, a major locust infestation occurred in the subordinate counties, leaving the fields devoid of living grass. However, when the locusts passed through Xiaohuang, they flew away and did not settle. The governor, on inspection, blamed Xu for failing to manage the situation. Xu resigned his office, and the locusts responded to his resignation by appearing. The governor apologized and invited him back to his official residence, and the locusts immediately flew away. Wang Ye, courtesy name Zi Xiang, served as the governor of Jingzhou during the reign of Emperor He of Han. Before each inspection, he took a ritual bath and fasted, praying to heaven and earth to enlighten and assist his foolish heart and prevent the oppression of the people. Under his governance, benevolent policies were widely implemented, harsh and wicked deeds did not occur, and there were no tigers or wolves in the mountains. He died in Xiangjiang, and two white tigers, crouching down and dragging their tails, guarded beside his body. Upon his departure after death, the tigers crossed the borders of the state and suddenly disappeared. The people collectively erected a stele and named it: The Tomb of the White Tigers of Xiangjiang. During the Wu period, Ge Zuo served as the governor of Hengyang. There was a large log blocking the river in the jurisdiction, capable of causing mischief. The people built a temple for it, and travelers prayed and made offerings. The log would sink if they did so, but if not, it would float and destroy boats. Ge Zuo was about to leave his office and gathered many axes to remove the burden on the people. That night, he heard loud noises like people in the river. He went to see and found the log had moved away, flowing downstream several miles and stopping in a bay. From then on, travelers no longer suffered from the danger of sinking. The people of Hengyang erected a stele for Ge Zuo, naming it: Righteousness and prayers moved the divine wood.

Zengzi was following Confucius in the state of Chu when his heart was moved, and he respectfully declined and returned home. He asked his mother, who said, "I miss you, and I bite my finger." Confucius remarked, "Zengzi's filial piety could move over a thousand miles." Zhan Chang was a kind and filial man who lived alone with his mother. Whenever she wanted to call him, she would bite her hand, and Zhan Chang would feel pain in his hand and come to her. The governor of Henan did not believe this story. He waited for Zhan Chang to be in the fields and made his mother bite her hand, and Zhan Chang immediately returned. In the second year of the Yuanchu era, Zhan Chang became the governor of Henan. There was a severe drought in summer, and long prayers had no response. Zhan Chang gathered and buried more than ten thousand travelers' remains near the city of Luoyang and established a public cemetery. In response to the time, abundant rain fell. Wang Xiang, with the courtesy name Xiuzheng, was from Langya. He had an extremely filial nature and lost his parents at a young age. His stepmother Zhu was not kind and often slandered him, causing him to lose his father's affection. He was often made to clean under the ox. When his parents were ill, he did not take off his clothes or remove his belt. His mother often wanted fresh fish, and it was cold at the time with the ice frozen. Wang Xiang took off his clothes and intended to cut through the ice to find it. The ice suddenly split open by itself, and two carp jumped out. He took them and returned. His mother also thought of roasted quail, and there were again dozens of quail that entered his tent, which he used to provide for his mother. The local people were amazed and exclaimed, believing that his filial piety caused these miracles.

Wang Yan was known for his utmost filial piety. His stepmother, Bo, once in the severe winter, thought of fresh fish and ordered Wang Yan to find it. He failed to obtain it and was beaten until he bled. Wang Yan then went to the Fen River and knocked on the ice while crying. Suddenly, a fish five feet long leaped out of the ice. Wang Yan caught it and presented it to his mother. Bo ate it, and for several days it did not finish. She then realized the truth and treated Wang Yan as her own son. Liao, on the other hand, lost his mother early and showed utmost filial piety to his stepmother. His stepmother suffered from boils, and her appearance became increasingly emaciated. Liao slowly sucked on the boils, and blood came out, and by night she was able to sleep peacefully. One night, she dreamed of a little boy who said to her, "If you can eat a carp, your illness will be cured, and you can live longer. Otherwise, you will die soon." She woke up and told Liao about the dream. It was December, and the ice was frozen. Liao then looked up to the sky, wept, and took off his clothes to lie on the ice. A little boy appeared and broke the ice where Liao lay, and a pair of carps leaped out. Liao took them back to his stepmother, and her illness was cured. She lived to be 133 years old. It is said that his utmost filial piety moved the gods, and thus this miraculous event occurred. This is similar to the stories of Wang Xiang and Wang Yan.

Sheng Yan, courtesy name Wengzi, was from Guangling. His mother, Wang, had lost her sight due to an illness, and Yan personally took care of her. Whenever his mother ate, he would feed her himself. After his mother's illness persisted for a long time, the servant, resentful of being frequently beaten, took advantage of Yan's brief absence to prepare a roasted beetle larva and serve it to his mother. His mother thought it delicious but suspected it was something unusual, so she secretly kept it to show to Yan. When Yan saw it, he held his mother and cried bitterly, causing her to faint and then revive. His mother's eyes suddenly opened, and she was healed at that moment. Yan Han, courtesy name Hongdu, had a sister-in-law named Fan who also lost her sight due to an illness. The doctor prescribed a remedy that required the bile of a king cobra, and they searched thoroughly but could not obtain it. Yan Han worried and sighed for a long time. One day, while sitting alone during the day, he suddenly saw a young boy dressed in green, about thirteen or fourteen years old, holding a green bag and giving it to him. Yan Han opened it and found it contained the snake bile. The boy hesitated and went out the door, transforming into a green bird and flying away. With the bile, the medicine was prepared, and his sister-in-law's illness was cured.

Guo Ju was from Longlu (modern-day Henan), though some say he was from Henan's Wen County. He had two younger brothers, and after their father died, they completed the funeral rites. The two younger brothers then requested to divide the inheritance, which amounted to two million coins. Each brother took one million coins, leaving Guo Ju to live alone with his mother in a rented house, where he and his wife worked as hired laborers to support their mother. After a short while, Guo Ju's wife gave birth to a son. Guo Ju thought that raising a son would interfere with serving his parents, and the elderly enjoyed sharing food with their grandchildren, reducing their own meals. So, he dug a hole in the wilderness, intending to bury his son. He found a stone lid covering a pot of gold, and inside there was a red scroll that read, "To the filial son Guo Ju, a pot of gold, to be used as a gift to you." His fame spread throughout the world. Liu Yin, from Xinxing, whose courtesy name was Changsheng, lost his father at the age of seven. His grief and mourning exceeded the prescribed rituals, and he observed the three-year mourning period without smiling. He cared for his great-grandmother Wang. Once, he dreamed that someone told him, "There is millet under the western fence." He woke up and dug there, finding fifteen shi of millet. The inscription said, "Seven years of millet, to be given to the filial son Liu Yin." He and his family ate from this supply for seven years until it was finished. When Wang passed away, his husband and wife were so grief-stricken that they nearly lost their lives. When the coffin was being mourned, a fire broke out in the west neighbor's house. The wind was very strong. Liu Yin and his wife knelt by the coffin and cried, and the fire was extinguished. Later, two white doves came to nest in the courtyard trees.

Elong, from Yanggong, was a man from Long County who originally made a living as a merchant. By nature, he was filial. After his parents' death, he buried them at Wu Zong Mountain and settled there. The mountain was eighty li high, and there was no water at the top. Elong fetched water and made a charitable drink at the top of the slope, and all passersby drank it. After three years, a man came to drink and gave him a basket of stones, instructing him to plant them in a high, flat place with good soil. He said, "Jade will grow from these stones." Elong had not yet married, and the man also said, "You will eventually get a good wife." After speaking, the man disappeared. Elong planted the stones, and after several years, he often went to check on them and saw jade sprouting from the stones. No one knew about this. There was a woman from the Xu family, a prominent family in the right and north of Ping, who was very virtuous. Many people sought her hand in marriage, but she refused them all. Elong then tried to marry her, and she laughed at him, thinking he was crazy. She said, "Bring me a pair of white jade, and I will agree to the marriage." Elong went to the field where he had planted the stones and found five pairs of white jade. He brought them to the Xu family, and she was greatly surprised. She then married him. The emperor heard about this and was impressed, and he appointed Elong as a high official. In the place where the jade grew, four large stone pillars were built, each one zhang tall, and the central area of one mu of land was named "Jade Field."

衡农，字剽卿，是东平人。他自幼失去父亲，对继母极为孝顺。有一次，他在别人家住宿时，遇到雷雨天气，频繁梦见老虎咬他的脚。衡农便叫妻子一起到院子里，向天叩头三次。突然，房屋倒塌，压死了三十多人，唯独衡农夫妇幸免于难。 罗威，字德仁，八岁时父亲去世，他对母亲非常孝顺。母亲年已七十，每当天气寒冷，罗威都会用自己的身体先温暖床铺，然后再让母亲睡上去。 王裒，字伟元，是城阳营陵人。他的父亲王仪被文帝所杀。王裒在父亲的墓旁搭建了一间草屋，早晚都会到墓前拜祭，有时还会爬到柏树上痛哭流涕，眼泪滴在树上，以至于树都枯萎了。母亲生前害怕打雷，母亲去世后，每当打雷，王裒就会跑到墓前说：“我在这里。”

Zhen Hong was promoted to the position of Governor of Linyi. A local resident named Xu Xian, while in mourning for his parents, expressed his grief so deeply that a white dove nested near his house. Zhen Hong recommended Xu Xian as a孝廉. The court then referred to him as "White Dove Gentleman." During the Han Dynasty, there was a filial woman from the Eastern Sea region who took care of her mother-in-law with utmost diligence. Her mother-in-law said to her, "You have been taking care of me with great effort. I am old, and why should I cherish my remaining years and burden you, the young?" She then hanged herself. The woman's daughter reported to the authorities, claiming that her mother had killed her grandmother. The officials arrested the filial woman and subjected her to severe torture. Unable to bear the pain, she falsely confessed to the crime. At that time, Yu Gong was a prison official who said, "This woman has cared for her mother-in-law for over ten years, known for her filial piety, and surely would not kill her." However, the governor did not listen. Yu Gong argued in vain, and with the case documents in hand, he cried before the governor and left. After this, the entire county experienced a severe drought that lasted for three years without rain. When the new governor arrived, Yu Gong said, "This filial woman should not have died; the previous governor wrongfully executed her, and the calamity should be attributed to this." The new governor immediately went to the filial woman's grave to offer sacrifices and erected a monument at her tomb. Immediately, it rained, and the year produced a bountiful harvest. The elders passed down that the filial woman's name was Zhou Qing. Before her execution, she loaded a cart with a ten-zhang bamboo pole and hung five flags, swearing to the crowd, "If I am guilty, I wish to be killed; my blood should flow downward. If I am wrongfully executed, my blood should flow upward." After the execution, her blood flowed upward, turning green and yellow as it climbed the flagpole, reaching the top, and then flowed back down.

During the Yongjian era, in the third year, Shu Xian Ni He of Jianwei was a county official responsible for various administrative duties. The county magistrate, Zhao Zhi, sent him to deliver an official notice to pay respects to the governor of Bashu. In October, while traveling by boat, he fell into the turbulent waters of Chengtuan and drowned, and his body could not be recovered. His daughter, Xiong, who was twenty-seven years old, was overcome with grief and wept loudly, declaring that she had no intention of living. She told her brother Xian and her husband, instructing them to diligently search for her father's body. If they failed to find it, she said, she would drown herself in search of it. At the time, Xiong had two sons: Gòng, who was five years old, and Shì, who was three years old. She made each son a small embroidered bag filled with gold and jewels, and wore them around their necks, continuously crying out in grief. Her family privately worried about her. On the fifteenth day of December, when her father's body still could not be found, Xiong took a small boat to the place where her father had fallen, wept several times, and then jumped into the water, sinking to the bottom. She appeared in a dream to her brother and said, "On the twenty-first day, I will come out with my father." On that day, as predicted in her dream, she emerged from the water with her father, holding him tightly as they floated out of the river. The county magistrate reported this to the governor of the prefecture, who in turn reported to the imperial court. The court then sent an official to erect a stele in honor of Xiong, depicting her image, to show her extreme filial piety.

**The Wife of Le Yangzi from Henan** The wife of Le Yangzi from Henan, whose family name is unknown, diligently cared for her mother-in-law. Once, a hen from another house mistakenly entered their garden, and the mother-in-law secretly killed and ate it. The wife, upon seeing this, did not eat the meat and wept. Her mother-in-law was surprised and asked her why she was crying. The wife replied, "I feel ashamed to live in poverty and eat food that is not earned by my own labor." The mother-in-law was so moved that she threw away the meat. Later, a thief intended to rob them. Before attacking, he first kidnapped the mother-in-law. The wife, hearing the commotion, took a knife and went out. The thief said, "Put down your knife. If you follow me, you can be spared; if you do not, I will kill your mother-in-law." The wife, looking up to the sky, sighed and then cut her own throat and died. The thief did not kill the mother-in-law. The local official, hearing of this, captured and killed the thief, granted the wife silk and cloth, and buried her with proper rites. **Gu Yun, also known as Shubiao** During the Xian period of the Three Kingdoms era, a great epidemic broke out. Two of Gu Yun's brothers died, and his third brother, Bi, was gravely ill. The epidemic was at its peak, and his parents and younger brothers all left the house. Only Gu Yun stayed behind, refusing to leave. His elders and brothers tried to persuade him to go, but he said, "I am not afraid of the disease." He stayed with his brother, Bi, day and night, never sleeping. Occasionally, he would mourn at his brother's coffin without ceasing. This continued for more than three months. Once the epidemic subsided, the family returned. Bi recovered, and Gu Yun remained unharmed.

King Kang of Song had a retainer named Han Peng, who married a woman named He, renowned for her beauty. King Kang coveted her and took her for himself. Han Peng, filled with resentment, was imprisoned by the king and sentenced to penal labor. His wife secretly sent him a letter, obfuscating its meaning with poetic language: “The rain falls heavily, the river is deep and wide, the sun rises in my heart.” Later, the king obtained the letter and showed it to his attendants, none of whom could decipher its meaning. Minister Su He explained, “The rain falling heavily signifies sorrow and longing. The deep and wide river indicates separation. The sun rising in the heart means she has resolved to die.” Soon after, Han Peng committed suicide. His wife then secretly made her clothes rot, and the king took her to the tower. She leaped from the tower, and her attendants tried to catch her, but her clothes did not catch, and she died. She left a message on her belt: “The king benefits from my life, but I benefit from my death. I wish to be buried together with Han Peng.” The king was furious and refused to allow it, ordering her to be buried by the villagers, with their tombs facing each other. The king said, “If your love for each other persists, and you can make the tombs join, then I will not obstruct you.” Within a night or day, a large catalpa tree grew from the ends of the two tombs, growing to a size that could be embraced within ten days. The trees bent towards each other, their roots intertwining below and their branches above. There were also mandarin ducks, one male and one female, always perched on the tree. They did not leave day or night, calling to each other in sorrow, and their sounds moved people. The people of Song mourned their plight and named the tree the “Thinking of Each Other Tree.” The term “Thinking of Each Other” originated from this. Southern people say: these birds are the spirits of Han Peng and his wife. Now in Suizhou, there is a city named after Han Peng, and its songs and ballads still exist today.

During the end of the Han Dynasty, the governor of Lingyang County, Shi Man, had a daughter who fell in love with a subordinate clerk. She secretly sent a female servant to fetch the leftover water from the clerk's handwashing and drink it, and thus became pregnant. Later, she gave birth to a son. When the child was able to walk, the governor ordered him to be taken out to find his father. The child crawled directly into the clerk's arms. The clerk pushed him down, and he turned into water. When thoroughly questioned, he fully understood the previous events, and so married his daughter to the clerk. West of Poyang there is a hill called Wife-Watching Hill. In the past, a man named Chen Ming from the county was betrothed to the family of Mei. But before the marriage was completed, a demon deceitfully took the bride away. Chen Ming went to a fortune-teller, who advised him: "Go fifty miles northwest to find her." Chen Ming followed the advice and saw a large cave, deep and bottomless. He lowered someone with a rope and eventually found his wife. He then ordered his wife to come out first, but the neighbor Qin Wen did not take Chen Ming. His wife then vowed to remain faithful and climbed to the top of this hill to look for her husband, and thus the hill was named after her.

During the Eastern Han Dynasty, in Nankang, there was a man named Deng Yuanyi. His father, Deng Bokao, held the position of Secretary of the Imperial Secretariat. After returning to his hometown, Deng Yuanyi's wife remained to serve her mother-in-law with great respect. However, the mother-in-law detested her and confined her in an empty room, restricting her food and causing her to weaken and suffer daily, yet she never complained. At this time, Deng Bokao was surprised and asked about it. Deng Yuanyi's son, Lang, who was only a few years old at the time, said, "Mother is not sick, she just suffers from hunger." Deng Bokao wept and said, "How could treating one's mother-in-law result in such misfortune!" He sent her back home to be remarried, and she became the wife of Hua Zhong. Hua Zhong was the Chief Architect, and when his wife rode out in an official carriage, Deng Yuanyi watched from the side of the road and said to others, "This is my former wife; she had no other fault, but my wife treated her terribly, and we were originally respected by each other." At that time, Deng Lang was a junior official. When his mother wrote to him, he did not reply, and when she gave him clothes, he would burn them. The mother did not take it to heart. She wanted to see him and went to the R family's hall, sending someone to invite Lang with another excuse. When Lang arrived, he bowed twice and wept, then got up and left. The mother followed and said, "I nearly died. I was abandoned by your family, what have I done wrong to be treated like this!" Because of this, they became estranged.

Yan Zun served as the governor of Yangzhou. On one of his inspections, he heard a woman weeping by the roadside, and her voice was not sorrowful. He asked whom she was weeping for. She replied, "My husband died in a fire." Yan Zun ordered the officials to carry the corpse to him. When he spoke to the dead man, the man claimed, "I did not die from fire." Yan Zun then arrested the woman and ordered someone to guard the corpse, saying, "There must be some injustice." An official reported, "There were flies gathering around her head." Yan Zun ordered the corpse to be examined, and they found an iron spike through her head. Upon investigation, it was discovered that she had killed her husband through adultery. During the Han dynasty, Fan Shi, courtesy name Jüqing, was from Jinxiang, Shanyang. He was also known by the name Pan and was friends with Zhang Shao from Runan, whose courtesy name was Yuanbo. Both of them studied at the Imperial Academy. Later, they returned to their hometowns. Fan Shi told Yuanbo, "In two years, I will return. I will visit my esteemed relatives and see your young son." They agreed on a specific date. When the agreed date arrived, Yuanbo told his mother in detail, asking her to prepare a meal to welcome him. His mother said, "After two years of separation, you are making a promise from a thousand miles away. How can you be so sure of your trust?" Yuanbo replied, "Jüqing is a trustworthy man; he will surely not go back on his word." His mother said, "If that is so, I will prepare wine for you." When the day arrived, Fan Shi indeed came. They went up to the hall, bowed, drank, and parted in joy. Later, Yuanbo fell seriously ill. His fellow townsman Dao Junzhang and Yin Zizheng visited him day and night. As Yuanbo lay dying, he sighed, "I regret not seeing my dear friend die."

Zǐzhēng said, "I and your brother devoted ourselves to you. These are not true friends, but whom else do we seek?" Yuánbó replied, "If these two are true friends, they are only my living friends. Fan Jüqing of Shanyang, that is what is meant by true friends." Soon after, he died. Zhì suddenly dreamed of Yuánbó, who was wearing a black cap, with hanging tassels, and sandals, and called out, "Jüqing! I will die on a certain day, and will be buried at that time. I will forever return to the Yellow Springs. You have not forgotten me, how could you not come?" Zhì suddenly realized, and wept bitterly. He immediately dressed in mourning clothes, and on the day of the burial, he rushed to attend. Before he arrived, the funeral had already started. After arriving at the grave, when they were about to lower the coffin, the coffin would not move forward. His mother comforted him, saying, "Yuanbo! Is there any hope?" So they stopped the coffin for a while. Then they saw a white carriage, with a white horse, and came crying. His mother looked at them, saying, "It must be Fan Jüqing." When they arrived, they knelt in mourning, saying, "Farewell, Yuanbo! Life and death are different paths, we will never meet again." A thousand people gathered for the funeral, all of them wept. Zhì then took hold of the ropes and led the coffin. So it moved forward. Zhì then stayed at the grave, and repaired the tomb and planted trees, and then left.

Heaven has five elements, and all things are transformed through them: when wood is pure, it embodies benevolence; when fire is pure, it embodies propriety; when metal is pure, it embodies righteousness; when water is pure, it embodies wisdom; when earth is pure, it embodies thought. When the five elements are entirely pure, the virtues of a sage are complete.
