When wood is impure, it becomes weak; when fire is impure, it becomes licentious; when metal is impure, it becomes violent; when water is impure, it becomes greedy; when earth is impure, it becomes stubborn. When the five elements are entirely impure, it results in the lowest level of the people. The Central Land produces many sages because of the harmonious interaction of the elements. Remote regions produce many strange creatures because of the unusual elemental influences. If one is endowed with such elements, they will have such a form; if one has such a form, they will have such a nature. Therefore, those who eat grains are intelligent and cultured; those who eat grass are strong but foolish; those who feed on mulberry leaves produce silk and become silkworms; those who eat meat are brave and fierce; those who eat earth have no heart but continue to live; those who live on qi (vital energy) are divine and long-lived; and those who do not eat physical food are immortal and divine.

A creature with a large body lacks a male counterpart, and one with a slender body lacks a female counterpart; without external fertilization by males, and without external nurturing by females. There are insects that become pregnant before mating, and animals that can change their sex; parasitic organisms depend on tall trees, and climbing plants rely on fungi, while trees grow in the soil, and water plants thrive in water. Birds soar through the air, and beasts walk on solid ground; insects burrow into the earth and hibernate, and fish hide in deep waters. Those rooted in heaven are drawn upward, those rooted in earth are drawn downward, and those rooted in time are drawn to the sides: each follows its own kind. A thousand-year-old pheasant transforms into a sea shell; a hundred-year-old sparrow turns into a clam; a thousand-year-old turtle or softshell turtle can communicate with humans; a thousand-year-old fox transforms into a beautiful woman; a thousand-year-old snake can regenerate after being cut; a hundred-year-old mouse can predict the future: this is the limit of numbers. On the day of the Spring Equinox, eagles transform into doves; on the day of the Autumn Equinox, doves transform into eagles: this is the transformation of time.

Therefore, when decaying grass transforms into fireflies, rotten reeds into cicadas, rice into [unknown creature], and wheat into butterflies; wings and feathers grow, eyes and eyes form, and intellect arises: this is the transformation from a state of no consciousness to one of consciousness, with a change in qi. When sparrows transform into deer, and cicadas into shrimp: the vital energy remains the same, but the form and nature change. Such transformations are numerous and cannot be fully discussed. Responding to changes and acting accordingly is in accordance with the natural order; if the order is disrupted, it becomes an omen of misfortune. When the lower body is born from the upper, and the upper body is born from the lower: this is a reversal of the usual order of generation. When humans transform into animals, and animals transform into humans: this is a transformation between genders. When men transform into women, and women transform into men: this is a transformation of gender. In the state of Lu, Niu Ai, suffering from illness, transformed into a tiger after seven days; his form changed, and his claws and teeth emerged. His brother opened the door and entered, and he attacked and ate him.

When it is human, one does not know it will become a tiger; when it becomes a tiger, one does not know it was once human. Thus, during the Western Jin dynasty, in the reign of Emperor Sima Zhong, a man from Chenliu named Ruanyi was bitten by a snake. Unable to bear the pain, he sniffed his wound repeatedly, and later two snakes formed in his nose. In the reign of Emperor Sima Ying, a guest from Liyang named Jiyuan ate a turtle, which later turned into a tumor. A doctor treated him with medicine, and several liters of turtle offspring were expelled, each as large as a small coin, with heads, feet, and shells intact, though the internal organs were dead. The nose is not a place for reproduction, and enjoyment of the Dao is not a tool for bringing down things. From this perspective, the birth and death of all things, and their transformations, cannot be understood without a divine insight, even if one seeks within oneself, how can one know the origin of such transformations? Yet, rotten grass becomes fireflies due to decay, and wheat becomes butterflies due to moisture. Therefore, all transformations of things have their causes. The farmer stops the transformation of wheat by soaking it in ash; the sage manages the transformations of all things by guiding them with the Dao. Is this true, or is it not?

Duke Ji Huan was digging a well and found an object resembling a clay jar. Inside it, there was a sheep. He sent someone to ask Confucius, saying, "When I dug the well, I found a dog. Why is that?" Confucius replied, "According to what I have heard, it is a sheep. I have heard that among the strange creatures of wood and stone are the *kui* and *wailing*; among those of water are the dragon and *wangxiang*; and among those of the earth is the *benyang*." The *Xia Ding Zhi* states, "The *wangxiang* looks like a three-year-old child, with red eyes, black skin, large ears, long arms, and red claws. If you bind it, you can eat it." The Prince said, "The spirit of wood is called *youguang*, and the spirit of metal is called *qingming*."

During the Yuan Kang period of Emperor Huai of Jin, a man named Huai Yao from Lou County in Wu Prefecture suddenly heard faint barking coming from underground. Upon examining the source of the sound, he found a small hole, about the size of a worm's burrow. Using a stick, he probed the hole and, after inserting it several feet, felt something inside. He then dug further and discovered two puppies, one male and one female, whose eyes were not yet open and whose bodies were larger than those of ordinary dogs. He fed them, and they were soon taken to see by everyone around. An elder remarked, "These are called 'rhinoceros dogs,' and whoever finds them will bring prosperity and wealth to their household. They should be kept." Since the puppies' eyes were not yet open, he returned them to the hole, covered it with a stone mill, and left them there. After a night or two, when he checked again, the hole was gone, and the puppies had disappeared. For many years, Huai Yao's family experienced no other misfortune. Later, during the Tai Xing period, the governor of Wu Prefecture, Zhang Mao, heard barking coming from under his bed in his study. He searched but could not find the source. Suddenly, the ground split open, and two puppies emerged. He took them and kept them, but both died soon after. Eventually, Zhang Mao was killed by Wu Xing's military leader, Shen Chong. Shi Zi said, "A dog found underground is called 'earth wolf'; a person found underground is called 'unharmed.'" The *Xia Ding Zhi* states, "A dog found by digging is called 'Jia'; a pig found by digging is called 'Yi'; a person found by digging is called 'Ju'; 'Ju' means 'unharmed.'" These are natural occurrences and should not be attributed to ghosts or gods and seen as strange. Thus, 'Jia' and 'earth wolf' have different names but refer to the same thing. Huainan Bi Wan said, "A thousand-year-old sheep's liver transforms into an 'earth ruler'; a toad that gets 'upper grass lower melon' suddenly becomes a pheasant." All these phenomena are due to the transformation of qi and mutual influence.

Wu's Zhuge Ke served as the Governor of Danyang. Once, while out hunting, he encountered a creature between two mountains that resembled a child, stretching out a hand to pull someone. Zhuge Ke ordered it to stretch its hand, and it pulled away from its original place. Upon leaving its original place, it died. Later, his advisors asked about the reason for this, thinking it was a divine being. Zhuge Ke replied, "This matter is recorded in the *Bai Ze* Diagram; it says: 'Between two mountains, its essence is like a child, and when it sees people, it stretches out a hand to pull them, called "Xinang," and if it is pulled away from its original place, it dies.' Do not consider it divine and be surprised by it. You gentlemen have just not seen it before." During the fourth year of Wang Mang's reign in the Xin Dynasty, in Chi Yang, there appeared small figures about a foot and a half long, some riding in carriages, others walking, carrying all kinds of objects, large and small, in proportion to their size, and this continued for three days. Wang Mang greatly disliked it. Afterward, bandits became more rampant, and Wang Mang was eventually killed. The *Guanzi* states: "A dried-up river for several hundred years, the valley does not move, but the water does not stop, giving birth to 'Qing Ji.' 'Qing Ji' is a creature that looks like a person, four inches tall, wearing yellow clothes, a yellow crown, a yellow canopy, riding a small horse, and enjoys running fast. If you call its name, it can return a report from a thousand miles away in one day." Perhaps the figures in Chi Yang are 'Qing Ji'? It also states: "A dried-up small water spirit gives birth to 'Chi.'" 'Chi' is a creature with one head and two bodies, looking like a snake, eight feet long, and if you call its name, it can fetch fish and turtles.

During the Jin Dynasty, Yang Daohe from Fufeng was in the fields during the summer when he encountered rain. He took shelter under a mulberry tree, and a thunderbolt struck from above. Daohe used a hoe to block and break its leg, causing it to fall to the ground and be unable to leave. The creature had lips red like cinnabar, eyes bright like mirrors, hairs and horns three inches long, an overall appearance resembling livestock, and a head resembling a macaque. During the Qin Dynasty, there were mythical people in the south called "落头民," whose heads could fly. Their kind had a ritual called "虫落," which is why they were named as such. During the Wu Dynasty, General Zhu Huan obtained a servant. Every night after she lay down, her head would fly away. It would fly through dog holes or skylights, using its ears as wings, and return in the morning. Those around her found this strange. One night, they illuminated her to see that she had only a body with no head, and her body was slightly cold with just a breath of life remaining. They covered her with a blanket. In the morning, her head returned, obstructed by the blanket and unable to settle, falling to the ground several times. She was very distressed, her body was very tense, and she seemed on the verge of death. They then removed the blanket, and her head returned to her neck. After a while, she calmed down. Zhu Huan thought this was a great marvel and was afraid to keep her, so he released her. Later, he realized that this was her natural nature. At that time, generals on southern campaigns also often encountered such cases. There was once a case where a copper plate was used, and the head could not enter, leading to death.

The regions along the Yangtze and Han rivers are home to the "貙人," whose ancestors are descendants of Lord Lin, and who can transform into tigers. In a county under the jurisdiction of Changsha, the residents of Donggao set a trap to catch a tiger. The trap was triggered, and the next day, a group of people went to confront it. They saw a local official, wearing a red headgear and a large hat, sitting in the trap. They asked him, "How did you enter here?" The official, named Hengzhang, became very angry and said, "I was suddenly summoned by the county last night, and while avoiding the rain, I accidentally entered here. Let me out quickly!" They replied, "Shouldn't you have received a summons?" He then took out a document from his chest, which was the summons. Upon inspection, he transformed back into a tiger and ran up the mountain. It is said that the "貙" is a creature that can transform into a human, often wearing a purple cotton robe and having feet without heels, and tigers with five fingers are all "貙."

In the high mountains of the southwest of Shu, there is a creature similar to monkeys, seven feet tall, capable of walking like a human and good at chasing people. It is called "Jiaoguo," also known as "Mahuo," or "Jueyuan." It waits for beautiful women traveling on the road and steals them, taking them away without anyone knowing. If there are passersby nearby, they all use long ropes to pull each other, but they still cannot escape. This creature can distinguish between the scent of men and women, so it takes women but not men. If it takes a human woman, it makes her its wife. Those who do not have children cannot return for life. After ten years, their appearance becomes similar to the creature. They also become confused and no longer think of returning. If they have children, they are carried back to their families. The children they produce are all human-like. If they do not raise the children, the mother will die; therefore, they are afraid and dare not neglect the children. When they grow up, they are no different from humans. All are surnamed Yang. Therefore, there are many people surnamed Yang in the southwest of Shu today, most of whom are descendants of the "Jiaoguo" and "Mahuo."

In the mountains around Linchuan, there is a supernatural entity that often appears during violent storms. It makes a howling sound and can shoot at people. Those struck by it will swell and become severely poisoned within a short time. The entity has both male and female forms: the male acts quickly, while the female acts slowly. The male form causes effects within half a day, while the female form takes a whole night. There are methods to save those affected, but if the rescue is delayed, the victim will die. This entity is commonly known as the "Dao Laogui." As an external source states, "Ghosts and spirits are the manifestations of fortune and misfortune in the world." Laozi says, "In the past, those who attained unity: heaven attained clarity, earth attained peace, spirits attained divinity, valleys attained fullness, and rulers attained the stability of the state." Thus, heaven, earth, ghosts, and spirits coexist with us; different essences lead to different natures, and different realms result in different forms, and none can be combined. Living beings are associated with yang, while the dead are associated with yin. Each entity finds its place and lives according to its nature. In the realm of yin, strange and monstrous entities exist.

In the deep mountains of Yue, there is a bird as large as a jay, with a greenish-blue color, named "冶鸟." It digs into large trees to build its nest, which is about the size of a container holding five or six sheng, with an entrance several inches in diameter. The nest is decorated with earth and clay, alternating between red and white, resembling a target. Woodcutters who see such a tree will immediately avoid and leave it. Sometimes, at night when the woodcutter cannot see the bird, the bird also knows that the person cannot see it and will call out, "Dude, go up!" The woodcutter should climb the tree quickly the next day. "Dude, go down!" The woodcutter should climb down quickly the next day. If the bird does not want the woodcutter to leave, it will laugh persistently until the woodcutter stops. If the nest is defiled, tigers will guard it all night long, and if the woodcutter does not leave, the tigers will harm them. This bird appears as a bird during the day and makes bird-like sounds at night. Sometimes, it transforms into a human-like form, about three feet tall, to fetch stone crabs from a stream. It roasts the crabs and warns humans not to interfere. The people of Yue consider this bird to be the ancestor of the "越祝" (Yue shamans).

Beyond the South Sea, there are "鲛人" (mermaids), who live in water, resembling fish yet do not cease from weaving. When their eyes weep, they can produce pearls. In the regions of Lujiang and Zongyang, there are sometimes heard cries, often as many as dozens of people, men, women, young and old, as if they had just lost someone. Neighbors are startled and rush to the scene, but often do not see anyone. However, at the place where they cried, there is always a death. Generally, if the sound was loud, it was for a large family; if it was soft, it was for a small family. In the vast mountains of Lujiang, there are "山都" (mountain people), who resemble humans, are naked, and run away when they see people. They can grow up to four or five zhang tall, can call to each other, and often reside in dark and obscure places, resembling ghosts and spirits. During the reign of Emperor Guangwu in the Zhongping era (Editor's note: Zhongping should be Zhongyuan, as Emperor Guangwu did not have a Zhongping era. Or perhaps it is a mistake and refers to Emperor Ling.), there was a creature in the river, known as "yù" or "short fox." It can shoot people with sand. Those who are hit experience muscle stiffness, headache, fever, and severe cases can lead to death. People from the river use techniques to suppress it, and sand and stones are found in the flesh. As the poem says, "they are ghosts or yù," they are unpredictable. Nowadays it is called "river poison." Ancient scholars believed that it was caused by men and women bathing in the same river, with promiscuous women being the source of disorder.

During the Han dynasty, in the county of Buiwu of Yongchang Prefecture, there was a river known as the Forbidden Water. This river emitted toxic gases, making it only safe to cross in the 11th and 13th lunar months. From the 1st to the 10th months, it was dangerous to cross, as doing so would cause illness and death. The air around the river contained something evil, which could not be seen but seemed to make a sound. If anything were thrown into the middle of the water, the wood would break; if it hit a person, it would harm them. Locals called this phenomenon "Ghost Bullet." Therefore, criminals from the prefecture were exiled to the forbidden area, and within ten days, they all died. My sister-in-law's husband, Jiang Shi, had a hired guest who fell ill and bled. The doctor diagnosed it as a case of poisoning by a venomous creature. He secretly placed the roots of the *náng hé* (a type of herb) under the mat, without the patient knowing. The patient then spoke wildly, saying, "The one who ate my worm is Zhang Xiaoxiao." He also cried out, "Xiaoxiao has died." In modern times, treating poisonings often involves using *náng hé* roots, and it is often effective. *Náng hé* is also called "good grass." In Poyang, Zhao Shou had a dog that was also affected by poisoning. When Chen Cen visited Zhao Shou, suddenly six or seven groups of large yellow dogs appeared and barked at Chen Cen. Later, my uncle returned home with Zhao Shou's wife and they ate together, and she vomited blood and nearly died. He then ground up *qi ji* (a type of herb) and gave it to her to drink, and she recovered. Poisonous creatures are described as monsters like ghosts, with strange forms that change into various kinds of creatures. Sometimes they appear as dogs or pigs, sometimes as insects or snakes. The person affected does not know their own form. They act among the people, and those who are affected all die.

In Xingyang Prefecture, there was a family surnamed Liao, who had practiced the art of "蛊" for generations, thereby becoming wealthy. After they took a new wife, they did not tell her about their practice. One day, when the family members went out, leaving only the new wife at home, she suddenly saw a large jar in the house. Curious, she opened it and found a large snake inside. She then poured water over it and killed it. When the family returned, the new wife reported the incident in detail, and the entire family was shocked and grieved. Soon after, the family suffered from illness and pestilence, and nearly all of them died. *Search for the Gods* Volume Thirteen In the east of Mount Tai, there is a spring called Li Spring, which looks like a well and is actually made of stone. Those who wish to drink from it must cleanse their hearts and minds, kneel down, and scoop the water. The spring then flows out as if flying, providing enough water for their needs. If the water is polluted, the spring will stop flowing. This is said to be a test of one's sincerity by the divine. The two peaks of Mount Hua were originally one mountain, situated in the path of the Yellow River. The river flowed around it in a curved path. The river god, Great Spirit, used his hands to split the mountain apart and his feet to separate it further, dividing it into two. This action was done to facilitate the flow of the river. Today, one can still see the handprints on Mount Hua, with the shapes of fingers and palms clearly visible. The footprints are found at the base of Mount Shouyang and are still present. This is what Zhang Heng referred to in his *Western Capital Fu* when he wrote, "The Great Spirit, with his powerful hands and long feet, flowed through the bends of the Yellow River."

During the reign of Emperor Wu of Han, the sacrifices to the Southern Mountain (Nanyue) were relocated to the area of Lujiang, in Qian County and on Mount Huoshan. There was no water there, but the temple had four ritual vessels, each capable of holding forty dou. When the sacrifices were performed, the vessels would always fill with water on their own, and the water was sufficient for the rites. Once the sacrifices were completed, the vessels would become empty, and neither dust nor leaves could defile them. This phenomenon lasted for fifty years, during which four sacrifices were held each year. Later, only three sacrifices were held, and one of the vessels broke on its own. At the mouth of Fan East, there is Fan Mountain. If there was a drought, people would burn the mountain, and immediate heavy rain would follow. This practice is still in use today and has been verified. In a place now called Kongbao, located in the south of Lu, there is a cave outside a mountain. Outside the cave, there are two stones, standing like pillars, several zhang tall. The people of Lu would sing and play music during their sacrifices. There was no water in the cave, but whenever the sacrifices were performed, after cleaning and announcing, there would always be a clear spring emerging from between the stones, sufficient for the rites. Once the sacrifices were completed, the spring would stop flowing. This phenomenon still exists to this day. In the cave of Xiang, there is black soil. During a severe drought, people would block the cave with water, and once the cave was blocked, heavy rain would immediately arrive. In the 27th year of King Hui of Qin's reign, he ordered Zhang Yi to build the city of Chengdu. The city repeatedly collapsed until suddenly, a large turtle floated down the river and died at the southeast corner of the eastern city. Zhang Yi asked the diviners about this, and they said, "Build the city according to the turtle." The city was completed, and thus it was named the Turtle Transformation City.

Yōuquán County, during the Qin dynasty, was formerly known as Chángshuǐ County. During the reign of the First Emperor, a children's rhyme circulated: "If there is blood at the city gate, the city will sink and become a lake." An old woman heard this and went to peek at the city gate every day. The city gate guard wanted to tie her up. The old woman explained the reason. Later, the guard smeared dog blood on the city gate. When the old woman saw the blood, she ran away. Suddenly, a great flood came, threatening to engulf the entire county. The county clerk sent a messenger to inform the magistrate. The magistrate asked, "Why did you suddenly become a fish?" The messenger replied, "The magistrate also became a fish." The county was thus submerged and became a lake. During the Qin dynasty, a city was built within the Wúzhōu pass to defend against the Hu (northern nomads). The city was repeatedly destroyed before it could be completed. A horse ran around and turned repeatedly, which the elders found strange. They then built the city following the horse's path, and the city did not collapse. It was then named Mǎyì. The ruins of the old city are now located in Shuozhou. During the reign of Emperor Wu of Han, he dug the Kunming Pool, which was very deep, filled with ash and soot, with no soil. The entire court could not understand why. They asked Dongfang Shuo, who replied, "I am too foolish to know." They suggested asking a person from the Western Regions. The emperor thought Dongfang Shuo did not know and found it hard to ask someone else. During the reign of Emperor Ming of the Eastern Han, a Buddhist monk from the Western Regions came to Luoyang. Someone remembered what Dongfang Shuo had said and tried asking about the ash and soot from Emperor Wu's time. The monk said, "The scriptures say, 'When the great calamity of the world is about to end, there will be a conflagration.' This is the aftermath of the conflagration." Thus, it was understood that Dongfang Shuo's words were meaningful.

In Lin Si County, there was a family named Liao, who were long-lived throughout generations. After they moved, their descendants suffered misfortune. Others who lived in their old house also lived long lives for generations. It was then known that the house was responsible for this. It is not known why. It was suspected that the well water was red. They then dug around the well and found dozens of hu of ancient people burying cinnabar. The cinnabar juice seeped into the well, so drinking from it granted longevity. In Jiangdong, it is called "Yu Fu." In the past, when King Helu of Wu was traveling on the river, he ate raw fish slices and had some leftovers, which he discarded into the middle of the river, and they all turned into fish. Now, there are fish in the river called "King Helu's leftover raw fish slices," which are a few inches long, the larger ones like chopsticks, still resembling raw fish slices. Peng (a type of crab). It once communicated dreams to people, calling itself "Changqing." Now, people near the sea often call it "Changqing." In the south, there is a bug named "Yuyu," also known as "Yuzhu," and another name is "Qingfu." It resembles a cicada but is slightly larger, with a spicy and delicious taste, and can be eaten. When it gives birth, it must rely on grass leaves, and the offspring are as large as silkworm eggs. If you take its offspring, the mother will fly over, regardless of distance. Even if you secretly take its offspring, the mother will definitely know where they are. Use the mother's blood to coat eighty-one coins, and use the offspring's blood to coat eighty-one coins. Every time you buy something, either use the mother's coins or the offspring's coins, and they will all return to fly back. They will keep rotating endlessly. Therefore, the technique in the *Huainanzi* uses this to return money, and it is called "Qingfu."

Earth Wasps, Known as "Gua Mo," are also called "Mo Mo" in modern times, and are classified as "slender-waisted" insects. They are all male and do not mate or reproduce; they often take silkworms or locust eggs to raise, which transform into their own offspring. They are also sometimes referred to as "mengling." The *Book of Songs* says, "The mengling has children, and they are carried by the wasp," which is true. Woodworms, which live in wood, transform into butterflies. Hedgehogs have many spines, which prevent them from jumping over willows. Mount Kunlun, known as the "empty mountain," is the head of the earth and the lower capital of the emperor. Beyond it lies the deep waters of the Weak Water, and it is surrounded by the fiery mountains. On the mountain, birds, animals, and plants all grow in the midst of fire; hence there is "fire-washed cloth," which is either made from the bark of the mountain's plants or the fur of its animals. During the Han dynasty, the Western Regions used to offer this cloth, but it had been absent for a long time. By the early Wei dynasty, people doubted its existence. Emperor Wen of Wei believed that fire is harsh and destructive, without life-generating properties, and wrote about this in his *Classic Treatise* to show that it was impossible, thus silencing the wise. When Emperor Ming ascended the throne, he ordered the Three Excellencies to inscribe the *Classic Treatise* on stone outside the temple and at the Imperial Academy, alongside the "Stone Classics," to be shown to future generations. Eventually, envoys from the Western Regions presented "fire-washed cloth" robes, leading to the destruction of the *Classic Treatise*, and people laughed at the emperor's earlier skepticism.

The nature of metal is uniform. It was cast at noon on the day of Bingwu (丙午) in the fifth month to create a yang mirror, which could start a fire; and at midnight on the day of Renzi (壬子) in the eleventh month to create a yin mirror, which could collect water. (This means that casting on Bingwu day creates a yang mirror capable of starting a fire, and casting at midnight on Renzi day creates a yin mirror capable of collecting water.) During the reign of Emperor Ling of Han, Cai Yong from Chenliu repeatedly submitted memorials that offended the emperor's wishes and was further disliked by the emperor's favorites. Fearing he could not escape punishment, he fled to the rivers and seas, distancing himself to Wu and Kuaiji. When he arrived in Wu, there was a Wu person who was burning a paulownia log for cooking. Cai Yong heard the sound of the burning log and said, "This is fine material." He asked for it, had it carved into a zither, and indeed it produced beautiful music. But the tail was charred, so it was named the "Jiaowei Zither." Cai Yong once went to Keting, where he used bamboo for the rafters. He looked up and said, "This is fine bamboo." He took it to make a flute, which produced a clear and resonant sound. Another account says: "Cai Yong told the Wu people, 'I once passed by the Gaoqianting Pavilion in Kuaiji and saw that the sixteenth rafter in the eastern room could be used to make a flute. When I used it, it indeed produced a unique sound.'"

In ancient times, the GaoYang clan had siblings who became husband and wife. The emperor exiled them to the wilds of the KongTong mountains. They embraced each other and died. A divine bird covered them with the herb of immortality. After seven years, they were born as a hermaphrodite, with two heads, four hands, and four feet. This became the MengShuang clan. In the era of the GaoXin clan, there was a woman in the royal palace who suffered from an ear ailment for a long time. A physician treated her by picking out a parasite, which was as large as a silkworm cocoon. After the treatment, the parasite was placed in a gourd container and covered with a plate. Soon, the parasite transformed into a dog with fur of five colors. It was named PanHu and kept as a pet. At that time, the RongWu were powerful and strong, repeatedly invading the borders. The king sent generals to attack but could not capture or defeat them. So, a bounty was offered to anyone who could bring back the head of a RongWu general. The reward was a thousand catties of gold and a fief of ten thousand households, and also a young maiden as a reward. Later, PanHu carried back the head of one and brought it to the king's palace. The king examined it and found it to be indeed a RongWu general. He asked what should be done. All the ministers said, "PanHu is an animal, it cannot be given an official rank, nor can it be married. Even though it has done good, it cannot be rewarded." The young maiden heard this and approached the king, saying, "Your Majesty has already promised me to the world. PanHu brought back the head, eliminating the enemy for the country. This is the will of heaven, not the intelligence of a dog. A king values his words, a hegemon values his trust. One cannot break a clear promise to the world because of a woman's small body. It would be a disaster for the country." The king feared and agreed. He ordered the young maiden to follow PanHu, who took her to the Southern Mountain. The grass and trees were lush, with no signs of human activity.

Thereupon, the woman undressed and tied herself up like a servant, wearing a self-reliant garment. She followed the dog, Pangu, up the mountain, into the valley, and stopped in a cave. The king mourned for her and sent people to look for her, but the heavens were overcast with rain and wind, the mountains trembled, and the clouds darkened, preventing anyone from reaching her. After about three years, she gave birth to six sons and six daughters. When Pangu died, they paired off and became couples. They wove clothes from tree bark and dyed them with plant seeds. They liked colorful clothes, all cut and made with tail shapes. The mother returned and told the king, who sent envoys to bring back all the men and women, and the heavens no longer separated them. Their clothes were tight-fitting, their speech short and simple, they ate and sat in a squatting position, and they liked mountains and disliked cities. The king followed their wishes and gave them famous mountains, vast lakes, and called them the barbarians. These barbarians seemed simple on the outside but were clever inside, loved their land and valued tradition, because they received a different breath from heaven, they were treated with special laws. They farmed and traded without customs barriers, official documents, or taxes. They had settlements, and chiefs were given seals and ribbons. They wore hats made of otter fur because they lived by fishing in the water. These are the people of Lianghan, Bashu, Wuling, Changsha, and Lujiang. They used millet mixed with fish and meat, beat the trough and called out to worship Pangu, and this custom continues to this day. Therefore, the world calls them "Red legs, wide skirts, descendants of Pangu."

The King of Gao Li had a palace servant who became pregnant. The king wanted to kill her. She said, "A ball of air like an egg came down from heaven, so I became pregnant." Later, she gave birth to a son, whom she abandoned in the pigsty. The pigs breathed on him with their snouts; later, when he was moved to the horse stable, the horses also breathed on him. Thus, he survived. The king suspected that he was a heavenly child and ordered his mother to raise him, naming him Dongming. He often ordered him to herd horses. Dongming was skilled in archery, and the king feared he might seize his kingdom, so he wanted to kill him. Dongming fled south to the Shiyin River, striking the water with his bow. Fish and turtles floated to form a bridge, allowing Dongming to cross. When the fish and turtles dispersed, the pursuing soldiers could not cross. He then established his capital in Fuyu. In ancient Guxu, a palace woman gave birth to an egg, which was considered an omen, and she abandoned it by the water. There was a dog named Huaguang, which carried the egg back. Later, it gave birth to a son who became the heir of Guxu. After Huaguang was on the verge of death, it grew horns and had nine tails, and was actually a yellow dragon. It was buried in the village of Guxu, and there is a dog mound there. Dou Bibo's father died early, and he followed his mother back to his father-in-law's house. When he grew up, he had an affair with the daughter of Yunzi and gave birth to a son named Ziwén. Yunzi's wife felt ashamed that the daughter did not marry and gave birth to a child, so she abandoned him in the mountains. Yunzi went hunting and saw a tiger nursing a little boy. He brought the boy back to raise him and married his daughter to Bibo. The people of Chu then called Ziwén "Guwutu," and he served as the chancellor of Chu.

During the reign of Duke Qǐng of Qi, his concubine Xiao Tongshuzi was impregnated, but because she was lowly, she did not dare to speak of it. She gathered firewood and gave birth to Duke Qǐng in the wilderness, and she did not dare to raise him. A weasel nursed him, and a falcon covered him, and people saw and took him, thus they named him Wuyě, who became Duke Qǐng. Yuan (Jinren), a leader of the Qiang people, was captured and made a slave during the Qin period, but later escaped. The Qin people pursued him urgently, and he hid in a cave, which the Qin people burned. A tiger like a shadow came to shield him, thus he did not die. The Qiang people revered him and elevated him to a position of leadership. His descendants later became numerous and powerful. During the Later Han period, Dou Feng, the governor of Dingxiang, had a wife who gave birth to a son named Wu and also gave birth to a snake. Dou Feng sent the snake into the wild, and when Wu grew up, he gained a reputation as a talented person. When his mother died, before the burial, guests gathered, and a large snake came out from the forest and grass, came directly to the coffin, lay down and looked around, hit the coffin with its head, and blood and tears flowed, as if mourning, and after a while, it left. People at the time knew it was a sign of good fortune for the Dou family. During the Yongjia period of Emperor Huai of Jin, there was an elderly woman named Han who saw a large egg in the wild. She took it home and raised it, and got an infant, whom she named Ju'er. At the age of four, Liu Yuan, a Xiongnu leader, attempted to build the city of Píngyáng but did not succeed. He offered rewards to those who could build it. Ju'er responded to the call and transformed into a snake. He ordered the old woman to leave ashes as a marker and told her, "With the help of the ashes, the city can be built quickly." As he said, the city was built. Liu Yuan was surprised and threw the snake into a mountain cave, with part of its tail exposed. The messenger cut it, and suddenly a spring emerged from the cave and formed a pool, which was named the Golden Dragon Pool.

During the Yongchang period of Emperor Yuan, a man named Ren Gu from Jiyang was plowing when he rested under a tree. Suddenly, a person wearing a feathered robe approached him and had a supernatural encounter with him. Afterward, the person disappeared. Ren Gu then became pregnant. After several months, he was about to give birth, and the person in the feathered robe returned, using a knife to cut open his lower body and remove a snake-like child, then left. Ren Gu then became a eunuch, went to the palace to report, and was kept in the palace. According to an old story: In ancient times, there was a man who went on a long journey, leaving no one else in the house except for his daughter. He had a male horse, which the daughter raised herself. Living in a remote and secluded place, she missed her father and playfully said to the horse, "If you can bring my father back, I will marry you." The horse, hearing these words, broke its tether and left, going directly to the father's place. The father saw the horse, was surprised and delighted, and took it to ride. The horse looked back to where it came from and kept crying sadly. The father said, "This horse has no reason to behave this way; is there something wrong at home?" He quickly rode back home. Because the animal had unusual feelings, he fed it very well. However, the horse refused to eat. Every time it saw the girl coming and going, it would become angry and attack. This happened more than once. The father was puzzled and secretly asked the daughter, who told him everything. "It must be because of this," she said. The father replied, "Do not say anything. It might disgrace our family. Do not go out." He then set up a crossbow and shot the horse dead, flaying its skin and displaying it in the courtyard.

A Father Went Out, and His Daughter Played with a Neighbor's Daughter Using a Piece of Leather One day, the father went out, and his daughter played with a neighbor's daughter using a piece of leather. She stomped on it with her foot and said, "You are an animal, and you want to take someone as a wife! You will be slaughtered and skinned, how will you suffer yourself!" Before she finished speaking, the horse's skin suddenly stood up and rolled up the girl, carrying her away. The neighbor's daughter was frightened and helpless, unable to save her. She ran to tell the girl's father. The father returned to search for her, but she had already left and was lost. After several days, they found her on a tree branch. The girl and the horse's skin had both turned into silkworms, spinning silk on the tree. The silk fibers were thick and large, different from ordinary silkworms. A neighbor's wife took them and raised them. The yield was several times greater. Therefore, they named the tree mulberry. Mulberry means mourning. From this, people began to compete to plant mulberry trees, and this is what is cultivated today. The term "mulberry silkworm" refers to descendants of ancient silkworms. According to the *Tian Guan* (Astronomical Official), "the star of the horse is in the position of the Big Dipper." The *Silkworm Book* says, "When the month is in the position of the Big Dipper, then one should bathe the seeds." This indicates that silkworms and horses share a common essence. The *Zhou Li* (Rites of Zhou) states, "Those who raise silkworms are responsible for their cultivation." It is noted, "Nothing can be two great things at once; those who raise silkworms are forbidden because it harms horses." During the Han Dynasty, the empress personally picked mulberry leaves to worship the silk goddess, calling her "Wanyu Woman, Yushi Princess." Princess is a respectful title for women. Wanyu Woman is the first silkworm goddess. Therefore, in modern times, people sometimes refer to silkworms as "daughters," which is an ancient saying.

Yi requested the elixir of immortality from the Queen Mother of the West, and Chang'e stole it and fled to the moon. Before she left, she consulted the diviner You Huang. You Huang divined and said: "It is auspicious. The graceful maiden will fly west alone. She will encounter a dim and hazy sky, but she should not be afraid or alarmed. She will later flourish greatly." Chang'e then resided on the moon, becoming known as the toad (Chánér). The daughter of the emperor of Shetu Mountain died and transformed into a strange grass. Its leaves were lush, its flowers yellow, and its fruit resembled silk from rabbits. Therefore, those who consumed this strange grass always became charming to others. South of Xingyang County, more than a hundred miles away, there is a mountain called Lan Rock Mountain. The mountain is steep and rises a thousand feet, often hosting a pair of white cranes with bright feathers. Day and night, they fly together and gather in pairs. It is said that in the past, a couple lived in this mountain for several hundred years, transforming into two cranes, never ceasing to come and go. One day, one of the cranes was harmed by a person, and the other crane mourns year after year. To this day, the sound echoes through the mountains and valleys, and no one knows how many years have passed. A man from Xinyu County, Yu Province, saw six or seven girls in the fields, all wearing feathered clothing, not knowing they were birds. He crawled forward and obtained one girl's feathered garment, taking it and hiding it. He then went to join the other birds, and they all flew away, leaving one bird behind. The man took the bird as his wife and they had three daughters. Later, the mother sent one of the daughters to ask the father, who revealed that the feathered garment was under a pile of rice. The daughter retrieved it and flew away. Later, she returned to bring back the three daughters, who also flew away.

During the reign of Emperor Ling of Han, the mother of the Huang family from Jiangxia bathed in a basin of water and, after a long time, did not rise, transforming into a large freshwater turtle. The servant girl, frightened, ran to report the incident. By the time the family arrived, the turtle had turned into a deep pool. Afterward, it appeared frequently. Initially, while bathing, she wore a silver hairpin, which remained on its head. As a result, the Huang family refrained from eating turtle meat for generations. In the early years of the Huang period in the Wei dynasty, Song Shizong's mother from Qinghe bathed in the summer in a bathroom, sending all family members out, and remained alone in the room. After a long time, the family did not understand her intentions and peered through a hole in the wall. They saw no human body but a large turtle in the basin of water. They then opened the door, and all entered, completely not continuing as usual. She previously wore a silver hairpin, which was still on her head. They guarded her together, crying helplessly. She intended to leave but could never be kept. They watched for many days, becoming increasingly lax. She herself caught herself outside the door. Her departure was very swift, and they could not catch up, so she entered the water. After several days, she suddenly returned, walking around the house as usual, saying nothing before leaving. People thought Song Shizong should perform the funeral rites; however, he believed that although his mother's form had changed, her life force still existed, and thus he did not perform the funeral rites. This is similar to the case of the mother of the Huang family from Jiangxia.

In the first year of Emperor Sun Hao's reign during the Wu kingdom, in June, on the last day of the lunar month, the mother of Xuan Qian from Danyang, who was eighty years old, transformed into a turtle while bathing. The turtle looked like a yellow one. Xuan Qian and his three brothers closed the door and guarded the area, digging a large pit in the courtyard and pouring water into it. The turtle entered the pit and played. For a day or two, it constantly extended its neck to look outside, waiting for the door to open slightly, and then spun and leaped into the deep water. It never returned. During the Jian'an period of Emperor Xian of Han, a household in Dongjun experienced strange phenomena; without reason, jars and pots made loud noises, as if someone was striking them. Plates and tables in front suddenly disappeared, and chickens laid eggs, but the chicks immediately disappeared. This continued for several years, greatly displeasing the people. They prepared many delicious foods, covered them, and placed them in a room, hiding behind the door to watch. Indeed, the entity returned again, making sounds as before. Hearing this, they closed the door and moved around the room, seeing nothing. They struck it with a stick in the dark. After a long time, they hit something in the corner of the room, hearing groaning sounds, saying, "●! ●!" It should die. Opening the door to see, they found an old man, about a hundred years old, whose words did not make sense, and whose appearance somewhat resembled an animal. They proceeded to investigate and found his home several miles away, where he said, "I have been missing for over ten years." Finding him, they felt a mix of sadness and joy. After a year or so, he disappeared again. They heard that similar strange occurrences happened in Chenliu. People at the time believed it was this old man.

*Search for the Supernatural, Volume Fifteen* During the reign of Qin Shi Huang, there was a man named Wang Dao Ping from Chang'an. In his youth, he fell in love with a girl from his village, the daughter of Tang Shuxie, whose pet name was Fu Yu. She was both beautiful in appearance and character, and they swore to be husband and wife. Soon after, Wang Dao Ping was ordered to go to war and was lost in the southern lands, not returning for nine years. When his parents saw their daughter had grown up, they immediately betrothed her to Liu Xiang. The girl, however, had taken a strong oath with Wang Dao Ping and refused to change her mind. Despite their parents' pressure, she could not avoid being married to Liu Xiang. Three years later, she was miserable and unhappy, constantly thinking of Wang Dao Ping. Her deep resentment and sorrow led her to die in a gloomy state. Three years after her death, Wang Dao Ping returned home and questioned his neighbors, "Where is this girl?" The neighbors replied, "This girl had feelings for you, but she was forced by her parents to marry Liu Xiang, and she has already passed away." Wang Dao Ping asked, "Where is her tomb?" The neighbors led him to the tomb. He cried out in grief, calling her name three times, circling the tomb in sorrow, unable to stop his tears.

Dao Ping then prayed, saying, "I and you swore an oath to heaven and earth, to keep our promise for life. Who would have thought that official matters would entangle us, leading to such separation, causing your parents and Liu Xiang to no longer be in harmony with our original intentions, and to part in life and death. Yet, if you have a spiritual presence, let me see your face as it was in life. If there is no divine intervention, then let us part forever." After he finished speaking, he wept and hesitated. The spirit of the woman emerged from the tomb and asked Dao Ping, "Where have you come from? We have been separated for so long. We swore to be husband and wife, to bind our lives together, but our parents forced me to marry Liu Xiang. It has been three years, and I thought of you day and night, until my hatred led to my death, and we were separated in the netherworld. Yet, I still remember our old feelings and wish to comfort each other again. My body is not damaged, and I can be reborn, to return as husband and wife. Quickly open the tomb, break the coffin, and release me, and I will live." Dao Ping carefully listened to her words and opened the tomb door, touching and examining. Indeed, the woman came back to life. She dressed herself up and followed him back home. Her husband, Liu Xiang, upon hearing of this, was surprised and shocked, and appealed to the local officials. The officials checked the law to decide, but found no relevant law, so they recorded the case and reported it to the king. The king decided to give the woman back to Dao Ping as his wife. She lived to be one hundred and thirty years old. Indeed, sincere and pure-hearted, permeating heaven and earth, she received such a response.

During the reign of Emperor Wu of Jin, in the county of Hejian, there was a man and a woman who had privately agreed to marry. Soon after, the man went to war and did not return for many years. The woman's family then wanted to marry her off to someone else, but she did not wish to go. Her parents forced her, and she went against her will. Soon after, she fell ill and died. When the man returned from his military service, he asked about the woman's whereabouts. Her family told him, and he went to her grave to mourn and express his grief. Overwhelmed by his emotions, he opened the coffin, and the woman immediately revived. He carried her home, nursed her for several days, and she recovered completely as before. The second husband, upon hearing about this, went to ask for her. The man refused to give her back, saying, "Your wife is already dead; how can anyone in the world hear of a dead person coming back to life? This is a gift from heaven to me; she is not your wife anymore." They then sued each other, and the local officials could not decide. The case was sent to the Grand Judge, and the Secretary of the Secretariat, Wang Dao, presented a report stating, "The utmost sincerity moved heaven and earth, therefore, she died and came back to life. This is not an ordinary matter; it cannot be judged by ordinary rules. Request that the man who opened the grave be allowed to keep her." The imperial court followed his suggestion.

During the Jian'an period of Emperor Xian of Han, there was a man from Nanyang named Jia Ou, courtesy name Wenhe, who died of an illness. At that time, there was an official who escorted him to the realm of Mount Tai, where the deity in charge of life and death reviewed the records. The deity said to the official, "This person is supposed to be summoned from a certain prefecture named Wenhe. Why is this person being summoned? Can you quickly send him away?" It was dusk, so they stopped under a tree outside the city to rest. There, Jia Ou saw a young woman walking alone and asked her, "You seem to be a gentleman or scholar. Why are you walking on foot? What is your name?" The woman replied, "I am from the Sanhe region, and my father is currently the magistrate of Yiyang. I was summoned yesterday but returned today. It is now dusk, and I fear being accused of impropriety. I see that you are a gentleman, so I hope you are wise. That is why I stopped, relying on your protection." Jia Ou said, "I am pleased with your heart, and I wish to enjoy each other's company tonight." The woman responded, "I have heard from my aunts that a woman's virtue lies in her chastity and purity." Jia Ou engaged in repeated conversation with her, but she remained steadfast in her resolve. At dawn, they parted ways. Jia Ou had already stayed for two nights, and the funeral preparations were underway. When they looked at his face, it showed color, and when he touched his chest, it was slightly warm. A short while later, he revived. Later, Jia Ou wanted to verify the truth, so he went to Yiyang, prepared a letter, and visited the magistrate. He asked, "Did your daughter die and then revive?" He described the girl's appearance, clothing, words, and the entire story in detail. The magistrate asked the girl, and her answers matched. The magistrate was greatly surprised. In the end, he arranged for this girl to marry Jia Ou.

During the second month of the fourth year of the Jian'an era (199 AD), a woman named Li E from Chong County in Wuling, who was sixty years old, fell ill and died. She was buried outside the city walls, and fourteen days had passed. Li E's neighbor, Cai Zhong, heard that she was wealthy and believed that her burial must contain valuable treasures. He decided to rob her grave, using an axe to open the coffin. As he struck the coffin several times, Li E spoke from within, saying, "Cai Zhong! Protect my head!" Cai Zhong was startled and fled immediately. He was soon spotted by local officials, who arrested him and brought him to trial. According to the law, he was sentenced to be executed in public. Li E's son, upon hearing that his mother had come back to life, came to retrieve her and took her home. The governor of Wuling, upon learning of Li E's miraculous resurrection, summoned her to ask about the details of what had happened. Li E replied, "I heard that I was mistakenly summoned by the God of Life and Death. When I arrived, I was allowed to leave. As I passed by the west gate, I unexpectedly met my distant relative Liu Bo Wen. We were both surprised and greeted each other, and we cried and grieved deeply."

E said, "Bo Wen! One day I was mistakenly summoned, and now I have been sent back. I do not know the way and cannot travel alone. Can you find me a companion? Also, I have been summoned here for over ten days, and my body has been buried by my family. When I return, how will I be able to come out on my own?" Bo Wen said, "I will ask about it." He immediately sent a door guard to consult with the official in charge of the dead: "The deity in charge of life and death mistakenly summoned a woman from Wuling named Li E, and now she has been sent back. Li E has been here for many days, and her body has been buried. How can she come out? Also, she is a weak woman and cannot travel alone. Should she have a companion? She is my cousin, so please arrange it so that she is comfortable." The official replied, "There is a man named Li Hei in the western part of Wuling who has also been sent back. He can be her companion. Also, order Hei to pass by Pian's house and get Li E out of the grave." Thus, Li E was able to come out.

Upon parting with Bo Wen, Bo Wen said, "I have a letter for you to give to Er Tuo." E then returned home with Hei. The situation unfolded as such. The prefect, upon hearing of it, sighed and remarked, "The affairs of the world truly cannot be predicted." He then proposed that, "Although Cai Zhong had to open the tomb due to the influence of spirits; even if he did not wish to, the circumstances left him no choice, and thus he should be pardoned and shown leniency." The imperial edict approved this suggestion. The prefect wished to verify the truth of the words and immediately dispatched a horse officer to the western boundary to interrogate Li Hei. Upon finding him, Li Hei's statements were consistent. The prefect then gave Bo Wen's letter to Er Tuo, who recognized the paper as the one sent by his father at the time of his death. The text of the memorial was still there, but the letter itself was not understandable. He then asked Fei Changfang to read it, who said, "It says to Er Tuo: 'I will accompany the prefect on an inspection tour, at noon on the 8th day of the 8th month, at the water's edge of the southern ditch of Wuling City. You must come at that time.'"

When the time came, all people of all ages were gathered in the south of the city. Soon, they heard the faint sounds of people and horses approaching. They reached the ditch and heard a voice calling out, "He has come! Have you received the letter I sent to Li E?" One responded, "I have received it, so I came here." Baiwen then called out to each family member in turn, and after a long while, he was overcome with grief, saying, "Death and life are different paths; I cannot often hear about you. After I die, your children and grandchildren will be this big!" After a long pause, he told the visitor, "By the following spring, there will be a great illness. Give this pill to you, and apply it to the door and window, and it will ward off evil and illness next year." After finishing speaking, the visitor suddenly left, and they never saw his form. By the following spring, Wuling indeed fell seriously ill and saw ghosts during the day, but only at Baiwen's house did ghosts not dare to approach. Fei Changfang examined the pill and said, "This is the brain of the Fangxiang."

During the Han Dynasty, in Kaocun of Chenliu, there was a woman named Shi Xu, whose courtesy name was Weiming. When she was young, she once fell ill. On the verge of death, she told her mother, "If I die, I will come back to life. Bury me, and place a bamboo staff vertically on the grave. If the staff breaks, dig me up." After her death, she was buried as instructed. Seven days later, when they went to check, the staff had indeed broken. They dug her up, and she had revived. She walked to a well, bathed, and returned to her normal state. Later, she traveled with neighbors by boat to sell shovels in Xiangpi. The shovels did not sell immediately, and she said, "I want to return home." People did not believe her, saying, "How could one return home over a thousand miles in a short time?" She replied, "I will return in one night." She wrote a letter, and it was delivered as proof. Indeed, she returned in one night, and the letter was received. The magistrate of Kaocun, Jia He from Jiangxia, had a sister who was ill in the neighborhood. He wanted to quickly know her condition and asked Shi Xu to check on her. The journey was three thousand miles, but she returned in two nights to report. In Kuaiji, there was a man named He Yu, whose courtesy name was Yanju. He once fell ill and lost consciousness, but his chest remained warm. He died for three days and then revived. He said, "Officials took me to heaven, where I saw government offices and entered a small room. There was a multi-tiered shelf in the room, with a seal on the upper layer and a sword on the middle layer. They told me to take whatever I desired. However, I was too short to reach the upper layer, so I took the sword and left. The officials asked, 'What did you take?' I said, 'I took the sword,' and they replied, 'We could not give you the seal, which can command all spirits, but the sword can only command the village god.'" After his illness healed, a ghost came and claimed to be the village god.

Dai Yang, courtesy name Guoliu, was from Changcheng, Wu County. At the age of twelve, he died from illness but revived five days later. He said, "When I died, Heaven sent its wine store official to give me a talisman, accompanied by officials with flags and banners, and I was about to ascend to Mount Penglai, Kunlun, Jishu, Taishan, Lu, and Heng. Afterward, I was sent back." He had a remarkable ability to interpret omens. Knowing that Wu would fall, he pretended to be ill and did not take office. He returned to his hometown and traveled to Lai Village, passing by the Laozi Temple. These places were the same as those he had seen in his previous life, but he no longer saw the things he had seen before. He then asked the keeper of records, Ying Feng, saying, "About twenty years ago, was there once a person riding a horse eastward who did not dismount when passing by the Laozi Temple? Did he not reach the bridge and fall off the horse, dying?" Ying Feng confirmed that there was such a person. The things he asked about were mostly the same as what Dai Yang had experienced. Wu, from Songyang, Linhai, and Liu Rong, accompanied Wu's chancellor Zhang Ti to Yangzhou. Liu Rong fell ill and died on the ship. Two days later, the soldiers had already gone ashore, and no one buried him. Suddenly, he let out a loud cry, shouting, "Someone is binding the military advisor!" The sound was very forceful. He then revived. People asked him about it. Liu Rong said, "I was a low-ranking official under the North Star, and I saw someone binding Zhang Ti. I was greatly shocked and unconsciously shouted. Why is the military advisor being bound?" The officials under the North Star were angry with Liu Rong, scolded him, and drove him away. Liu Rong was terrified, and the sound of his voice still echoed. On that day, Zhang Ti died in battle. Liu Rong still lived during the reign of Emperor Yuan of Jin.

The wife of Ma Shi, a wealthy woman from Fuyang in the Wu Kingdom, surnamed Jiang, would often fall into a deep trance or sleep for days, during which she would see the death of someone and then awaken. Upon waking, she would fully recount what she had seen. Her family did not believe her, and she told others, saying, "Someone was ill, and I wanted to kill him. Because he was angry and strong, his soul was hard to kill, so he did not die immediately. I entered his house, where there were several types of fish on the shelf. I briefly passed by the stove, playing, when the maid wrongfully offended me. I struck her back, causing her to faint immediately. She remained unconscious for a long time before regaining consciousness." Her brother was also ill, and someone from Wuyi ordered her to kill him. She pleaded with them, but she ultimately did not carry out the order. When she woke up, she told her brother, "You will live." In December of the second year of the Xianning era during the Jin Dynasty, Yan Ji, a man from Langya, fell ill and sought treatment from Zhang Cuo. He died in Zhang's home. The body had already been prepared and placed in a coffin for some time. When the family came to mourn him, the funeral banner would get entangled in trees and could not be untangled. People were deeply moved by this. The mourners suddenly fell down, and the spirit of Yan Ji spoke through them, saying, "My life has not yet come to an end. I only died because I took too many medicines, which harmed my internal organs. I will be reborn now, so do not bury me." Yan Ji's father patted him and prayed, saying, "If you have a destiny, you will be reborn. Isn't this what family members wish? Now we just want to bring you home, not bury you." The banner then untangled. When they returned home, Yan Ji's wife dreamed of him and said, "I will be reborn, so open the coffin quickly." She told her family about the dream. That night, both Yan Ji's mother and the rest of the family also dreamed of him.

Upon hearing that they wanted to open the coffin, the father refused to allow it. His younger brother, Han, who was still young, then spoke resolutely, saying, "Extraordinary events have occurred throughout history; today, we have such a miraculous occurrence. Is the pain of opening the coffin worse than not opening it and bearing the guilt?" The parents agreed with him. They then jointly opened the coffin, and indeed, there was a sign of life. He scratched the coffin with his hand, injuring his fingers and nails, but his breathing was very faint, and it was unclear whether he was alive or dead. They quickly poured a small amount of liquid into his mouth with a cotton thread, and he was able to swallow. Thus, they brought him out. They took care of him for several months, and he ate and drank a little more. He was able to open his eyes and look around, bending and straightening his limbs, but he did not interact with people and could not speak. His needs for food and drink were communicated through dreams. Like this, it lasted for over ten years. The family was exhausted from caring for him and could no longer manage household affairs. Han then abandoned worldly affairs and personally took care of him, becoming well-known in the local area. Later, he became weaker and weaker, and finally, he died again.

Yang Hu, at the age of five, ordered his wet nurse to fetch the gold ring he had been playing with. The wet nurse said, "You did not have this object before." Yang Hu then went to the eastern wall of the mulberry tree in the courtyard of his neighbor Li and found it there. The owner was astonished and said, "This was an object lost by my deceased son; how did you take it away?" The wet nurse explained the matter in detail. Li's family was deeply moved. At the time, people were amazed by this. At the end of the Han dynasty, when the region of Guanzhong was in great turmoil, someone opened a tomb of a Han imperial concubine and found her still alive. After she was brought out, she returned to normal as if nothing had happened. The Wei empress Guo cherished her and recorded her in the palace, always keeping her close by. She would ask about matters in the Han palace, and she spoke about them clearly, with everything in order. When the empress Guo passed away, the concubine cried so bitterly that she died from grief. During the Wei dynasty, in Taiyuan, a tomb was opened, and the coffin was broken open. Inside was a living woman. When she was brought out, she was spoken to and confirmed to be alive. She was sent to the capital, where they asked her about her past, but she did not know. When they looked at the trees on the tomb, they were about thirty years old, so it was unclear whether this woman had been alive for thirty years underground or if she had suddenly come to life and coincidentally met the tomb robbers. In the Jin dynasty, Du Xi, whose courtesy name was Shigao, had a servant buried with him by mistake. After more than ten years, when the tomb was opened for a secondary burial, the servant was still alive. She said, "At first, I was as if asleep. After a while, I gradually regained consciousness." When asked, she said, "I thought I would stay there for a night or two." When the servant was first buried, she was around fifteen or sixteen years old, and after the tomb was opened, her appearance remained unchanged. She lived for another fifteen or sixteen years, was married, and had children.

Emperor Huan's noblewoman, Empress Feng, died of illness. During Emperor Ling's reign, bandits desecrated her tomb, which had been undisturbed for over seventy years. Her body remained well-preserved, with only a slight chill in the flesh. The bandits engaged in immoral acts with her, leading to internal conflict and mutual killings before their crimes were discovered. Later, when the family of Empress Dowager Dou was executed, there was a plan to have Empress Feng's remains accompany those of Chen Gongda of Xiapi in the afterlife. However, it was argued that although Empress Feng had been favored by the previous emperor, her body had been defiled by the bandits and was thus inappropriate to accompany the highest-ranking individuals in the afterlife. Instead, Empress Dowager Dou's remains were chosen for this honor. During the reign of Sun Quan's son, Sun Shao, military officers in Guangling dug up various tombs, taking materials to build the city, causing significant damage. They later discovered a large tomb with a multi-story structure inside, featuring doors and windows that could rotate and open/close. The tomb had surrounding pathways suitable for vehicles and was high enough to ride horses. Inside, dozens of bronze figures, each about five feet tall, wore large hats, red clothing, held swords, and stood in rows. The spirit seats were carved with bronze figures, and the back of the stone walls bore inscriptions reading "General of the Palace," "Attendant," or "Attendant-in-Attendance." The tomb seemed to belong to a high-ranking noble. When the coffin was broken open, a person with white hair was found inside, dressed in bright and clean clothes, with a face and body that looked like a living person. Inside the coffin was a layer of mica, about a foot thick, and thirty pieces of white jade discs were placed around the body. Soldiers used a cart to lift out the dead body and leaned it against the tomb wall. A jade piece, about a foot long and shaped like a winter melon, slipped out of the dead person's chest and fell to the ground. Gold pieces, about the size of dates, were found in the ears and nostrils.

The King of Guangchuan had a fondness for opening tombs. When he opened the tomb of Luan Shu, all the coffins, caskets, and ritual vessels were completely ruined and rotten, leaving nothing behind. There was only one white fox that started and ran away when it saw people. The attendants chased it but failed to catch it, and they injured its left foot with a halberd. That night, the king dreamed of a man with all-white beard and eyebrows who came to him and said, "Why did you injure my left foot?" Then he tapped the king's left foot with a stick. When the king woke up, his foot was swollen and painful, and he immediately developed sores, which did not heal until his death. In the sixteenth volume of *Soushenji* (Records of Searching for the Supernatural): Formerly, Emperor Zhuanxu had three sons who died and became plague spirits: one resided in the Yangtze River and became a malaria spirit; one resided in the River Luo and became a ghost of the dark; one resided in human palaces and was good at startling children, becoming a little ghost. Therefore, on the first day of the year, officials were ordered to lead the ritual of exorcism to drive away plague spirits. Funeral songs are the music played at funerals, and they are the harmonized sounds of those who hold the funeral ropes. There are two chapters in the funeral songs: "Allium Leaves and Dew" and "Hemp Fields," written by the disciples of Tianheng of the Han dynasty. When Tianheng killed himself, his disciples mourned him and sang mournfully, saying: "People are like dew on Allium leaves, easily evaporate and disappear; also, people die, their spirits return to the hemp fields." Therefore, there are two chapters.

阮瞻，字千里，素执无鬼论。物莫能难。每自谓，此理足以辨正幽明。忽有客通名诣瞻，寒温毕，聊谈名理。客甚有才辨，瞻与之言，良久，及鬼神之事，反复甚苦。客遂屈，乃作色曰：“鬼神，古今圣贤所共传，君何得独言无？即仆便是鬼。”于是变为异形，须臾消灭。瞻默然，意色太恶。岁余，病卒。 吴兴施续为寻阳督，能言论，有门生亦有理意，常秉无鬼论。忽有一黑衣白袷客来，与共语，遂及鬼神。移日，客辞屈。乃曰：“君辞巧，理不足。仆即是鬼。何以云无；问：“鬼何以来？”答曰：“受使来取君。期尽明日食时。”门生请乞，酸苦，鬼问：“有人似君者否？”门生云：“施续帐下都督，与仆相似。”便与俱往，与都督对坐；鬼手中出一铁凿，可尺余，安着都督头，便举椎打之。都督云：“头觉微痛。”向来转剧，食顷，便亡。 阮瞻, courtesy name Qianli, had always held the view that there were no ghosts. No one could refute it. He often said that this reasoning was sufficient to distinguish between the visible and the invisible. Suddenly, a guest came to visit him, they exchanged greetings, and began discussing the principles of names and principles. The guest was very skilled in debate, and after a long conversation, they discussed the matter of ghosts and spirits, which was intensely debated. The guest eventually conceded and changed his expression, saying, "Ghosts and spirits are commonly believed by ancient and modern sages; how can you alone say there are none? Then I am a ghost." He then transformed into an unusual form and disappeared in an instant. 阮瞻 remained silent, his expression becoming very displeased. After a year, he died of illness. 施续 from Wu County served as the supervisor of Xunyang, skilled in debate and discussion, and had a student who also held the view that there were no ghosts. Suddenly, a guest wearing a black robe and white undergarment came to visit him, and they began talking, eventually discussing the matter of ghosts and spirits. After a day, the guest conceded. He said, "Your words are clever, but your reasoning is insufficient. I am a ghost. How can you say there are none? You asked, 'Why did the ghost come?' The guest replied, 'I was sent to take you. I will finish by tomorrow at mealtime.' The student pleaded, feeling bitter, and the ghost asked, 'Is there anyone who resembles you?' The student said, 'There is a deputy under Supervisor Shixu, who resembles me.' The ghost then went with them, sat opposite the deputy, and took out an iron chisel about a foot long, placed it on the deputy's head, and struck it with a mallet. The deputy said, 'My head feels slightly painful.' It gradually became more severe, and after an hour, he died.

Jiang Ji, courtesy name Zi Tong, was from Ping’a in the state of Chu. He served in the state of Wei and held the position of General of the Army. His wife dreamed of her deceased son, who wept and said, “Life and death are different paths. When I was alive, I was the descendant of a high minister; now, in the underworld, I serve as a minor official under Mount Tai, suffering greatly and in misery, beyond words to describe. Now, a singer named Sun A from west of the Imperial Ancestral Temple has been summoned to serve as the magistrate of Mount Tai. I beg you, Mother, please tell Lord Jiang on my behalf, entrust him to Sun A, so that I may be transferred to a place of happiness.” After speaking, the mother suddenly awoke in alarm. The next day, she told Jiang about the dream. Jiang said, “Dreams are merely illusions, nothing to be surprised at.” That evening, she dreamed again, saying, “I have come to welcome the new magistrate, and I have just arrived at the temple, before he departs. I have only a moment to return. The new magistrate will depart at noon tomorrow. At the time of departure, there are many matters to attend to, and I will not be able to return. I must say goodbye forever here. Lord Jiang’s nature is strong and difficult to move with emotion, so I have come to plead with you, Mother. Why not at least try it once?” Then he described Sun A’s appearance in great detail.

At dawn, the mother urged Ji again: "Though it is not strange to dream, why is this so comfortable? Why not just test it once?" Ji then sent someone to the Imperial Ancestral Temple to investigate Sun A. They indeed found him, and his appearance matched the son's description exactly. Ji wept and said, "I almost let my son down." Thus, he met Sun A and told him everything. Sun A did not fear death but was happy to become the magistrate of Mount Tai, only worried that Ji might not believe him. He said, "If it is as you say, it is what I wish for. I do not know what position your honorable son wants." Ji replied, "Grant him any position he desires in the underworld." Sun A said, "I will follow your instructions." Ji then rewarded him generously. After finishing the conversation, he was sent back. Ji wanted to quickly know the outcome, so he placed a messenger every ten steps from the commander's gate to the temple to relay messages. At the hour of Chen, they reported that Sun A had a heartache; at the hour of Yi, they reported that Sun A was in severe pain; at noon, they reported that Sun A had died. Ji said, "Although I grieve for my son's misfortune, I am also glad that the deceased has consciousness." A month later, the son returned and told his mother, "I have already been promoted to the position of recorder." In the Han dynasty, there was a city called Guzhu in Buxi County, which was the ancient capital of the state of Guzhu. In the first year of the Guanghe era during Emperor Ling's reign, a man from Liaoxi saw a floating coffin in the Liao River and wanted to chop it open. The person in the coffin said, "I am the younger brother of Bo Yi, the ruler of Guzhu. The sea has damaged my coffin, so it has drifted here. Why would you chop me?" The man was frightened and did not dare to chop it. Therefore, they built a temple to worship him. Officials and commoners who wanted to open and inspect the coffin all died without apparent illness.

Wen Xu, courtesy name Gongci, was from Taiyuan in the state of Qi. He served as a military officer and traveled to Longxi, where he was captured by a general of Wei Xiu, who wanted to force him to surrender alive. Wen Xu was furious and struck the captors with his staff, killing some of them. The captors rushed to kill Wen Xu, but Xun Yu intervened and said, "A righteous man wishes to die for his principles." He was given a sword and ordered to commit suicide. Wen Xu accepted the sword, held his mustache in his mouth, and sighed, "Then let my mustache soil the earth." He then fell on the sword and died. Emperor Gengshi pitied him and sent his body to be buried near Luoyang, where a tomb was built for him. His eldest son, Wen Shou, who was the Marquis of Yinping, dreamed that Wen Xu told him, "I have been a long-term guest and miss my hometown." Wen Shou immediately resigned his position, wrote a petition to the emperor asking to be allowed to return and bury his father, and the emperor granted his request. In another account, Han Wen Ying, courtesy name Shuchang, was from Nanyang and served as a county magistrate in Ganling during the Jian'an period. One night, he stayed overnight outside the city boundary and, at the third watch of the night, dreamed of a person kneeling before him and saying, "My ancestors buried me here, but the water has washed away half of the tomb, and the coffin has sunk. There is no way for me to stay warm. I heard that you are here, so I have come to rely on you. I would like to ask you to move me to a higher, drier place for a short while tomorrow." The ghost showed Wen Ying his wet clothes. Wen Ying was deeply moved and woke up. He told his attendants, "The dream was unreal; what's there to be surprised about?"

Ying returned to sleep in the place where he was about to sleep. In his dream, he heard someone say to him, "I told you of my poverty and suffering, why don't you pity me?" Ying, still in his dream, asked, "Who are you?" The voice replied, "I was originally from Zhao, but now I belong to the god of the Wamang people." Ying asked, "Where is your coffin now?" The voice answered, "It is near your tent, northward by a dozen steps, beside a dead willow tree by the water, that is me. It will soon be dawn, and I won't be able to see you again, so you must remember me." Ying responded, "Yes!" Suddenly, he woke up. At dawn, as they were about to set out, Ying said, "Even though it's a dream, it's not unusual, but why is it so strange?" Those around him said, "Why not spend a little time to verify it?"
