Ying immediately got up, led a dozen people upstream, and indeed found a dead willow tree. He said, "That's it." They dug underneath, and in a short while, they indeed found the coffin. The coffin was very rotten and half-submerged in water. Ying told those around him, "What I heard from others, they said it was false; what is passed down by the common people cannot be without verification." They moved the coffin, buried it, and left.

During the Han Dynasty, He Chang of Jiujiang served as the governor of Jiaozhou. On his inspection tour, he arrived at Gao'an County in the苍梧郡 region and spent the night at the鹄奔亭 inn. It was still early in the night when a woman emerged from downstairs and called out: "I am Su E, also known as Shi Zhu, originally from Xiu Village in Guangxin County. I lost my parents at a young age and had no brothers. I married a man named Shi from the same county, but he died young. I have a hundred and twenty bolts of assorted silk fabrics and a maid named Zengfu. Being poor, weak, and destitute, I could not support myself. I wished to sell the fabrics in a neighboring county, so I rented a cart from a man named Wang Bo from the same county. The cart cost 12,000 coins, and I loaded myself and the fabrics onto it, having Zengfu hold the reins. We arrived at this inn on April 10th of the previous year. By then, it was already late in the day, and there were no more travelers. We dared not go further, so we stayed there. Suddenly, Zengfu suffered from severe abdominal pain.

A woman visited the residence of a local official to request water and fire. The official, named Gong Shou, wielding a spear and a halberd, approached the carriage and asked her, "Madam, where do you come from? What is in your carriage? Where is your husband? Why are you traveling alone?" The woman replied, "Why bother asking?" Shou then grabbed her arm and said, "Young men love beauty and hope to be pleased." The woman was terrified and refused, but Shou immediately drew a knife and stabbed her in the side, causing her to die instantly. He then stabbed another person, also causing them to die. Shou dug a pit beneath the carriage, buried the woman and her servant together, with the woman below and the servant above, and took their belongings. He then slaughtered a cow, burned the carriage, and stored the carriage parts and cow bones in a well in the east of the pavilion. The woman had died unjustly, and her pain moved the heavens. Having nowhere to appeal, she came to seek justice from the wise official. The official asked, "If we want to exhume your body, what will be the proof?" The woman replied, "I wore white clothes and had silk shoes, which are still intact. I wish to visit my hometown and return my bones to my deceased husband." The officials exhumed the bodies, and indeed, the descriptions matched. The official then returned quickly, sent officials to capture the perpetrators, interrogated them, and they confessed. The officials in Guangxin County verified the statements, and they matched those of the woman. The parents and brothers of the perpetrator were all captured and imprisoned. The official petitioned for the perpetrator, stating that according to the law, killing does not warrant collective punishment. However, the perpetrator was the leader of the crime, kept hidden for years, and thus could not escape the law. Let the ghosts and spirits who have appealed, who are rare in a thousand years, all be executed to demonstrate the power of the spirits and to assist in the divine punishment. The emperor approved the request.

There is a large ship at the mouth of the Ru River. When the ship overturned in the water, it became visible when the water level was low. The elders say, "This is Cao Cao's ship." Once, a fisherman spent the night beside it, tying his boat to it. He only heard the sounds of flutes, pipes, and singing, along with a pleasant fragrance, which was unusual. The fisherman finally fell asleep and dreamt of someone driving him away, saying, "Do not approach the official courtesans." It is said that Cao Cao carried courtesans on the ship, which sank here, and it remains here to this day.

Xiahou Kai, courtesy name Wan Ren, died of illness. A clan member named Gou Nu, who often saw ghosts, saw Kai appear multiple times, wanting to take a horse and harm his wife. He wore a simple head covering and a plain robe, entered the bed in the west wall of the house where he used to live, and approached people to ask for tea. Zhong Wu had a daughter named Xian Ai, who married Mi Yuanzong. She died during childbirth at home. According to custom, those who die in childbirth are marked with ink on their faces. Her mother did not want to do it, so Zhong Wu secretly marked her own face, and no one saw her. Yuanzong, as the county magistrate of Shixin, dreamed of his wife coming, climbing onto the bed, and clearly seeing her face, which had black marks. During the Jin Dynasty, Xincai Wang Zhaoping had a small cart in the main hall. At night, it entered the study room without reason and exited after hitting the wall. Later, he frequently heard shouting and attacking sounds coming from all directions. Zhaoping gathered people to set up bows and arrows for defense, pointing and shooting at the sounds. The ghosts caught the arrows, and they fell into the ground. In the Wu Kingdom, during the third year of the Xichu era, a man from Juchang named Yang Du traveled to Yuyao. One night, he met a young man holding a pipa, asking for a ride. Du accepted the request. The young man played the pipa for many songs. After the songs ended, he stuck out his tongue and opened his eyes wide, scaring Du and leaving. Du continued for about twenty miles and saw an old man who said his name was Wang Jie. Du let him ride again. The old man said, "The ghost plays the pipa very mournfully." Wang Jie replied, "I can play too," and was the same ghost. He opened his eyes and stuck out his tongue again, terrifying Du almost to death.

Lángyá Qín Jùbó, aged sixty, once traveled at night, drinking wine. On his way, he passed by Pénghuān Temple. Suddenly, he saw his two grandsons welcoming him. They supported him for more than a hundred steps, then grabbed Jùbó's neck and pressed him to the ground, scolding, "Old slave! You beat me on a certain day, and now I will kill you." Jùbó thought, only on a certain time did he believe he beat this grandson. Jùbó then pretended to be dead, and they left him. Jùbó returned home, intending to punish his two grandsons. The grandsons were surprised and grieved, knocking their heads and saying, "For descendants, how could there be such a thing? Fearing it might be a ghost or demon, begging to try again." Jùbó understood, and after several days, he pretended to be drunk and walked past the temple. He saw his two grandsons coming to support him again. Jùbó then quickly held them, and the ghosts could not move. When he arrived home, it turned out to be two people. Jùbó lit a fire and roasted them, their backs and bellies were both burned and cracked. He took them out and placed them in the courtyard, and at night they all fled. Jùbó could not bear not to kill them. After a month or so, he again pretended to be drunk and left with a knife, the family did not know. He did not return until very late at night. His grandsons feared they would be trapped again by this ghost, so they went together to meet Jùbó, and Jùbó finally stabbed and killed them. During the reign of Emperor Wu of Han, a man from Dōnglái named Chí, whose family frequently made wine, saw three strange guests one day. They brought cooked rice and asked for their wine to drink. They drank and then left. After a while, someone came and said, "Saw three ghosts drunk in the forest." Wú Xiānzhǔ killed the Wǔwèi soldier Qián Xiǎoxiǎo, and his spirit appeared on the main street. He looked for a hired man named Wú Yǒng, asking Yǒng to deliver a message to the temple on the south side of the street, borrowing two wooden horses, and sprinkled wine on them, turning them into good horses with saddles and bridles complete.

Nanyang's Song Dingbo, when young, was walking at night and encountered a ghost. He asked the ghost, "Who are you?" The ghost replied, "I am a ghost." The ghost then asked, "And who are you?" Dingbo deceived the ghost, saying, "I am also a ghost." The ghost asked, "Where do you wish to go?" Dingbo replied, "I wish to go to the market in Wan." The ghost said, "I also wish to go to the market in Wan." They then proceeded together. After walking a few miles, the ghost said, "Walking is too slow; we can take turns carrying each other, what do you think?" Dingbo said, "That's a great idea." The ghost then carried Dingbo for several miles. The ghost said, "You are too heavy; are you not a ghost?" Dingbo replied, "I am a new ghost, so my body is heavier." Dingbo then carried the ghost, and the ghost seemed to have no weight at all. They repeated this exchange several times. Dingbo again said, "I am a new ghost, and I don't know what I should be afraid of." The ghost replied, "I just don't like human spittle." They continued on their way. When they came across water, Dingbo told the ghost to cross first and listened carefully, but heard no sound. When Dingbo crossed the water, it made a splashing noise. The ghost asked, "Why is there a sound?" Dingbo said, "I am newly dead and not used to crossing water, so don't be surprised by me." As they were nearing the market in Wan, Dingbo suddenly picked up the ghost and placed it on his shoulder, quickly seizing it. The ghost let out a loud cry, sounding like "Zaza," and begged to be put down, but Dingbo did not listen. He carried the ghost directly to the market in Wan and put it down on the ground, where it transformed into a sheep. Dingbo then sold the sheep, fearing it might change again, and spat on it. He received one thousand five hundred coins and left. At that time, Shi Chong said, "Dingbo sold a ghost and received one thousand five hundred coins."

King Fuchai of Wu had a daughter named Ziyu, who was eighteen years old and both talented and beautiful. A young man named Han Zhong, aged nineteen, possessed Daoist techniques, and Princess Ziyu was fond of him. They secretly exchanged letters and agreed to marry. Han Zhong studied in the states of Qi and Lu. Before he left, he instructed his parents to propose marriage to the princess. King Fuchai became furious and refused the proposal. Princess Ziyu died of a broken heart and was buried outside the Chuan Gate. Three years later, Han Zhong returned and questioned his parents. They replied, "The king was very angry, and Princess Ziyu died of grief. She has already been buried." Han Zhong cried bitterly and prepared sacrificial offerings to mourn at her grave. The spirit of Princess Ziyu emerged from the tomb, saw Han Zhong, and wept, saying, "After you left, I asked my parents to seek your hand from the king, hoping we would fulfill our wish. I did not expect that after our separation, I would meet such a fate. What can I do?" Princess Ziyu then turned her head to the left and sang, "There is a bird in the south mountain, and a net in the north mountain. The bird has already flown high, what can the net do? I wish to follow you, but there are many slanderous words. Grief has caused me to fall ill, and I have died in the yellow earth. My fate was not kind, and I am full of grievances. The leader of the birds is called the phoenix. Losing the male one day, I grieve for three years. Even though there are many birds, they cannot form a pair. Thus, I see my humble self, and I meet your radiance. We are physically far apart, but close in heart. When will we temporarily forget this sorrow?" After finishing her song, she sighed and wept, inviting Han Zhong back to the tomb.

Zhong replied, "Life and death are different paths, and I fear I have committed a grave fault, so I dare not accept your command." Jade said, "Life and death are different paths, and I also know this; however, this parting will be forever, with no more meetings in the future. Are you afraid that I will become a ghost and bring disaster upon you? If you truly wish to honor me, why wouldn't you trust me?" Moved by her words, Zhong sent her back to her tomb. Jade entertained him with wine and music, and they spent three days and nights together, observing the rites of a married couple. As he was leaving, she gave him a pearl as large as a thumb and said, "Having destroyed my reputation and cut off my wishes, what more can I say? Take care of yourself in due time. If you come to my home, pay respects to the great king." After Zhong left, he went to the king to tell him about the matter. The king was furious and said, "My daughter is already dead, yet Zhong has spread false rumors to defile her spirit. This is nothing more than grave robbing, pretending to be guided by ghosts." He ordered Zhong to be arrested. Zhong fled, and when he reached Jade's tomb, he told her what had happened. Jade said, "Do not worry. I will return and explain to the king." The king, after dressing and combing his hair, suddenly saw Jade and was shocked, filled with both sorrow and joy. He asked, "Why have you come back to life?" Jade knelt and said, "In the past, the scholar Han Zhong came to seek me, but the great king did not allow it. My reputation was ruined, and our bond was severed, leading to my own death. Now that Zhong has returned from afar and heard that I am dead, he brought offerings to my tomb to mourn me. Moved by his sincerity, I met him and gave him the pearl, not for grave robbing. I hope you will not pursue this matter." The lady heard this and came out to embrace her. Jade then disappeared like smoke.

Xin Dao Du from Longxi, while traveling for study to within four or five miles of Yongzhou city, saw a large house nearby, with a maid in green clothes at the door. Dao Du went to the door to ask for food. The maid went inside to inform Qin's daughter, who ordered her to be called in. Dao Du hurried into the inner chamber, where Qin's daughter was sitting on the western couch. Dao Du introduced his name and discussed daily routines, after which she ordered him to sit on the eastern couch. She immediately prepared food and drink. After eating, Qin's daughter said to Dao Du, "I am the daughter of King Qin Min of Qin, who was betrothed to the state of Cao but unfortunately died without a husband. I have been dead for twenty-three years and have lived alone in this house. Today you have come, and I wish to be your wife for three nights." Three days later, Qin's daughter said to herself, "You are a living person, and I am a ghost. Our meeting is for three nights only, and I cannot stay long, as it will bring disaster. But since we have spent these two nights together, and now we must part, what shall I give you as a token of our bond?" She then ordered a box from behind the bed to be opened, took out a golden pillow, and gave it to Dao Du as a token.

They parted with tears, and immediately a maid was sent to see him out. No sooner had he taken a few steps than the house was no longer visible, and only a mound remained. At the time, Du had fled in a state of panic, but upon checking, he saw that the golden pillow was still in his possession, and there was no strange change. Eventually he arrived in the State of Qin, where he sold the pillow in the market. Just then, the Queen of Qin was traveling eastward and personally saw Du selling the golden pillow. She suspected something and asked to see it. She questioned Du as to where he had obtained it, and he fully explained the story. Upon hearing it, the queen wept bitterly, unable to control herself. Yet, she had only suspected it before, so she sent people to open the grave and examine the coffin. All the original burial items were there, but the pillow was missing. When she examined the body, she found that the affection between the deceased and the living was as real as ever. Only then did the Queen of Qin believe him. She sighed and said, "My daughter was a great sage, and she has been dead for twenty-three years, yet she could still interact with the living. This is my true son-in-law." Thus, she conferred upon Du the title of *驸马都尉* (驸马 Commandant), granted him gold, silk, carriages, and horses, and ordered him to return to his native land. From this time onward, later generations came to call a son-in-law *驸马*; even today's national son-in-law is also called *驸马*.

During the Han dynasty, there was a man named Tan who was forty years old and without a wife. He often moved by emotion while reading the *Book of Songs*. One night, a woman appeared, about fifteen or sixteen years old, with an appearance and attire unparalleled in the world. She came to Tan and proposed marriage, saying, "I am different from others; do not use fire to see me. After three years, you may see me." They lived together as husband and wife, and she gave birth to a son who was already two years old. Unable to bear it, one night, after she had fallen asleep, he secretly lit a fire to see her. Above her waist, she had flesh like a human, but below her waist, there were only dry bones. The woman awoke and said, "You have wronged me. I was almost complete, why could you not wait one more year before seeing me?" Tan begged for forgiveness and wept uncontrollably. She said, "Although we must part forever, I will take my son with me if he cannot live on his own. I will leave you something." Tan followed her, and they entered a magnificent hall, where the rooms and objects were extraordinary. She gave him a pearl robe, saying, "It will provide for you." She tore a piece of Tan's clothing and left it behind. Later, Tan took the robe to the market, where it was bought by the family of the Duke of Suixiang, and he received a million coins. The duke recognized it and said, "This is my daughter's robe. How could it be in the market? This must be a grave robbery." He then had him interrogated. Tan told him everything. The duke still did not believe him, so he inspected the tomb of his daughter, which was intact. When he opened the coffin, he found a piece of her clothing. He called his son to look, and it was indeed his daughter. The duke then believed him. He immediately summoned Tan, gave him gifts, and made him his son-in-law. He made his son an official.

Lu Chong, a man from Fan Yang, lived thirty li west of his home, where there was the tomb of Cui the County Magistrate. When Lu Chong was twenty years old, one day before the Winter Solstice, he went out to the west of his house to hunt and play. He saw a deer and raised his bow to shoot it, hitting the deer. The deer fell but then got up again. Lu Chong chased after it and, without realizing how far he had gone, suddenly saw, about one li north of the road, a tall gate and tiled house, surrounded by what looked like official residences. He no longer saw the deer. A bell in the gate announced guests to come forward. Lu Chong asked, "What kind of office is this?" The response was, "This is the office of the County Magistrate." Lu Chong said, "My clothes are shabby; how can I meet the County Magistrate?" Immediately, a person brought a bundle of new clothes and said, "The County Magistrate has sent these for you." Lu Chong put on the clothes and went inside to see the County Magistrate. He introduced himself. They drank and ate several rounds. The County Magistrate said to Lu Chong, "Your esteemed father did not consider my humble and lowly family, and recently received a letter asking for your hand in marriage for my daughter, so I have come to welcome you." He then showed Lu Chong the letter. Lu Chong, though young when his father died, still recognized his father's handwriting and sighed, unable to refuse any longer.

Immediately, the inner chamber was ordered: "The Lu scholar has arrived; can the young lady be asked to dress up?" And Chong was told: "You may go to the east corridor until dusk." The inner chamber reported: "The young lady has finished dressing up." Chong then went to the east corridor, where the young lady had already gotten off the carriage, stood at the head of the seat, and they bowed to each other. For three days, food was provided, and after three days, Cui said to Chong: "You may return now. The young lady has signs of pregnancy; if she gives birth to a boy, I will return him, so do not doubt. If she gives birth to a girl, she will be kept and raised by me." The outer chamber was ordered to prepare the carriage to send the guest. Chong then took his leave. Cui saw him off to the middle gate, holding hands and shedding tears. Upon leaving, he saw a small carriage driven by a servant in green, and also saw his original clothes and bow and arrow, still outside. Soon after, a messenger brought a person who held a bundle of clothes for Chong, and they said: "Our marriage alliance has just begun, and parting is very regrettable. Now I am sending another set of clothes and bedding for you." Chong got on the carriage, and left like lightning, arriving home in a short while. The family met, and they were both sad and happy, asking questions. They realized that Cui was a deceased person and entered her tomb, feeling regret and sorrow.

After four years of separation, on the third day of the third month, Chong was playing by the water when he suddenly saw two ox-drawn carts by the water, which were suddenly sinking and floating. Then they approached the shore, and all those present saw them. Chong went to open the back door of the cart and saw the Cui family woman and a three-year-old boy together. Chong saw her and was glad to take her hand, but the woman raised her finger and pointed to the rear cart, saying, "The official sees people." Then Chong saw the Lesser Official. Chong went to greet them, and the woman took the child back. Chong then gave her a gold bowl and also gave her a poem, saying: "Shining like a divine mushroom, how splendid and graceful! Beautiful and prominent at the time, wonderful and extraordinary, showing supernatural qualities. Holding beauty but not yet blooming, in midsummer suffering from frost and withering. Glory long fades into obscurity, the world's path forever has no place for it. Not understanding the workings of yin and yang, wise person suddenly appears. Brief encounter and quick separation, all due to spirits and deities. What to give as a gift to my dear ones, a gold bowl can nourish the child. Love and affection part from here, heartbroken and grieving." Chong took the child, the bowl, and the poem, and suddenly the two carts disappeared. Chong took the child back, and all present thought it was a ghost or demon, spitting at it from a distance. The child remained in the same form. Chong asked the child, "Who is your father?" The child immediately went into Chong's arms.

Initially, the crowd was surprised and repulsed, passing around and reading the poem, moved to sigh at the profound understanding of life and death. Chong then rode his carriage into the market, selling a coffin, setting a high price, not wanting to sell quickly, hoping someone would recognize it. Suddenly, an old servant recognized this, went back to inform the mistress, saying, "In the market, I saw a person riding in a carriage, selling a coffin from Lady Cui's daughter." The mistress, who is Lady Cui's aunt, sent her son to see it, and it was exactly as the servant had said. He got on the carriage, exchanged names, and told Chong, "My aunt married a minor official, had a daughter who died before marriage. The family mourned deeply, giving a golden coffin, placed in the coffin. Can you tell me the story of the coffin?" Chong recounted the story. This child also wept. He returned with the news to his mother, and the mother then ordered him to visit Chong's house, to welcome the child and see her. All the relatives gathered. The child resembled Lady Cui, and also resembled Chong's appearance. Both the child and the coffin were verified. The aunt said, "My nephew was born in late March. His father named him Chun, meaning warmth. Hoping he would be strong." So he was named Wenxiu. Wenxiu, it seems, was a secret marriage, and the signs were already evident. The child grew up to be a fine person, serving as a county magistrate and holding a high rank. His descendants continued in high office. One of his descendants, named Zigan, was famous throughout the world.

During the Later Han period, in the western gatehouse of Ruyang, Ru'an, there were ghosts and spirits that haunted the place. Whenever guests stayed overnight, they would invariably die. Those who were afflicted by the haunting would lose their hair and suffer from a loss of vital energy. Upon investigating the cause, it was said: "Previously, there were already strange phenomena. Later, when the prefectural official, Zheng Qi of Yilu, arrived, about six or seven miles away from the gatehouse, a beautiful woman appeared and asked to be carried along. Qi initially hesitated but then got on the carriage, entered the gatehouse, and hurried downstairs. The inn attendant reported: 'The upper floor cannot be entered.' Qi replied: 'I am not afraid.' It was already evening, so he went upstairs and spent the night with the woman. Before dawn, he left. The inn attendant went upstairs to clean and saw a dead woman, greatly shocked, he ran to inform the head of the inn. The head of the inn beat the drum, gathered all the staff, and together examined the body. It turned out to be a woman from the Wu family, who had recently died, during the night of her funeral, the fire went out, and when it was relit, she was gone. Her family took her away. Qi left, walked several miles, suffered from abdominal pain, and arrived at the South Dun Liyang Inn, where the pain worsened, and he died. The upper floor of the gatehouse was no longer dared to be entered.

Yingchuan Zhong You, courtesy name Yuan Chang, once went several months without attending court meetings, and his mood and nature were abnormal. Someone asked him why. He said, "There is a beautiful woman who comes to visit me, and she is extraordinary." The questioner said, "It must be a ghostly being; it can be killed." The woman came again, but she did not immediately enter. She stopped at the door. Zhong You asked, "Why?" She said, "You have an intention to kill me." Zhong You said, "No, I do not." He repeatedly called for her, and she finally entered. Zhong You felt regret and was unwilling to harm her, but he still injured her. She was injured in the thigh. The woman then left and used the silk lining in her armor to wipe the blood. The next day, Zhong You sent someone to follow the trail, and they arrived at a large tomb. Inside the tomb, there was a beautiful woman, her body like a living person, wearing a white silk robe and a red embroidered armor. She was injured in the left thigh and used the silk lining in her armor to wipe the blood.

**Records of the Supernatural, Volume Seventeen** In the state of Chen, there was a man named Zhang Hanzhi who traveled to Nanyang to study the *Zuo Zhuan* under the high-ranking official Yan Shujian. After he left, several months later, a spirit took his sister and spoke on her behalf, saying, "I died of illness. My body lies on the road, often suffering from hunger and cold. I have two or three items labeled 'not borrow,' hung on the paper behind the house. The man named Chuan Zifang sent me five hundred coins, which are buried under the north wall. All these things are true. Also, I bought a cow from Li You, and the original contract is in my book chest." When Zhang Hanzhi went to verify these claims, everything was exactly as the spirit had said. His wife did not know about this sister, as she had recently come from the village of Gou. The family was deeply saddened and became even more convinced of the spirit's authenticity. The parents and brothers, dressed in mourning attire, went to meet the supposed funeral procession. They traveled several miles and met Zhang Hanzhi and over ten students following behind. Zhang Hanzhi was surprised to see his family and wondered why they were there. The family saw Zhang Hanzhi and thought he was a ghost. They were filled with grief and confusion for a long time. Zhang Hanzhi then approached his father and explained the entire story. He said, "What I have heard and seen, such events are not isolated. We now know it was the work of an evil spirit."

Fan Dan of Waihuang, Chenliu, Han dynasty, courtesy name Shi Yun, served as an officer when young, following as an assistant to deliver official documents to the supervisor. Fan Dan had high moral standards and was self-blamed for being a low-ranking clerk. He then, in the great marsh of Chenliu, killed the horse he was riding, discarded his official hat, and pretended to encounter robbers. A spirit descended to his family and said, "I am Shi Yun. I was killed by robbers. Quickly take my clothes from the great marsh of Chenliu." His family obtained one hat. Fan Dan then went to Nanjun and turned to the three regions surrounding the capital, studying and traveling with outstanding scholars for thirteen years, then returned. His family no longer recognized him. People of Chenliu admired his virtue and conduct, and when he died, he was posthumously honored as Mr. of Chastity and Integrity.

A Man from Wu named Fei Ji, who had been a long-term guest in Chu, often worried about his wife because the roads were frequently plagued by bandits. One day, Fei Ji and his companions stayed overnight at the foot of Mount Lu, where they asked each other how long they had been away from home. Fei Ji said, "I have been away from home for several years now. Before I left, I bid farewell to my wife and asked her to give me a gold hairpin for the journey. I wanted to see if her heart was as resolute as mine. After obtaining the hairpin, I placed it on the door lintel. Just before I left, I forgot to tell her about it. The hairpin should still be on the door lintel." That night, his wife dreamed that Fei Ji said, "I encountered bandits on my journey and died two years ago. If you do not believe me, remember that when I left, I took your hairpin but did not take it with me; it was left on the door lintel. You can go and retrieve it." The wife woke up, felt for the hairpin, found it, and the family then held a funeral. About a year later, Fei Ji returned. In Yuyao, there was a man named Yu Dingguo, who was known for his good appearance. In the same county, there was a woman from the Su family who was also very beautiful, and Yu Dingguo often admired her. One day, when Yu Dingguo came to visit, the owner of the house invited him to stay for the night. In the middle of the night, the owner told Su Gong, "Your beautiful daughter is very charming, and I have a strong desire for her. Could she come out for a moment tonight?" The owner, considering Yu Dingguo a respected figure in the community, allowed the daughter to meet him. As their meetings became more frequent, Yu Dingguo told Su Gong, "There is nothing I can do to repay you. If you have any official matters, I will take care of them for you." The owner was pleased and, from then on, whenever there were official duties or summons, he would send someone to Yu Dingguo. Yu Dingguo was greatly surprised and said, "I have never spoken to you face-to-face. How could you do this? There must be something unusual." He asked for a detailed explanation. Yu Dingguo replied, "I would never ask for the father of someone and then defile their daughter. If you come again, I will chop you down." Indeed, something strange happened afterward.

During the reign of Sun Hao of Wu, Zhu Dan, the governor of Huainan, also known as Zhu Yongchang, served as the governor of Jian'an. Zhu Dan's attendant's wife was afflicted with a ghostly illness, and her husband suspected her of infidelity. After leaving on a trip, he secretly dug a hole in the wall to spy on her. He saw his wife weaving at the loom, while she gazed at a mulberry tree and spoke to someone on it. Looking up at the tree, he saw a young boy, about fourteen or fifteen years old, dressed in a blue collar and sleeves, and wearing a blue cap. Believing the boy to be real, the attendant drew his bow and shot at him. The boy transformed into a chirping cicada, as large as a winnowing basket, and flew away gracefully. The wife also exclaimed in surprise, saying, "Ah! Someone shot you." The attendant was puzzled by this. After some time, the attendant saw two children talking on the road, one of whom asked, "Why haven't you been seen again?" The other child, who was the boy from the tree, replied, "I was unfortunately shot by someone earlier and have been suffering from a wound for a long time." The other child asked, "How are you now?" The boy replied, "Thanks to the medicinal ointment on the beam provided by Governor Zhu, I have recovered." The attendant reported to Zhu Dan, saying, "Someone stole your medicinal ointment. Do you know about it?" Zhu Dan replied, "My ointment has been on the beam for a long time. How could someone steal it?" The attendant said, "That's not true. Please check it yourself, Governor." Zhu Dan did not believe him but decided to look. The seal was still intact. Zhu Dan said, "The servant is lying. The ointment is still the same." The attendant said, "Please open it and see." Upon opening it, they found that half of the ointment was gone. Upon scraping the container, they saw footprints. Zhu Dan was greatly shocked. He then asked the attendant in detail, and the attendant fully explained the beginning and end of the incident.

During the Wu period, Ni Yansi of Jiaxing lived in Xishan Village to the west of the county. Suddenly, a ghostly apparition entered his home, speaking with people and eating and drinking like a human, but could not be seen. Some of his servants and maids secretly cursed their master, and the ghost said, "I will now tell him." Ni Yansi punished them, and no one dared to curse him again. Ni Yansi had a concubine, and the ghost sought to take her. Ni Yansi then invited a Taoist priest to drive it away. When the wine and food were set out, the ghost took a tiger and placed it on the altar, blowing a horn-like sound. After a while, the Taoist priest suddenly felt cold on his back, jumped up, and removed his clothes, only to find a tiger there. The Taoist priest then left. At night, Ni Yansi secretly spoke with his wife under the covers, and they both suffered from this ghost. The ghost, perched on the roof beam, told Ni Yansi, "You and your wife talk about me, and I will now cut your roof beam." There was a loud noise. Ni Yansi feared the beam would break and lit a fire to look, but the ghost extinguished the fire. The sound of cutting the beam became more urgent. Ni Yansi feared the house would collapse and sent everyone out, then relit the fire to check, and the beam remained unchanged. The ghost laughed and asked Ni Yansi, "Are you still talking about me?" The local agricultural official heard about this and said, "This spirit is just a fox." The ghost then went to the official and said, "You took several hundred bushels of grain from the government and hid it somewhere, tainted by officials, and dared to speak about me! I will now report you to the authorities and have your stolen grain taken." The official was greatly frightened and apologized. After this, no one dared to speak about the ghost. Three years later, the ghost left, and no one knew where it went.

During the Huangchu era of the Wei dynasty, in the vicinity of Duntu, there was a man who was riding at night. He saw something on the road ahead that was as large as a rabbit, with eyes like mirrors, and it leaped in front of his horse, preventing him from proceeding. The man became terrified and fell off his horse. The spirit then seized him on the ground. Filled with fear, he died suddenly. After a long time, he regained consciousness. Upon waking, he had lost the spirit and did not know where it had gone. He then remounted his horse and continued his journey. After traveling several miles, he met another person. They exchanged greetings, and the man told him about the strange encounter. He was very pleased to have found a companion. The man said, "I was traveling alone, and now I have you as a companion, which is wonderful. Your horse moves quickly, so please go ahead, and I will follow behind." They continued their journey together. The companion asked, "What was that creature like, which made you so frightened?" The man replied, "It had a body like a rabbit, with eyes like mirrors, and its appearance was very ugly." The companion said, "Try looking at me." The man looked at him, and it was still the same. The spirit then leaped onto the horse. The man fell to the ground and died from fear. His family was surprised to find the horse returning alone and immediately went to search for him. They found him by the roadside. After a night, he woke up and recounted the events as described.

Yuan Shao, courtesy name Benchu, was in Jizhou when a deity emerged from Hedong, known as Lord Duosuo. The common people collectively built a temple for him. The temple had an official named Dafu, who brought great fortune. Chenliu's Cai Yong, serving as the governor of Qinghe, visited the temple. Cai Yong had a son named Dao, who had been dead for thirty years. Lord Duosuo set up wine for Cai Yong and said, "Your esteemed son came in the past and wants to meet you." Soon after, the son appeared. Lord Duosuo introduced himself, saying, "My ancestors were once in Yanzhou," and mentioned a scholar named Su whose mother was ill and had come to pray. The official said, "The lord will stay and wait for a celestial scholar." They heard drum sounds from the northwest, and the lord arrived. Soon, a guest appeared, wearing a dark-colored single robe, with five-colored hair on his head, several inches long. After he left, another person came, wearing a white cloth single robe, with a tall hat resembling a fish head, and said to the lord, "We once visited Mount Lushan together and ate white plums. Remembering it not long ago, it has already been three thousand years. Time passes easily, making people feel melancholic." After he left, the lord told the scholar, "The first one to arrive was the Lord of the South Sea." The scholar was a student of books, and the lord was well-versed in the Five Classics, skilled in the *Liji*. They discussed rites, but the scholar could not match the lord. The scholar asked for help to save his mother's illness.

The ruler said, "In the east where you reside, there is an old bridge that has been destroyed by people. The path this bridge spans was transgressed by your mother. If you can restore the bridge, then the matter will be resolved." General Cao attacked Yuan Tan and sent someone from the temple to exchange a thousand bolts of silk, but you did not give it. General Cao then sent Zhang He to destroy the temple. Before reaching one hundred li, you sent tens of thousands of troops, coming straight along the path. Zhang He had not reached two li when clouds and mist surrounded his army, and they did not know the location of the temple. You told the chief clerk, "General Cao's momentum is strong; it is advisable to avoid it." Later, a god descended at Su Jing's neighbor's house and recognized your voice, saying, "In the past, we moved into the lake and were absent for three years. Then we sent someone to inform General Cao, wanting to repair the old temple. The land is barren and not suitable for living, so we wanted to temporarily reside." General Cao said, "Very good." He built a tower in the north of the city to house the god. Several days later, General Cao hunted and obtained an object as large as a young deer, with large feet, white as snow, and soft, smooth fur that was lovable. He used it to rub his face, but no one could name it. That night, he heard crying on the tower, saying, "The little boy went out and did not return." General Cao clapped his hands and said, "This child's words are truly pitiful." In the morning, he led hundreds of dogs, surrounding the base of the tower. The dogs sensed the energy and charged inside and outside. They saw an object as large as a donkey, which jumped down from the base of the tower. The dogs killed it, and the temple god then ceased.

In the first year of the Yongchu era, Chen Chen, a wealthy man from Linchuan, was sitting in his study when he saw a person emerge from a plot of bamboo in his courtyard. The person was over ten feet tall and had a face like "Fang Xiang," a fearsome mythical creature. The figure directly spoke to Chen Chen, saying, "I have been in your house for many years, but you did not know. Now I am leaving, and you will know." About a month later, the house caught fire, and the slaves died suddenly. Within a year, Chen Chen became very poor.

In the region of Donglai, there was a family named Chen, with over a hundred members. One morning, as they heated water in a pot, it did not boil. Lifting the steamer to look at it, they suddenly saw an elderly man emerge from the pot. They went to a fortune-teller to seek advice. The fortune-teller said, "This is a great omen, and your family will be wiped out. Return home and make many weapons. Once made, place them under the door and seal it tightly. Inside, if anyone with a horse and a banner comes to knock on the door, do not answer." They returned home and made over a hundred weapons, placing them under the door. Indeed, someone came and called out, but they did not respond. The leader became furious and ordered his men to enter through the door. His men peered inside and saw over a hundred weapons, then reported back to him. The leader was greatly distressed and told his subordinates, "If you do not act quickly, no one will be left, and how can we atone for this? Travel eighty li north and find a family of 130 people to sacrifice to appease the spirits." Ten days later, the entire family was wiped out. This family also happened to be named Chen. During the reign of Emperor Huai of Jin in the year Yongkang, an unusual bird was found in the capital city, but no one could identify it. Sima Lun, the regent, had the bird shown around the city to seek identification. That same day, a child in the western part of the palace saw the bird and said, "It is a 'fúliú niǎo' (服留鸟)." The person who brought the bird returned to inform Sima Lun. Sima Lun then had the bird brought back and placed in a secret cage, along with the child, inside a closed room. The next day, when they went to check, the bird and the child were nowhere to be found.

In the south of Nankang County, there is a mountain to the east that can be seen from afar. Three people entered the mountain and saw a grove of fruit trees on the summit, with all kinds of fruits planted in neat rows, resembling human formations, and sweet fruits ripening. The three people ate together until they were full, then each took two fruits to show others. They heard a voice from the sky saying, "Release two fruits, and then you may leave." Qin Zhan, who lived in Qu'a Penghuang Field, suddenly experienced something snake-like entering his brain. The snake came, first smelling a foul odor, then entering through the nose, coiling in the head. He felt a loud noise and heard the sound of eating inside the head. After several days, the snake left. It went away, but soon returned. Qin Zhan tied a handkerchief over his nose and mouth, but the snake still entered. For many years, he had no other illnesses, only suffering from a heavy head. During the Jingzhong period of the Wei Dynasty, a strange occurrence happened in the household of an official in Xianyang County. Every night, they heard clapping and calling without reason. They tried to observe it, but saw nothing. The mother, tired from working at night, fell asleep. Soon after, she heard someone calling from under the stove, asking, "Why hasn't Wenyue come?" Her head responded, "I see the pillow, I can't go. You can come to drink with me." The next morning, it turned out to be a (食卞)臿 (a type of pest), which was then burned, and the strange phenomenon ceased.

A Man from Wei County There was a man from Wei County named Zhang Fen, whose family had originally been extremely wealthy. Suddenly, they grew old, their wealth scattered, and so they sold their house to Cheng Ying. Cheng Ying moved in, but soon the entire household fell ill, and so he sold the house to his neighbor, A Wen. Wen first took up a large sword, and at dusk entered the northern hall, climbing onto the central beam. He remained there until the third watch of the night ended. Suddenly, a figure appeared, over ten chi tall, wearing a tall hat and a yellow robe. He ascended the hall and called out, "Xi Yao!" Xi Yao responded. "Why is there the scent of living people in the house?" he asked. "There is none," was the reply. Then the figure departed. Shortly after, another figure appeared, wearing a tall hat and a green robe. Next, yet another figure came, wearing a tall hat and a white robe. The questioning and answering followed the same pattern as before. As dawn approached, Wen descended from the hall and called out to them as he had before. He asked, "Who is the one in yellow?" "Gold," came the reply. "It is beneath the western wall of the hall." "Who is the one in green?" "Money," he answered. "It is five steps from the well in front of the hall." "Who is the one in white?" "Silver," came the response. "It is beneath the column at the northeast corner of the wall." "And who are you?" Wen asked. "I am the pestle," the figure replied. "I am now beneath the stove." At dawn, Wen dug in the order indicated. He uncovered five hundred jin of gold and silver, and ten million guan of money. He then took the pestle and burned it. From this, he became greatly wealthy, and the house thereafter became peaceful and tranquil.

During the Qin dynasty, on the ancient road of Wudu, there was a temple dedicated to a fierce bull deity. On the temple grounds, a paulownia tree grew. In the twenty-seventh year of Duke Wen of Qin's reign, people were sent to cut down the tree, but there were always strong winds and heavy rain. The wounds on the tree would heal immediately, and it could not be cut down for days. Duke Wen then sent more soldiers, forty with axes, but still, the tree could not be cut down. The soldiers were tired and rested. One person was injured and could not walk, so he lay under the tree and heard the ghosts speaking to the tree spirit: "Tired? Fighting!" One person said, "What is there to be tired about?" Another asked, "Duke Qin will not stop. What can be done?" The tree spirit replied, "What can Duke Qin do to me?" Then, the ghost said, "If Duke Qin sends three hundred people, with their hair loose, tying red silk around the tree, wearing ochre clothes, and covering it with ash to cut it down, will you not be in trouble?" The spirit remained silent. The next day, the injured person told what he had heard. Duke Wen then ordered everyone to wear ochre clothes, cut at the wounds, and cover them with ash. The tree was cut down. A green ox emerged from the center of the tree and ran into the Feng River. Later, the green ox emerged from the Feng River, and the riders tried to attack it but failed. One rider fell off, got back on, his hair loose, and the ox feared him, so it went back into the water and did not come out. Therefore, Qin established the 'Máo Tóu Jì' (a type of cavalry unit).

In the Longshu County of Lujiang, near a watercourse by the Lu Pavilion, there was a large tree that stood several dozen zhang tall. It was home to thousands of yellow birds, and it was often said that the tree emitted a yellow aura, indicating the presence of a divine spirit. The elders of the village, believing that the tree had supernatural powers, decided to pray for rain. They brought wine and sacrificial offerings to the pavilion. One night, a widow named Ruan Xian saw a woman dressed in embroidered clothing appear in her room. The woman introduced herself as Huang Zu, the spirit of the tree, and said, "I can summon clouds and rain. Because of your pure nature, I will assist you in your livelihood. The elders of the village are going to pray for rain, and I have already asked the heavens. There will be heavy rain tomorrow at noon." As promised, it rained the following day, and the villagers built a shrine in gratitude. Huang Zu then said to Ruan Xian, "All of you here, I live near the water. I will bring some carp." After these words, dozens of carp flew to the courtyard, and those present were astonished. This continued for over a year, until Huang Zu said, "There will be a great war soon, and I must leave you now." She left behind a jade ring and said, "Hold onto this to protect yourself from harm." Later, Liu Biao and Yuan Shu engaged in a conflict, and the people of Longshu fled. However, only Ruan Xian's village remained unscathed by the war.

During the state of Wei, Jiangxia Zhang Liao, the governor of Guiyang, known by his courtesy name Shugao, left for Yanling, lived at home, and bought land. There was a large tree in the field, more than ten cun in circumference, with branches and leaves spread out, covering several acres, but no grain grew. He sent a guest to cut it down. After several strikes with the axe, red juice, six or seven dou, came out. The guest was terrified and returned to tell Shugao. Shugao became very angry and said, "The tree is old and the juice is red; how could it be strange?" Then he personally went and cut it down again. Blood flowed and splattered. Shugao ordered to first cut its branches. There was a hollow on top, and he saw a white-haired old man, about four or five chi long, pop out and rush towards Shugao. Shugao countered with his knife, like this, killing four or five heads, all of which died. Those around him were all terrified and fell to the ground. Shugao's spirit remained calm and unchanged. He looked carefully and saw that it was neither human nor animal. Then he cut down the tree. Is this what is called the wood and stone monster, the kui and wangling? That year, he was appointed as a censor, governor of Yan state, and held the rank of two thousand stones. Passing through his hometown, he offered sacrifices to his ancestors, dressed in embroidered clothes in broad daylight, admired by all, there were no other strange occurrences.

During the reign of Wu's first ruler, Lu Jingshu served as the Governor of Jian'an. He ordered the cutting down of a large camphor tree, and after several blows, suddenly blood flowed out. The tree split open, and a creature emerged with a human face and a dog's body. Lu Jingshu said, "This is called 'Peng Hou.'" He then cooked and ate it, and its taste was like that of a dog. The *Baize Tu* (Book of White Tiger) states, "The spirit of a tree is called 'Peng Hou,' it looks like a black dog without a tail, and can be cooked and eaten." During the Wu period, there was a paulownia tree with a huge circumference and leaves over ten feet wide, with branches spreading over several acres. Wu Wang cut down the tree to make a boat and had thirty young men and women pull it. The boat magically flew into the water, and the young men and women all drowned. Even now, the sound of singing and urging can be heard in the pond. Dong Zhongshu was studying with a curtain drawn when a visitor came to see him. He knew the visitor was not an ordinary person. The visitor also mentioned that it was about to rain. Dong Zhongshu teased him, saying, "Those who live in nests know the wind, and those who live in burrows know the rain. You are either a fox or a mouse." The visitor then transformed into an old fox.

Zhang Hua, courtesy name Maoxian, served as Chancellor during the reign of Emperor Huai of Jin. At that time, there was a spotted fox near the tomb of Yan Zhao Wang, which had lived there for many years and possessed the ability to transform. It changed into a scholar and wished to visit Zhang Hua. Passing by the stone tablet at the tomb, it asked, "With my talents and appearance, can I meet Chancellor Zhang?" The stone tablet replied, "Your profound understanding is such that nothing is impossible. However, Chancellor Zhang's wisdom and strategy may be difficult to outsmart. If you leave, you will likely suffer humiliation and may not return. Not only will you lose your long-lived nature, but you will also deeply mislead the old stone tablet." The fox did not heed the warning and instead held a visiting card to meet Zhang Hua. Zhang Hua saw the fox with a refined and elegant appearance, as pure as jade, with graceful movements and a charming presence, and held him in high regard. They then discussed literature, distinguishing and comparing appearances and realities, which Zhang Hua had never encountered before. They further explored the Three Histories, delved into the depths of all schools of thought, discussed the profound aspects of Laozi and Zhuangzi, uncovered the ultimate meanings of the *Feng* (Odes) and *Ya* (Elegance) sections of the *Shijing* (Book of Poetry), encompassed the wisdom of ten sages, connected the three talents, advised eight Confucian scholars, and analyzed the five rites. Zhang Hua was unable to respond without hesitation. He exclaimed, "How can there be such a young man in the world! If he is not a ghost or a spirit, then he must be a fox." Zhang Hua then swept the mat to invite the fox to stay and arranged for guards to protect him. The fox said, "Your Excellency should respect the wise and accommodate the many, commend the good and pity the incapable. Why do you hate learning? Mozi's philosophy of universal love, is that not the case?" After finishing his words, the fox requested to leave. Zhang Hua had already arranged for guards to watch the gate, so the fox could not go out. The fox then said to Zhang Hua, "Your gate is guarded by armed soldiers and cavalry, which must be to doubt and mistrust me."

Zhang Huan, the Vice Minister, had a guest who was a young man, exceptionally talented and eloquent. Zhang suspected he might be an evil spirit and ordered his guards to watch the young man closely. At the time, Feicheng County Magistrate Lei Huan, whose courtesy name was Kongzhang, was a learned scholar. He visited Zhang and told him about the guest. Kongzhang said, "If you doubt him, why not try a hunting dog on him?" Zhang then ordered the dog to test the young man, but the young man showed no fear. The fox said, "I was born with natural talent and intelligence, but it is mistakenly regarded as evil. You are using a dog to test me. Even if you try me a thousand times, with a myriad of considerations, how could I possibly cause harm?" Zhang heard this and became even angrier, saying, "This must indeed be a true evil spirit. I have heard that spirits and monsters fear dogs. They can only distinguish those that are several hundred years old. A thousand-year-old spirit cannot be distinguished. Only a thousand-year-old dry wood can reveal its form." Kongzhang asked, "How can one obtain a thousand-year-old tree?" Zhang replied, "It is said that the tablet in front of Yan Zhao Wang's tomb has already existed for a thousand years." He then sent someone to cut down the tablet. As the messenger was about to reach the tree, a little green-clad child appeared in the air and asked the messenger, "Where are you going?" The messenger said, "Zhang Huan has a young man who came to pay respects. He is very talented and eloquent, but Zhang suspects he is an evil spirit. He sent me to get the tablet to test him." The green-clad child said, "The old fox is not wise enough to listen to my advice. Now the disaster has already befallen me, and there is no way to escape!" He then cried out and suddenly disappeared. The messenger then cut down the tree, which was stained with blood. The messenger brought the tree back and burned it to illuminate the young man, revealing him to be a spotted fox. Zhang said, "These two beings would not have been caught if they had not encountered me. They are rare and valuable, and will not be found again in a thousand years." He then cooked and consumed them.

During the Jin Dynasty, in Wu County, there was a man who had two sons. They were working in the fields when they often saw their father come to scold and beat them. The children reported this to their mother. The mother asked the father about it. The father was greatly surprised and realized it was a ghost or demon. He then ordered his sons to cut it down. The ghost became silent and stopped coming. The father, worried that his sons would be trapped by the ghost, went to check himself. The sons thought it was a ghost and killed and buried it. The ghost then returned, taking the form of the father, and told their family that the two sons had already killed the demon. The sons returned home in the evening, celebrating together, and did not realize the truth for many years. Later, a monk passed by their house and told the two sons, "Your honorable lord has a great evil aura." The sons reported this to their father, who became very angry. The sons told the monk and ordered him to leave quickly. The monk made a sound and entered, and the father turned into a large old fox, going under the bed, where he was captured and killed. The one they killed earlier was actually their real father. They changed the burial and mourning attire. One son committed suicide, and the other, filled with anger and regret, also died. In Milu Village, Jurong County, there was a villager named Huang Shen who was farming in the fields. One day, a woman passed through his field, crossing the ridge from east to west and back again. Huang Shen initially thought she was a person. Every day, she would do the same, and he found it very strange. He asked her, "Where do you come from so often?" The woman stopped briefly, just smiled, and then left. Huang Shen became even more suspicious. He prepared a long sickle to wait for her return, but he did not dare to attack the woman; instead, he attacked the servant girl following her. The woman transformed into a fox and ran away. When he looked at the servant girl, she had only a fox's tail. Huang Shen tried to chase her but could not catch her. Later, someone saw the fox come out of a pit and dug it up, finding no tail left.

Liu Bozhu of Boling served as the governor of Hedong. While staying there, a spirit on the ceiling could speak and often called out to Liu Bozhu to talk. Whenever news of imperial edicts or messages from the capital came, the spirit would foretell Liu Bozhu. Liu Bozhu asked what it ate, and it replied that it wanted sheep liver. He then bought sheep liver and cut it into pieces, which disappeared as he cut them. All the sheep liver was consumed. Suddenly, an old cat appeared, mewing in front of the table. The person holding the knife wanted to chop it, but Liu Bozhu stopped him, and the cat climbed onto the ceiling. After a moment, it laughed and said, "Just now, I ate the sheep liver, got drunk, and accidentally revealed my true form to you. I am very embarrassed." Later, when Liu Bozhu was to become the Governor of Sili, the spirit again told him, "On a certain day in a certain month, the imperial edict will arrive." On the appointed day, it came as predicted. When he entered the Sili office, the spirit followed and often spoke about matters inside the office. Liu Bozhu was greatly terrified and told the spirit, "Now that I am in charge of investigating and reporting, if the powerful people hear that the spirit is here, they might harm me." The spirit replied, "Indeed, as you have feared. I will leave you." And then it was silent.

During the Jian'an period of the Later Han dynasty, Chen Xian of Pei State served as the military commander of Xihai. One of his subordinates, Wang Lingxiao, disappeared without cause. Xian intended to kill him. Not long after, Lingxiao fled once more. After failing to see him for some time, Xian imprisoned his wife, who truthfully confessed what had happened. Xian said, "This must be a spirit that has taken him away—we must search for him." He then led a group of dozens of foot soldiers and cavalry, accompanied by hunting dogs, and searched extensively outside the city. Indeed, he found Lingxiao inside an empty tomb. Upon hearing the sounds of people and dogs, the spirit fled away. Xian had his men carry Lingxiao back home, but his form was now quite like that of a fox. He no longer responded to people, but only cried out, "Azi." Azi is the name for a fox. After more than ten days, he gradually regained his awareness and said, "The fox first came to me when it took on the form of a beautiful woman, calling herself Azi, and lured me. This happened more than once. Suddenly, I followed her, and immediately became her wife, returning home with her every evening. I did not realize there was a dog nearby. It was infinitely joyful." A Taoist priest said, "This is a mountain spirit." The *Ming Shan Ji* records: "The fox was once a woman of loose virtue from ancient times, named Azi, who transformed into a fox." Therefore, these spirits often call themselves Azi.

In the western suburbs of Nanyang, there was a pavilion where no one could linger, for doing so would bring misfortune. Cao Da Xian, a virtuous man who conducted himself according to the righteous way, once stayed in the pavilion's tower. At midnight, a ghost climbed the ladder and spoke with Cao Da Xian, glaring at him with its eyes and grinding its teeth, its appearance grotesque. Yet Cao Da Xian continued to play the qin as before. The ghost then left. It went to the market to fetch a severed human head and returned, speaking to Cao Da Xian: "Would you like to rest a bit?" It then threw the severed head in front of Cao Da Xian. Cao Da Xian replied, "Very good! I have no pillow for my evening sleep, and this is just what I need." The ghost left again. After a long time, it returned, saying, "Would you like to engage in hand-to-hand combat?" Cao Da Xian replied, "Yes!" As the ghost approached, Cao Da Xian seized its waist from behind. The ghost merely cried out in fear. Cao Da Xian then killed it. The next day, it was discovered that the ghost was an old fox. From then on, the pavilion was free of ghosts.

A Postal Inspector from Xiping named Dao Bei, about thirty years old, was very talented and decisive. He was the grandson of the former Governor of Changsha, Dao Ruozhang. At the time of day when the sun is setting, he arrived at a post station and ordered the leading person to stop. The clerk said, "It's still early, we can reach the next post station." Dao Bei replied, "I want to write documents." He decided to stay, causing the officials and soldiers to be terrified and say they should leave. The message said, "The postal inspector wants to observe from the upper floor, quickly clean up." A short while later, he went up. Before it got dark, there were lights under the staircase, and there was another fire command. "I think about the Dao, cannot see fire, extinguish it." The officials knew something would happen, should use it for illumination, but put it in a pot. When it got dark, he got dressed and sat down, recited the Six Jia, the Classic of Filial Piety, and the I Ching, and then lay down. After a while, he turned to face east, used a handkerchief to tie his feet and head, secretly drew his sword and untied his belt. At night, there was a dark figure about four to five feet tall, gradually grew taller, ran to the pillar of the house, and then covered the inspector. Dao Bei held the blanket to cover it, but his feet slipped off, almost losing it. He hit the demon's feet with his sword and belt several times, called for the light below to illuminate above. He looked at it and saw an old fox, bright red, with almost no fur. He took it down and burned it. The next morning, he searched the upper floor and found more than a hundred hair buns from shaved people. From then on, it stopped. In Wu, there was a scholar, white-haired, called Hu Doctor, who taught the students. Suddenly, he disappeared again. On the ninth day of the ninth month, scholars went up the mountain together to enjoy the scenery and heard the sound of teaching. They ordered the servant to look for it and saw a group of foxes arranged in an empty tomb. The foxes ran away when they saw people, but the old fox did not leave. It was the white-haired scholar.

Tchen Jun's Xie Kun, after resigning from office due to illness, sought refuge in Yuzhang. Once, he passed through an abandoned pavilion and stayed there for the night. This pavilion was notorious for killing people. At the fourth watch of the night, a man in yellow clothing called out to Xie Kun by name, saying, "Young Yu! Open the door!" Xie Kun remained calm and fearless, extending his arm through the window. The man then took his hand. Xie Kun exerted all his strength to pull, and the man's arm was torn off. The man then departed. The next day, they found a deer's arm. Following the trail of blood, they caught the deer. After this, the pavilion was no longer haunted. During the Jin dynasty, there was a scholar named Wang, whose family lived in Wu Commandery. On his way back to Qu'e, he arrived late in the evening and docked his boat at a large embankment. He saw a young woman, around seventeen or eighteen years old, on the embankment and called out to her, inviting her to stay for the night. At dawn, he removed a golden bell from her arm and sent someone to follow her home, but there was no woman there. They then searched the pig pen and found a sow with a golden bell on its arm. In the Han dynasty, there was a man from Qi named Liang Wen, who was fond of Daoism. His family had a shrine to a deity, and they built three or four rooms for it. There was a black curtain on the seat, and the deity would often appear there. After several years, during a ritual, someone suddenly spoke from the curtain, introducing himself as Lord of the High Mountain. He was said to be able to eat and drink, and his cures were effective. Liang Wen served him with great reverence. After several years, he was allowed to enter the curtain, and the deity became drunk. Liang Wen then asked to see the deity's face. The deity said, "Give me your hand!" Liang Wen reached out, and the deity took his chin, with a long beard and mustache. Liang Wen gradually wrapped his hand around the deity's neck and suddenly pulled, hearing a sheep's bleat. The people in the room were startled and helped Liang Wen pull. It turned out to be a sheep from Yuan Shu's household, which had been missing for seven or eight years. They killed the sheep, and the supernatural occurrences ceased.

In the city of Beiping, there was a man named Tian Yan who was observing the mourning for his mother. He always lived in a thatched hut facing east. One night, a ghost suddenly entered his wife's room and whispered in surprise, saying, "You are in a place of destruction, fortunately you cannot bear it." Tian Yan did not listen and closed the door. Later, when Tian Yan briefly entered the room, he did not speak to his wife. His wife was puzzled by his silence and blamed him for the earlier incident. Tian Yan then realized it was a ghost. That evening, he stayed awake all night, hanging his mourning attire in the hut. Soon after, he saw a white dog climb the thatched hut and bite the mourning attire, then transform into a person and enter the room. Tian Yan followed and saw the dog about to climb onto his wife's bed. He struck it down and killed it. His wife, ashamed and humiliated, died. In another incident, the Grand Works Minister from Nanyang, Lai Jide, had a funeral in progress when he suddenly saw the deceased sitting on the sacrificial bed. The appearance, clothing, and voice were all familiar. He then proceeded to teach and admonish his grandchildren and daughters-in-law in turn, with everything being done in an orderly manner. He even punished the servants and slaves according to their faults. After the food and drink were finished, he bid farewell and left. The entire family, young and old, felt a deep sense of sorrow and grief. This continued for several years, and the household grew increasingly weary and troubled. Later, after drinking too much wine and becoming drunk, the ghost's true form was revealed, and they found it was just an old dog. They all beat and killed it. Upon investigation, it turned out to be a dog from the village wine shop. In the city of Shanyang, there was a man named Wang Hu, whose courtesy name was Menglian. He served as the magistrate of Lanling in the state of Donghai. Every midnight, a black-capped, white-garbed official would come to the county office and knock on the door. When they went to greet him, he would suddenly disappear. This happened for several years. Later, they waited for him and saw an old dog with a white body, which transformed into a person upon reaching the gate. They informed Menglian, who then killed it, and the apparitions ceased.

In the prefecture of Guiyang, there was a magistrate named Li Shujian who served as an assistant official. He had a dog at home that would mimic human behavior. The family suggested, "We should kill it." Li Shujian replied, "Dogs and horses are metaphors for virtuous men. The dog sees people walking and imitates them; what harm is there in that?" Soon after, the dog was seen wearing Li Shujian's hat and running away. The family was greatly alarmed. Li Shujian said, "It must have accidentally caught the hat on its head." The dog also helped to keep the fire going in the kitchen. The family became even more anxious. Li Shujian reassured them, "All the children and servants are out working in the fields. The dog is helping to keep the fire going, which is fortunate as it means we don't need to bother the neighbors. There is nothing wrong with this." After a few days, the dog died suddenly. There were no further unusual occurrences. In the prefecture of Wu, in Wuxi, there was a large pond known as Shanghu Dipei. The official responsible for the pond, Ding Chutian, would patrol the embankment during heavy rains. One spring, when the rains were particularly heavy, Ding Chutian was walking along the pond when he looked back and saw a woman dressed in green clothes and carrying a green umbrella. She called out to him, "Wait for me, Official Ding!" Initially, Ding Chutian felt a mix of emotions and considered staying to see the woman. However, he then became suspicious, thinking that he had never seen her before and that she might be a ghost appearing in the rain. He quickly ran away. As he looked back, he saw the woman following him, but he ran faster. As he ran, he got further away from her. When he looked back again, he saw the woman jump into the pond, making a splash and scattering her clothes and umbrella. Upon closer inspection, he realized that the woman was actually a large water otter, with the clothes and umbrella being made of lotus leaves. It turned out that this otter had transformed into a human form to seduce young men.

During the Zhengshi era of King Wang of Wei, Prince Zhou Nan of Zhongshan, who served as the magistrate of Xiangyi, suddenly saw a mouse emerge from its hole in the main hall and speak to him: "Prince Zhou Nan! You will die on a certain day and month." Prince Zhou Nan hurried to the spot but did not respond. The mouse returned to its hole. Later, on the designated day, the mouse emerged again, this time wearing a headgear and black robe, and said: "Prince Zhou Nan! You will die at noon." Prince Zhou Nan still did not respond. The mouse went back into its hole. Shortly after, it emerged again, moving around and repeating the same words as before. Just as the sun reached its zenith, the mouse said: "Prince Zhou Nan! You should not die, what else can I say!" After speaking, the mouse fell and died. The clothes and headgear it had worn were immediately lost. Upon inspection, the mouse was no different from ordinary mice. In the south of Anyang, there was a pavilion that was said to be dangerous to stay in at night; anyone who stayed there would be killed. A scholar skilled in divination and numerology decided to spend the night there. The villagers warned him: "This place is not safe. No one has survived after staying here." The scholar replied: "There is no need to worry. I can manage." He then stayed in the pavilion and sat upright, reciting books. After a long time, he rested. After midnight, a man dressed in a black robe appeared, went outside, and called the pavilion's caretaker. The caretaker responded: "Do you see anyone in the pavilion?" The man answered: "Earlier, there was a scholar reading here. He just rested and seems not yet asleep." The man then sighed and left. Shortly after, another man appeared, wearing a red headgear, and called the caretaker. The exchange was the same as before. The man sighed again and left. After they left, everything was silent.

The scholar knew that no one else was coming, so he immediately rose and went to the place where he had heard the call, imitating the call of the innkeeper. The innkeeper responded affirmatively. The scholar then asked, "Is there someone in the inn?" The innkeeper answered as before. The scholar asked, "Who is the one coming in black clothing?" The innkeeper replied, "The old sow from the north house." The scholar asked again, "Who is the one coming with a red headband?" The innkeeper replied, "The old rooster father from the west house." The scholar asked, "And who are you?" The innkeeper replied, "I am the old scorpion." The scholar then quietly began reciting books and did not dare to sleep until dawn. When morning came, the innkeeper came to see and was surprised, saying, "How did you manage to survive alone?" The scholar said, "Quickly fetch the sword, I will help you catch the demon." He held the sword and went to the place where it had responded the previous night. Sure enough, he found the old scorpion, which was as large as a lute and had a poison several feet long. From the west house, he caught the old rooster father; from the north house, he caught the old sow. In total, he killed three creatures. The inn was then quiet, and there were no more disasters or calamities.

During the Wu period, there was a two-story building at the town gate of庐陵郡 that was often haunted by ghosts. Anyone who stayed there died immediately. After that, no official dared to enter the building to stay overnight. At that time, there was a man from DanYang named Tang Ying, who was very brave and strong. When he was sent to庐陵, he decided to stay in the building for the night. The official advised against it, but Tang Ying refused to listen. He dismissed his attendants and went inside, carrying only a large knife, and stayed alone in the building. It was the third watch of the night when suddenly he heard someone knocking on the door. Tang Ying asked from afar who it was. The answer came, "This is from the prefecture." Tang Ying allowed them to enter and they delivered their message and left. Soon after, there was another knock on the door, and the voice said, "This is from the prefecture's official." Tang Ying allowed them to enter again. The person was dressed in black. After they left, Tang Ying thought it was a human and had no doubts. Soon after, there was another knock on the door, and the voice said, "This is the prefecture's official coming to visit." Tang Ying then became suspicious, saying, "It is not the proper time for this visit, and the prefecture's official should not be traveling together." He realized they were ghosts. He then held his knife and went to meet them. He saw two people, both well-dressed, and they both entered and sat down. The one called "prefecture's official" began talking to Tang Ying. Before the conversation was over, the prefecture's official suddenly got up and approached Tang Ying from behind. Tang Ying turned around and struck him with his knife, hitting him. The prefecture's official got up and ran out. Tang Ying quickly chased him to the back wall of the building and caught up with him, striking him several times. He then returned to his room and lay down. At dawn, he took people to search for the prefecture's official and found bloodstains. They confirmed that the one called "prefecture's official" was an old pig, and the prefecture's official was an old fox. From then on, the haunting stopped.

**Search for Gods, Volume 19** In the region of Dongyue (Eastern Yue) in Fujian (Minzhong), there is a mountain called Yongling, which is tens of miles high. In the gap in the northwest of this mountain, there is a large snake, about seventy to eighty feet long and over ten encirclements in girth. The local people are always afraid of it. The Eastern Commandant and officials of the subordinate cities have often died. They sacrifice oxen and sheep, but they do not receive blessings; sometimes the snake appears in people's dreams, or gives instructions to the shamans, wanting to eat young girls aged twelve or thirteen. The Commandant and officials all suffer from this, but the spirit is fierce and relentless. They jointly request that families provide young maidservants, and also provide daughters from families with crimes, and raise them until the eighth day of the eighth month, when they are sacrificed at the snake's den. The snake comes out and swallows them. For many years this has been the case, and already nine girls have been used. At that time, they again sought volunteers, but no suitable girl was found. In Jiangle County, Li Dan's family has six daughters. They have no sons, and their youngest daughter is named Ji. She volunteered to go, but her parents did not allow it. Ji said, "Our parents have no good fortune, and we have only six daughters, no sons. Even though we have them, it is as if we do not."

The girl had no virtue like that of Tiying to save her parents, nor could she provide for them, only wasting food and clothing, living brought no benefit, it would be better to die early. To sell one's own body, one can earn a little money, to provide for one's parents, isn't that good? Her parents showed kindness and pity, never allowing her to leave. Ji secretly left, could not be stopped. Ji carried a sword and led a dog, first taking several stone of sweet rice cakes, using honey to moisten them, placing them at the entrance of the hole. The snake came out, its head was as big as a granary, its eyes were like two-foot mirrors, smelling the fragrance of the cakes, first eating the cakes. Ji then released the dog, the dog bit and gnawed, Ji struck from behind, causing several wounds, the wounds were painful, the snake jumped out, dying in the courtyard. Ji entered the hole, finding the skulls of nine girls, all lifting them out, exclaiming, "You are all weak and timid, eaten by the snake, very pitiful." She slowly returned home. King Yu heard of it, marrying Ji's daughter as his queen, appointing her father as the governor of Jiangle, her mother and sister also received rewards. From then on, there were no more evil spirits in Dongzhi, its songs and ballads still exist today.

During the Xianning period of Emperor Wu of Jin, Wei Shu served as Simu, and in his government office, there were two large snakes, about ten zhang long, living on the flat beams of the hall. They stayed there for several years, but the people did not know about them, only noticing because children and poultry were frequently missing.
